60804.explanation:
In redox both oxidation and reduction take place simultaneously.

zinc is oxidised and silver is reduced.

60806.explanation: It is aldohexose. An aldohexose is a hexose with an aldehyde group on one end.it is naturally occurring in nature and is found in fruits.

60807.explanation: Glucose is correctly named as D(+)-glucose. ‘D’ before the name of glucose represents the configuration whereas ‘(+)’ represents dextrorotatory nature of the molecule. When the -OH group lies on right hand side in the structure it is said to have D configuration. In (+) glucose, -OH on the lowest asymmetric carbon is on the right side so (+) glucose is assigned D-configuration.

60808.explanation: The letters ‘D’ or ‘L’ before the name of any compound indicate the relative configuration of a particular stereoisomer of a compound with respect to configuration of some other compound, configuration of which is known. In the case of carbohydrates, this refers to their relation with a particular isomer of glyceraldehyde. When the -OH group lies on right hand side in the structure it is said to have D configuration. In L configuration -OH group is on left hand side of the asymmetric C.

60809.explanation: Glyceraldehyde has one chiral centre (the asymmetric C) and has two enantiomers as we can see they are mirror images of each other. Stereoisomers of a compound are those isomers of the compound which have same molecular formula but different spatial arrangements of their atoms in space. So these compounds are stereoisomers. These compounds are dextrorotatory (+) isomer and laevorotatory (-) isomers as one rotates the path of light in right direction whereas the other rotates in left direction.

60811.explanation: Glucose is probably the most abundant organic compound on earth. It is the monomer of many of the larger carbohydrates, namely starch, cellulose

60812.explanation: Monosaccharides (such as Glucose/dextrose) are building blocks of disaccharides (such as sucrose and lactose) and polysaccharides (such as Cellulose and Starch).

60813.explanation: Glucose reacts with hydroxylamine (NH2OH) to form an oxime. Oxime is formed by combination of carbonyl group of glucose and hydroxyl group of hydroxyl amine.

60814.explanation: Acetylation of glucose with acetic anhydride gives glucose pentaacetate which confirms the presence of five –OH groups. Since it exists as a stable compound, five –OH groups should be attached to different carbon atoms.

60815.explanation: Glucose is the monomer of many of the larger carbohydrates, namely starch, cellulose. Hydrolysis of starch gives glucose.

60817.explanation: Cyclic form of fructose is called Fructofuranose. The ring, thus formed is a five membered ring and is named as furanose with analogy to the compound furan. Furan is a five membered cyclic compound with one oxygen and four carbon atoms.

60818.explanation: Glycosidic bond is an ether linkage between two monosaccharides.

60819.explanation: Honey is a high carbohydrate substance. It also contains proteins.

60833.explanation:
Commercially glucose is obtained by hydrolysis of starch by boiling it with dilute H2SO4 at 393 K under pressure (2-3 atm).

{tex}(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + nH_2O \rightarrow nC_6H_{12}O_6{/tex}

60834.explanation: The particles in nucleus of the cell, responsible for heredity, are called chromosomes which are made up of proteins and another type of biomolecules called nucleic acids. These are mainly of two types, the deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA). Since nucleic acids are long chain polymers of nucleotides, so they are also called polynucleotides. Thus nucleic acid is a biological polymer.

60836.explanation: Complete hydrolysis of DNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid and nitrogen containing heterocyclic compounds (called bases). In DNA molecules, the sugar moiety is β-D-2-deoxyribose.

60837.explanation: B group vitamins and vitamin C are soluble in water so they are grouped together as water soluble vitamins. These must be supplied regularly in diet because they are readily excreted in urine and cannot be stored (except vitamin B12) in our body.

60839.explanation: A unit formed by the attachment of a base to 1′ position of sugar is known as nucleoside. Adenosine is nucleoside composed of adenine attached a ribose sugar molecule.

60840.explanation: When nucleoside is linked to phosphoric acid at 5′-position of sugar moiety, we get a nucleotide. ATP (Adenosine triphosphate) is nucleotide of adenosine with 3 phosphoric acid molecules.

60841.explanation: Various RNA (ribonucleic acid) molecules in the cell guide synthesise the proteins.

60843.explanation: Deficiency of Vitamin B12 results in Pernicious anaemia (Red Blood Cells deficient in haemoglobin)

60844.explanation: Nucleotides are together by phosphodiester linkage between 5′ and 3′ carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.

60845.explanation: DNA has a double strand helix structure. Two nucleic acid chains are wound about each other and held together by hydrogen bonds between pairs of bases. The two strands are complementary to each other because the hydrogen bonds are formed between specific pairs of bases. In secondary structure of RNA, helices are present which are only single stranded. Sometimes they fold back on themselves to form a double helix structure.

60846.explanation: Those amino acids which cannot be synthesised in the body and must be obtained through diet, are known as essential amino acids. Proline is not an essential amino acid.

60848.explanation: Deficiency of Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) results in Cheilosis (fissuring at corners of mouth and lips).

60849.explanation: Information regarding the sequence of nucleotides in the chain of a nucleic acid is called its primary structure. This gives information about sequence of bases in DNA which is primarily responsible for the hereditary character. Thus nucleotides are carrier of hereditary character.

60850.explanation: Proteins are the polymers of α-amino acids. So building unit of a protein is α – Aminoacid.

60851.explanation: In proteins α-amino acids are connected to each other by peptide bond or peptide linkage. Chemically, peptide linkage is an amide formed between –COOH group and –NH2 group. The reaction between two molecules of similar or different amino acids, proceeds through the combination of the amino group of one molecule with the carboxyl group of the other. This results in the elimination of a water molecule and formation of a peptide bond –CO–NH–. So protein is condensation polymer.

60852.explanation: If adrenal cortex does not function properly then one of the results may be Addison’s disease characterised by hypoglycemia, weakness and increased susceptibility to stress.

60853.explanation: The combination of ovum and sperm forms the fertilized egg which further gets implanted in the uterus. This is brought about by the hormone progesterone which prepares uterus for this process.

60854.explanation: The role of insulin is to keep the blood glucose level within the narrow limit. Insulin is released in response to the rapid rise in blood glucose level. On the other hand hormone glucagon tends to increase the glucose level in the blood.  The two hormones together regulate the glucose level in the blood.

60855.explanation: When the polypeptide chains run parallel and are held together by hydrogen and disulphide bonds, then fibre– like structure is formed. Such proteins called fibrous proteins are generally insoluble in water. A common example is keratin (present in hair, wool, silk).

60856.explanation:
DNA fingerprinting is now used

  1. in forensic laboratories for identification of criminals.
  2. to determine paternity of an individual.
  3. to identify the dead bodies in any accident by comparing the DNA’s of parents or children.
  4. to identify racial groups to rewrite biological evolution.

60857.explanation: Globular proteins are formed when the chains of polypeptides coil around to give a spherical shape. These are usually soluble in water. For example albumin and insulin.

60858.explanation: Globular proteins are formed when the chains of polypeptides coil around to give a spherical shape. These are usually soluble in water. When the polypeptide chains run parallel and are held together by hydrogen and disulphide bonds, then fibre– like structure is formed. Such proteins called fibrous proteins are generally insoluble in water. Myosin (present in muscles), is a fibrous protein and not a globular protein.

60859.explanation: Amino acids contain amino (–NH2) and carboxyl (–COOH) functional groups.

60861.explanation: B group vitamins and vitamin C are soluble in water so they are grouped together as water soluble vitamins, these must be supplied regularly in diet because they are readily excreted in urine and cannot be stored (except vitamin B12) in our body.

60862.explanation: B group vitamins and vitamin C are soluble in water so they are grouped together as water soluble vitamins. These must be supplied regularly in diet because they are readily excreted in urine and cannot be stored (except vitamin B12) in our body.

60863.explanation: Vitamins which are soluble in fat and oils but insoluble in water are kept in fat soluble vitamins. These are vitamins A, D, E and K. They are stored in liver and adipose (fat storing)  tissues. All these are insoluble in water.

60864.explanation: Vitamins which are soluble in fat and oils but insoluble in water are kept in fat soluble vitamins. These are vitamins A, D, E and K. These are generally stored in liver and adipose (fat storing) tissues.

60865.explanation: Vitamin A keeps eyes in good health. Deficiency of Vitamin A causes Night Blindness.

60866.explanation: Complete hydrolysis of RNA yields a pentose sugar, phosphoric acid and nitrogen containing heterocyclic compounds (called bases). In RNA molecule, the sugar moiety is β-D-ribose.

60867.explanation: DNA contains four bases viz. adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C) and thymine (T). RNA also contains four bases, the first three bases are same as in DNA but the fourth one is uracil (U).

60868.explanation: A unit formed by the attachment of a base to 1′ position of sugar is known as nucleoside. In nucleosides, the sugar carbons are numbered as 1′, 2′, 3′, etc. in order to distinguish these from the bases.

60869.explanation: When nucleoside is linked to phosphoric acid at 5ꞌ – position of sugar moiety, we get a nucleotide.

60870.explanation: When nucleoside is linked to phosphoric acid at 5′-position of sugar moiety, we get a nucleotide and nucleotides are joined by a phosphodiester bond between 5′ and 3′ carbon atoms of the pentose sugar.

60871.explanation: Thyroxine produced in the thyroid gland is an iodinated derivative of amino acid tyrosine

60872.explanation: Two nucleic acid chains are wound about each other and held together by hydrogen bonds between pairs of bases. The two strands are complementary to each other because the hydrogen bonds are formed between specific pairs of bases. Adenine forms hydrogen bonds with thymine whereas cytosine forms hydrogen bonds with guanine. So base pair in a double helix of DNA is A-T.

60873.explanation: A sequence of bases on DNA is unique for a person and information regarding this is called DNA fingerprinting. It is same for every cell and cannot be altered by any known treatment.

60874.explanation: Proteins may have one or more polypeptide chains. Each polypeptide in a protein has amino acids linked with each other in a specific sequence and it is this sequence of amino acids that is said to be the primary structure of that protein. Thus the most appropriate structure for knowing about the sequence of nucleotides in the DNA chain is its primary structure.

60875.explanation: Curdling of milk is caused due to the formation of lactic acid by the bacteria present in milk. Milk contains proteins. When a protein in its native form, is subjected to physical change like change in temperature or chemical change like change in pH, the hydrogen bonds are disturbed. Due to this, globules unfold and helix get uncoiled and protein loses its biological activity. This is called denaturation of protein.

60876.explanation: Starch is a polymer of α-glucose and consists of two components - Amylose and Amylopectin. Amylose is water-soluble component which constitutes about 15-20% of starch. Amylopectin is insoluble in water and constitutes about 80-85% of starch. Thus, Amylopectin is the major component of starch.

60880.explanation: Vitamins which are soluble in fat and oils but insoluble in water are fat soluble vitamins. These are vitamins A, D, E and K. They are stored in liver and adipose (fat storing) tissues.

64493.explanation: osmotic pressure, as it depends upon number of particles(moles).

64494.explanation: When a non volatile solute is added the elevation in BP takes place with decrease in vapour pressure.

64495.explanation: Isotonic solutions contain equal concentrations of impermeable solutes on either side of the membrane and so the cell neither swells nor shrinks.

Molality (m) is defined as the number of moles of the solute per kilogram (kg) of the solvent

molality {tex} = \frac{{\Delta {T_f}}}{{{K_f}}}{/tex}

64501.explanation:
Osmotic pressure depends on number of solute particles irrespective of their nature relative to the total number of particles present in the solution.

C = Molarity

64652.explanation:
{tex} 2 {Ag}^{+}+{H}_2 \rightarrow 2 {Ag}+2 {H}^{+}{/tex}
By convention, the cell may be represented as.
{tex} H_2\left|H^{+}\right| A g^{+} \mid A g {/tex}
{tex} \therefore E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{A g^{+} / A g}^{\circ}-E_{H^{+} / H_2}^{\circ} {/tex}
or {tex}E_{A g^{+} / A g}^{\circ}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}+E_{H^{+} / H_2}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex} =0.80+0=0.80 v{/tex}
Thus, standard reduction potential of {tex}A g{/tex} electrode as,
{tex} E_{A g^{+} / A g}^{\circ}=0.80 {~V}{/tex}
and standard oxidation potential of {tex}A g{/tex} electrode as,
{tex} E_{A g / A g^{+}}^{\circ}=-0.80 {~V}{/tex}

64655.explanation: Fe2O3 and Fe(OH)3 rust has general formula Fe2O3..xH2O. it is formed when iron compounds corrode in the presence of air and oxygen. So it is a mixture of oxides and hydroxides.

∆ G 0 = -nFE0

For Cu2++ 2e →Cu ;∆ G1 0 =-2F( +0.337)---(i)

64657.explanation:
Data:

  1. Current in ampere (I)= 0.5 amp
  2. Time in seconds =193 s
  3. Mass of Ag metal deposited (w) = 0.108g

Formula:

w = Z I t ........(i)

{tex}Z= \frac {Equivalent weight}{ 96500 C} {/tex} ........(ii)

Substitute (ii) in (i),

{tex}w = \frac {Equivalent weight} {96500 C} \times I \times t {/tex}

or Equivalent weight {tex} = \frac{{w} \times {96500C}} {{I} \times {t}} {/tex}

Equivalent weight {tex} = \frac{{0.108g} \times {96500C}} {{0.5amp} \times {193sec}} {/tex}{tex}= \frac{{0.108} \times {96500}} {{0.5} \times {193}} \times \frac {{g} \times {C}} {{amp} \times {sec}} {/tex}{tex}= \frac{{0.108} \times {96500}} {96.5} \times \frac {{g} \times {C}} {C} {/tex}{tex}= 108 g {/tex} [∵ 1C={tex}1amp \times 1sec{/tex}]

Equivalent weight of Ag = 108 g

64659.explanation:

64660.explanation: The flow of electricity, through solutions of electrolytes, is due to the migration of ions when a potential difference is applied between the two electrodes.

64796.explanation:
unit of rate constant for nth order of reaction are:
unit of k for nth order = (molL-1)1-n s-1
put n=2 for second order reaction.

64798.explanation:
rate of first-order = k[R]
{tex}{k = \frac{{rate}}{{\left[ R \right]}} = \frac{{0.69 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}mol{L^{ - 1}}mi{n^{ - 1}}}}{{0.2mol{L^{ - 1}}}}}{/tex}
{tex}{k = 3.45 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}mi{n^{ - 1}} = \frac{{3.45 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}\;{s^{ - 1}}}}{{60}}}{/tex}
now,
{tex}{{t_{{1_{{/_2}}}}} = \frac{{0.69}}{k} = \frac{{0.69 \times 60}}{{3.45 \times {{10}^{ - 2}}}} = 1200s}{/tex}

the half-life period is = 1200s

64799.explanation:
For second-order reaction:
{tex}{{t_{{1_{{/_2}}}}} = \frac{{2k}}{{\left[ R \right]}} \Rightarrow k = \frac{{{t_{{1_{{/_2}}}}}\left[ R \right]}}{2}}{/tex}
Applying this equation,
{tex}{\frac{{{t_{{1_{{/_2}}}}}\left[ R \right]}}{2} = \frac{{{{t'}_{{1_{{/_2}}}}}\left[ {R'} \right]}}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}{\frac{{8.0 \times 0.50}}{2} = \frac{{{{t'}_{{1_{{/_2}}}}} \times 0.10}}{2}}{/tex}
{tex}{{{t'}_{{1_{{/_2}}}}} = \frac{{8 \times 0.50}}{{0.10}} = 40}{/tex}

64800.explanation: Energy is needed to break bonds. This energy, used to initiate the reaction, is called the activation energy.

64801.explanation:
Rate of reaction is first-order w.r.t A reactant and 2nd-order w.r.t D reactant, therefore:
{tex}rate=\;k\lbrack A\rbrack\lbrack D\rbrack^2{/tex}

64802.explanation:
rate {tex} = K\left[ A \right]{\left[ B \right]^2}{/tex}
since the rate of a given reaction is first-order wrt A reactant and second-order wrt B reactant.
order of the reaction is the sum of powers of each reactant in rate law expression.
so, the order of reaction = 1+2 = 3 (a chemical reaction in which the rate of reaction is proportional to the concentration of each of three reacting molecules).

64803.explanation: With an increase in temperature the effective molecular collisions increases, hence the rate of reaction also increases.

65371.explanation: Oxygen combines with chromium to create a protective film of chromium oxide (Cr2O3) on the surface.

65460.explanation: The oxidation state of Mn in {tex}MnO_4^ -{/tex} is +7. Which means that Mn does not have any unpaired d-electrons left. However, {tex}MnO_4^ - {/tex} is deep purple in colour because of charge transfer from the ligand (O2–) to the metal center. This is called a ligand-to-metal charge transfer.

65461.explanation: Cu2+ has an electronic configuration of [Ar] 3d9 with the presence of one unpaired electron which is responsible for paramagnetism with a magnetic moment of 1.8 - 2.2. It shows a blue colour due to the d-d transition of this unpaired electron in the visible region.

65462.explanation: This effect is particularly pronounced in the case of lanthanides, as the 4f subshell which is filled before 5d is not very effective at shielding the outer shell (n = 5 and n = 6) electrons. Thus the shielding effect is less able to counter the decrease in radius caused by increasing nuclear charge. This leads to "lanthanoid contraction".

65463.explanation: O2 is carried in the haemoglobin protein by the heme group. The heme group (a component of the haemoglobin protein) is a metal complex, with iron as the central metal atom, that can bind or release molecular oxygen. The structure of haemoglobin is as given below:

65464.explanation: In Mn2O7, each Mn is tetrahedrally surrounded by oxygen including Mn-O-Mn bridge.

65465.explanation: Zinc, cadmium, and mercury of group 12 have full d10 configuration in their ground state as well as in their common oxidation states and hence, are not regarded as transition metals. However, being the end members of the three transition series, their chemistry is studied along with the chemistry of the transition metals.

65466.explanation: Both CuSO4 and CuF2 are white in colour when they are anhydrous.

65467.explanation: Ionisation isomerism arises when the counter ion in a complex salt is itself a potential ligand and can displace a ligand which can then become a counter ion. Here Br- and SO42- exchanged places as counter ion and ligand. So these complexes exhibit ionisation isomerism.

65468.explanation: Ethylenediaminetetraacetate ion is a hexadentate ligand as it can bind to the central metal atom/ion through 6 donor atoms i.e. 2 N and 4 O atoms.

65469.explanation: Vitamin B12, cyanocobalamine, the antipernicious anaemia factor is a coordination compound of Cobalt.

65471.explanation: The linkage isomer of the given complex is [Co(ONO)(NH3)5]+2 which is red in colour.

65472.explanation:
Linkage isomerism is exhibited in those coordination compounds which have ambidentate ligands means ligands which can bind through two different donor atoms.
SCN​​​​- can bind to the central metal atom/ion either through S or N. NO​​​​​2​​​​- can bind to the central metal atom/ion either through N or O. CN​​​​​- can bind to the central metal atom/ion either through C or N. Thus all of these can exhibit linkage isomerism.

65473.explanation: CrCl3 .6H2O can be written as [Cr(H2O)6]Cl3. The three chloride ions are outside the square bracket means they satisfy the primary valence and hence are ionisable. Thus, it will give three chloride ions per formula unit.

65475.explanation: The given option is correct because it is a double salt (a double salt dissociates into simple ions completely when dissolved in water) whereas others are complexes which don't dissociate completely into solution.

65476.explanation: The given compound will dissociate to give [Co(NH​​​​​3)6]2+ and 2 Cl​​​​​- ions in solution i.e. a total of 3 ions. The two Cl​​​​​- ions satisfy the primary ionisable valence of Co​​​​​2+ whereas the 6 NH​3​​​ molecules satisfy the non ionisable secondary valence of Co​​​​​2+.

65477.explanation: Potassium ion K+ carries a +1 charge. So the overall charge on the given complex is -1. Now CO is a neutral ligand. Hence the oxidation number of Co in this complex is -1.

65616.explanation:
The preparation of alkyl chloride is carried out by passing dry hydrogen chloride gas through a solution of alcohol.
ROH + HCl {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} RCl + H2O
The reactions of primary and secondary alcohols with HCl require the presence of a catalyst, ZnCl2. With tertiary alcohols, the reaction is conducted by simply shaking with concentrated HCl at room temperature.

65617.explanation:
neo-Pentyl bromide is (CH3)3CCH2Br. C attached to Br is given position 1, so 2 -CH3 groups are placed on 2nd C of the propane (parent chain).
Therefore, the IUPAC name of neo-Pentyl bromide 1-Bromo-2,2-dimethylpropane.

65618.explanation: A mixture of an alkyl halide and aryl halide gives an alkylarene when treated with sodium in dry ether and is called Wurtz-Fittig reaction. In above reaction ethyl bromide and bromobenzene react with sodium in dry ether to give ethyl benzene.

65620.explanation: In chlorobenzene, the hybridization of carbon attached to Cl is sp2, and in methyl chloride hybridization of C attached to Cl is sp3. In sp2 hybridization, s-character is 33% and in sp3 s-character is 25%. The sp2 hybridized carbon with a greater s-character is more electronegative and can hold the electron pair of C—X bond more tightly than sp3-hybridized carbon in haloalkane with less s-character resulting in a short bond length of C-Cl bond. Since it is difficult to break a shorter bond than a longer bond, means it is stronger. Also in chlorobenzene, the electron pairs on Cl atom are in conjugation with {tex}\pi{/tex}-electrons of the ring, so C—Cl bond acquires a partial double bond character due to resonance which makes the bond stronger.

65621.explanation: Since halogen atoms are more electronegative than carbon, the carbon-halogen bond of alkyl halide is polarized; the carbon atom bears a partial positive charge whereas the halogen atom bears a partial negative charge.

65622.explanation: The forces of attraction between the molecules of a compound get stronger as they get bigger in size and have more electrons. Also, for a straight-chain compound, the points of interaction between the molecules are more than for a branched compound having the same molecular formula. Thus CH3CH2CH2CH2Cl has the highest melting point since it is the longest chain compound among the given options.

65705.explanation:
The Dow process is the electrolytic method of bromine extraction from brine and was Herbert Henry Dow's second revolutionary process for generating bromine commercially.
Dow's Process may also refer to the hydrolysis of chlorobenzene in the preparation of phenol. Benzene can be easily converted to chlorobenzene by electrophilic aromatic substitution. It is treated with dilute sodium hydroxide at 350 °C and 300 bar to convert it to sodium phenoxide, which yields phenol upon acidification. This reaction is quickened manifold in the presence of electron-withdrawing groups (such as - NO2) ortho and/or para to the halogen group.

65707.explanation:
Victor Meyer's test: This test consists of the following steps.

  • The given alcohol is first converted into its alkyl iodide by treating it with HI.
  • The alkyl iodide is then treated with silver nitrite (AgNO2) to convert it into the corresponding nitroalkane.
  • The nitroalkane is then treated with nitrous acid (HNO2), i.e., NaNO2 + HCl.
  • The resulting solution is finally made alkaline with aqueous NaOH or KOH.

If blood red colouration appears it is a primary alcohol.
If blue colouration appears it is a secondary alcohol.
If the solution remains colorless, it indicates tertiary alcohol.

65708.explanation: Phenol is reduced to benzene when it is distilled with zinc dust or its vapour is passed over granules of zinc at 400oC.

65709.explanation:
Secondary alcohol is a compound in which a hydroxy group, ‒OH, is attached to a saturated carbon atom which has two other carbon atoms attached to it.
{tex}H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_H^H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_H^H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{OH}^H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_H^H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_H^H - H{/tex}

65710.explanation:
CH3MgBr reacts with CH3OH and form CH4.
Grignard Reagent act as both base as well as a nucleophile. In the presence of alcohol, H2O or other groups having acidic hydrogen Grignard reagent act as base and abstract acidic H.

65711.explanation: The Williamson ether synthesis is an organic reaction, forming an ether from an organohalide and deprotonated alcohol (alkoxide). This reaction was developed by Alexander Williamson in 1850. Typically it involves the reaction of an alkoxide ion with a primary alkyl halide via an SN2 reaction.

Acetophenone {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 
Benzophenone {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 
Acetophenone contain {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{\mathrm O}{\underset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{/tex} unit thus it well give haloform test and precipitate CHI3 (yellow ppt). On other hand benzophenone does not form haloform.

65861.explanation: CH3CH2CHO will give aldol reaction because of the presence of alpha hydrogen in it.

65862.explanation: aldehydes give positive fehling's test with a red precipitate of Cu2O

65864.explanation:
Aldehydes are more reactive than ketones towards nucleophiles. The addition of nucleophiles is based upon the positive charge present on carbon atom of  group. in aldehyde  group is present with at least one alkyl group (except formaldehyde) which has +1 effect and which decreases the positive charge of carbon, thereby making the attack to nucleophilic in ketones having minimum of two alkyl groups.
Thus nucelophilic addition order is:
{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}\;>\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}\;>\;{({\mathrm{CH}}_3)}_2\mathrm{CO}\;>{/tex}{tex}\;{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_3-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\overset{\mathrm O}{\overset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_3{/tex}

65865.explanation: CH3COCH3 will not give a silver mirror test (Tollens Test). Tollens test is given by aldehydes only and HCOOH is the only acid that gives tollen's test. Ketones do not give tollen's test.

65866.explanation:
Aldehydes and ketones react with NH2OH (hydroxylamine) to form oximes as shown in the given reaction.
RCOR' + NH2OH  {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} RR'C=NOH  (oxime)

65867.explanation: Acetaldehyde (CH3CHO) have alpha hydrogen hence will undergo aldol reaction in presence of base rather than cannizaro reaction. Cannizaro reaction is given when there is no alpha hydrogen present on carbonyl group.

65868.explanation:
C6H6 + CH3COCl {tex}\xrightarrow{AlCl_3}{/tex} C6H5COCH3
This is known as Friedel craft acylation reaction. AlCl3 acts as a lewis acid and will generate CH3CO+ carbocation and this will attack benzene to give C6H5COCH3.

65869.explanation:
{tex}CH_3COR\;+\;I_2\;+\;NaOH\;\rightarrow CHI_3\;+\;RCOO^-Na^+{/tex}
Iodoform test is a characteristic test given by methyl ketones. CHI formed is yellow precipitate.

65997.explanation: Mixture of ortho, meta, and para nitroaniline is formed because of the formation of anilinium ion which is formed by direct nitration of aniline.

65998.explanation: A primary nitro compound on reduction will give primary amine. The reduction can be done using Fe/HCl or Sn/HCl.

65999.explanation: This is Libermann's nitroso reaction.  Secondary amines react with HNO2 to give N-N nitrosodialkylamine (R2N-N=O). Nitrosamines are water-soluble yellow oils which when warmed with phenol and concentration H2SO4 gives a green color solution and with alkali, they give a blue color solution. Tertiary amines do not react with nitrous acid.

66000.explanation: But, R−NH2 {tex}\xrightarrow[cold]{\text{4NO}_2}{/tex} R−{tex}\underset{\text{alcohol}}{\text{oh}}{/tex} + N2 + H2O

66001.explanation: Aliphatic primary amines react with nitrous acid (prepared in situ from NaNO2 and a mineral acid such as HCl) to form aliphatic diazonium salts, which is unstable and decomposes to give a carbocation and evolve N2 gas. The carbocation so formed reacts with water from medium to give further produce alcohol.

66002.explanation:
(CH3)2NH, because of +I effect of two alkyl group which increases electron density on nitrogen hence basicity increases compare to CH3NH2 which has only one alkyl group with +I effect.
In C6H5NH2, lone pair on nitrogen is in resonance with the phenyl system hence lone pair availability on N of -NH2 group decreases and basicity also decreases.
In case, (C6H5)2NH, there are two phenyl rings attached to -NH group, the lone pair on N is engaged in resonance with two phenyl systems, and hence it is least basic.

66003.explanation: Benzene sulphonyl chloride, C6H5SO2Cl2, is called Hinsberg reagent. It is used to distinguish between primary, secondary and tertiary amines.

66004.explanation: Only primary amines react with CHCl3 and alc. KOH to produce foul-smelling isocyanide. This test is known as Carbylamine Test (Hoffman's Isocyanide Test) for primary amines.

66005.explanation: Aniline reacts with NaNO2 and HCl to produce HNO2 (nitrous acid). HNO2 reacts with aniline at low temperature (273-278 k) to give benzene diazonium chloride which is stable.

66006.explanation:
In carboxylic acids, the intermolecular hydrogen bonding is very strong compared to alcohols, therefore, it has the highest boiling point.
On the other hand, alcohols form stronger hydrogen bonds (intermolecular) compared to amines. And alkanes are non-polar so do not form any hydrogen bonds.
As the strength of hydrogen bonding increases, the boiling point also increases. 
Thus boiling point order will be HCOOH > CH3OH > CH3NH2 > CH3CH3.

66113.explanation: Only α-amino acids are obtained on hydrolysis of proteins.

66115.explanation: A unit formed by the attachment of a base to 1′ position of sugar is known as nucleoside. When nucleoside is linked to phosphoric acid at 5′-position of sugar moiety, we get a nucleotide. Nucleotides are joined together by phosphodiester linkage between 5′ and 3′ carbon atoms of the pentose sugar. So Phosphate - sugar - base is the sequence in nucleic acids.

66120.explanation: Glucose is an aldohexose. It has an aldehyde group. It is a six membered sugar so the term hexose.

66121.explanation: A disaccharide, maltose is composed of two α-D-glucose units in which C1 of one glucose (I) is linked to C4 of another glucose unit (II).

66122.explanation: DNA contains four bases viz. adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C) and thymine (T). So Uracil is not present in DNA.

174455.explanation:
Moles of 2.5 L of 1 M NaOH = 2.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 2.5
Moles of 3.0 L of 0.5 M NaOH = 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 = 1.5
Total males of NaOH solution = 2.5 + 1.5 = 4.0
(Total volume of solution = 2.5 + 3.0 = 5.5 L)
Thus, M1V1 = M2V2
4 = M2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molarity of resultant solution M2
= {tex}\frac{4}{5.5} \mathrm{M} \approx{/tex} 0.73 M

232122.explanation:
Xsolvent = 0.8 (Given) It means that nsolvent (n1) = 0.8 and nsolute (n2) = 0.2
Using formula m = {tex}n_{2} \times \frac{1000}{n_{1} \times M_{1}}=0.2 \times \frac{1000}{0.8 \times 18}{/tex} = 13.88 mol kg-1

232124.explanation:
The molality of 20% (mass/mass) aqueous solution of KI can be calculated by following formula.
{tex}m=\frac{w_{2} \times 1000}{M w_{2} \times w_{1}}{/tex}
20% aqueous solution of KI means that 20 gm of KI is present in 80 gm solvent.
{tex}m=\frac{20}{166} \times \frac{1000}{80}=1.506 \approx{/tex} 1.51 mol/kg

232127.explanation:
Liquid M and N form an ideal solution. Vapour pressures of pure liquids M and N are 450 and 700 mm Hg respectively.
{tex}\begin{aligned} \therefore \ p_{N}^{\circ}>p_{M}^{\circ} \end{aligned}{/tex}
so, by using Raoult's law
yN >xN ...(i)
xM > yM ...(ii)
Multiplying (i) and (ii) we get
yN XM > yM XN

{tex}\therefore \ \frac{x_{M}}{x_{N}}>\frac{y_{M}}{y_{N}}{/tex}

232131.explanation:
According to Henry’s law (at constant temperature)
{tex}p_{\mathrm{gas}}=K_{\mathrm{H}} \times \chi_{\mathrm{gas}( \text { solute } }{/tex} = {tex}K_{\mathrm{H}} \times\left[1-\chi_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\text { solvent })}\right]{/tex}
{tex}p_{\mathrm{gas}}=K_{\mathrm{H}}-K_{\mathrm{H}} \chi_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex}
Pgas = partial pressure of the gas above its solution with a liquid (solvent) say water.
{tex}\chi_{\mathrm{gas}}{/tex} = mole fraction of the gas (solute) in the solution.
{tex}\chi_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = mole fraction of water (solvent).

[i.e. Pgas = KH ] Higher the value of KH, higher will be the partial pressure of the gas (pgas), at a given temperature. The plot of pgas vs {tex}\chi_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} gives a (-ve) slope.
Pgas = KH - KH {tex}\times \ \chi_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex}
Comparing the above equation with the equation of straight line y - m x + c
Slope = - KH , intercept = KH
So, (i) Higher the value of KH more (-ve) will be the slope and it is for z (KH = 40 K bar)
(ii) Higher the value of KH, higher with the value of intercept, i.e. partial pressure and it is also for z.

232138.explanation:
According to Dalton’s law of partial pressure
ptotal = pA + pB
= {tex}p_{A}^{\circ} \chi_{A}+p_{B}^{\circ} \chi_{B}{/tex} ...(i)
Given, {tex}p_{A}^{\circ}{/tex} = 400 mm Hg, {tex}p_{B}^{\circ} {/tex} = 600 mm Hg
{tex}\chi_{B}=0.5, \chi_{A}+\chi_{B}=1{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \chi_{A}=0.5{/tex}
On substituting the given values in Eq. (i). We get,
{tex}p_{\text {total }}=400 \times 0.5+600 \times 0.5{/tex} = 500 mm Hg
Mole fraction of A in vapour phase,
{tex}Y_{A}=\frac{p_{A}}{p_{\text {total }}}=\frac{p_{A}^{\circ} \chi_{A}}{p_{\text {total }}}=\frac{0.5 \times 400}{500}=0.4{/tex}
Mole of B in vapour phase,
YA + YB = 1
YB = 1 -0 .4 = 0.6

232140.explanation:
For ideal solution,
P =x’A P°A + x'B P°B
x’A = 0.4, x’B = 0.6
p°A =7 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 Pa, p°B = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 Pa
On substituting the given values in Eq. (i),
we get
p = 0.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 7 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 + 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 103
= 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 Pa = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 Pa
In vapour phase,
{tex}x_{A}=\frac{p_{A}}{p}=\frac{x^{\prime} A p_{A}^{\circ}}{p}=\frac{0.4 \times 7 \times 10^{3}}{1 \times 10^{4}}=0.28{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} xB = 1 - 0.28 = 0.72 [{tex}\because{/tex} xA + xB = 1]

232143.explanation:
At constant temperature, the solubility of a gas (S) varies inversely with Henry’s law constant (Ku)
{tex}K_{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{\text { Pressure }}{\text { Solubility of a gas in a liquid }}=\frac{P}{S}{/tex}
Thus, the higher the value of KH at a given pressure, the lower is the solubility of the gas in the liquid.

232147.explanation:
Vapour pressure of water (p°) = 760 torr
Number of moles of glucose = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass }(\mathrm{g})}{ \text { Molecular mass (g mol }\left.^{-1}\right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{18 \mathrm{g}}{180 \mathrm{gmol}^{-1}}=0.1 \mathrm{mol}{/tex}
Molar mass of water = 18 g/mol
Mass of water (given) = 178.2g
Number of moles of water = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of water }}{\text { Molar mass of water }}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{178.2 \mathrm{g}}{18 \mathrm{g} / \mathrm{mol}}=9.9 \mathrm{mol}{/tex}
Total number of moles = (0.1 + 9.9) moles = 10 moles
Now, mole fraction of glucose in solution = Change in pressure with respect to initial pressure i.e. {tex}\frac{\Delta p}{p^{\circ}}=\frac{0.1}{10}{/tex}
or {tex}\Delta p=0.01 p^{\circ}{/tex} = 0.01 {tex}\times{/tex} 760 = 7.6 torr
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Vapour pressure of solution = (760 - 7.6) torr = 752.4 torr

232149.explanation:
Given, p° = 185 Torr at 20°C
ps = 183 Torr at 20°C
Mass of non-volatile substance, m = 1.2 g
Mass of acetone taken = 100g
M = ?
As, we have {tex}\frac{p^{\circ}-p_{s}}{p_{s}}=\frac{n}{N}{/tex}
Putting the values, we get,
{tex}\frac{185-183}{183}=\frac{\frac{1.2}{M}}{\frac{100}{58}} \Rightarrow \frac{2}{183}=\frac{1.2 \times 58}{100 \times M}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad M=\frac{183 \times 1.2 \times 58}{2 \times 100}{/tex}
M = 63.684 = 64 g/mol

232153.explanation:
Give, KH = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 atm, {tex}\chi_{N_2}{/tex} =0.8
{tex}n_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}{/tex} = 10 moles, ptotal = 5 atm
{tex}p_{N_2}{/tex} = {tex}p_{\text {total }} \times \chi_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=5 \times 0.8{/tex} = 4 atm
According to Henry’s law,
{tex}p_{N_{2}}=K_{H} \times \chi_{N_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}4=10^{5} \times x_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\chi_{N_{2}}=4 \times 10^{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{n_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+n_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}=4 \times 10^{-5}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{n_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}}{n_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}+10}=4 \times 10^{-5}{/tex}
{tex}n_{\mathrm{N}_{2}}=4 \times 10^{-4}{/tex}

232155.explanation:
The relative lowering of vapour pressure :
{tex}\frac{-\Delta p}{p^{o}}=\chi_{2}{/tex} (mole fraction of solute)

232156.explanation: In case of positive deviation from Raoult's law, the observed vapour pressure is greater than the ideal vapour pressure and boiling point of azeotrope becomes lower than either of pure liquid.

232161.explanation:
For the relation, {tex}\pi=C R T=\frac{n}{V} R T{/tex}
Given, mass of urea = 0.6 g
Molar mass of urea = 60 g mol-1
Mass of glucose = 1.8 g
Molar mass of glucose = 180 g mol-1
{tex}\pi=\frac{\left[n_{2}(\text { urea })+n_{2}(\text { glucose })\right]}{V} R T{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{\left(\frac{0.6}{60}+\frac{1.8}{180}\right)}{100} \times 1000 \times 0.0821 \times 300{/tex}
{tex}=(0.01+0.01) \times 10 \times 0.0821 \times 300{/tex}
{tex}\pi{/tex} = 4.92 atm

232165.explanation:
The expression of elevation of boiling point,
{tex}\Delta T_{b}=K_{b} \times m \times i{/tex}
{tex}=k_{b} \times \frac{w_{2} \times 1000}{M_{2} \times w_{1}} \times i{/tex}
where, m =molality
i =van’t Hoff factor = 1 (for non-electrolyte/non-associable)
w2 = mass of solute in g-= 1 g (present in both of the solutions)
M2 = molar mass of solute in g mol-1 (same solute in both of the solutions)
Wl = mass of solvent in g = 100 g (for both of the solvents A and B)
Kb = ebullioscopic constant
So, the expression becomes,
{tex}\Delta T_{b} \propto K_{b}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta T_{b}(A)}{\Delta T_{b}(B)}=\frac{K_{b}(A)}{K_{b}(B)}=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} {tex}\left[\text { Given } \frac{K_{b}(A)}{K_{b}(B)}=\frac{1}{5}\right]{/tex}

232167.explanation:
Given,
p°= vapour pressure of pure water of 25° C = 35 mm Hg
{tex}\chi_B{/tex} = mole fraction of solute (urea)
{tex}=\frac{n_{B}}{n_{A}+n_{B}}=\frac{\frac{0.60}{60}}{\frac{360}{18}+\frac{0.60}{60}}=\frac{0.01}{20+0.01}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{0.01}{20.01}=0.0005{/tex}
i = van’t Hoff factor = 1 (for urea)
Now, according to Raouit'g law
{tex}A_p=\chi_{B} \times i \times p^{\circ}{/tex}
On substituting the above given values, we get
{tex}\Delta{/tex}p = 0.0005 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 35 = 0.017 mmHg

232169.explanation:
Kf = 4.0 K kg mol-1 (Given)
m = 0.03 mol kg-1 (Given)
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = ?
For K2SO4, i = 3
It can be verified by the following equation :
{tex}\mathrm{K}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{K}^{+}+\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
Using formula
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=i K_{f} \times m{/tex}
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=3 \times 4 \times 0.03=0.36 \mathrm{K}{/tex}

232173.explanation:
Concentration of BaCl2 = 0.01M (Given)
{tex}\pi_{X Y}=4 \pi_{\mathrm{BaCl}_{2}}{/tex} (Given)
{tex}i \times C R T=4 \times i \times C R T{/tex} ...(i)
For the calculation of i,
XY{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} X+ + Y- (Here, i=2)
BaCl2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Ba2+ + 2Cl-  (Here, i=3)
Putting the values of i in (i)
2 {tex}\times{/tex} [XY] = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} [BaCl2]
2 {tex}\times{/tex} [XY] = 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.01
So, the concentration of XY = 0.06 mol L-1
= 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 mol L-1

232174.explanation: In a lead storage battery, sulphuric acid is consumed as : Pb + PbO2 + 2H2SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2PbSO4 + 2H2O

232178.explanation:
Molecules of benzoic acid dimerise in benzene as:
2(C6H5COOH) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} (C6H5COOH)2
Now, we know that depression in freezing point ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf) is given by the following equation:
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=i \times K_{f} \times m=\frac{i \times K_{f} \times w_{\text {solute }} \times 1000}{M w_{\text {solute }} \times w_{\text {solvent }}}{/tex} ...(i)
Given, wsolute (benzoic acid) = w g
wsolvent (benzene) = 30 g
Mwsolute (benzoic acid) = 122 g mol-1, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 2 K
Kf = 5 K kg mol-1, % {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 80 or {tex}\alpha{/tex} = 0.8

Total number of moles at equilibrium = 0.2 + 0.4 = 0.6
{tex}i=\frac{\text { Number of moles at equilibrium }}{\text { Number of moles present initially }}{/tex}
{tex}i=\frac{0.6}{1}=0.6{/tex}
On substituting all the given values in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}2=\frac{0.6 \times 5 \times w \times 1000}{122 \times 30}, \quad w=2.44 \mathrm{g}{/tex}
Thus, the weight of acid (w) is 2.4 g

232180.explanation:
Given, Freezing point of 4% aqueous solution of X.
= Freezing point of 12% aqueous solution of Y
or {tex}\begin{aligned} &\left(\Delta T_{f}\right)_{X}=\left(\Delta T_{f}\right)_{Y} &\left[\because \Delta T_{f}=T_{f}^{\circ}-T_{f}\right] \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}K_{f} \times m_{X}=K_{f} m_{Y}{/tex}
where, mx and mY are molality of X and Y, respectively.
or mX = mY
Now, {tex}\text { molality }=\frac{\text { Number of moles of solute }(n)}{\text { Mass of solvent (in kg) }}{/tex}
{tex}n=\frac{\text { Weight }}{\text { Molecular mass }}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{w_{X}}{M_{X} \times\left(w_{\text {solvent }}\right)_{1}}=\frac{w_{Y}}{M_{Y} \times\left(w_{\text {solvent }}\right)_{2}}{/tex}
Given,  {tex}w_{X}=4\ and \ w_{\text {(isolvent)_1}}=96{/tex}
{tex}w_{Y}=12 \text { and } w_{\text {(solvent)}_{2}}=88{/tex}
MX = A
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{4 \times 1000}{M_{X} \times 96}=\frac{12 \times 1000}{M_{Y} \times 88}{/tex}
Thus, {tex}M_{Y}=\frac{12 \times 1000 \times M_{X} \times 96}{4 \times 1000 \times 88}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{96 \times 12}{4 \times 88} \times \mathrm{A}=3.27 \mathrm{A} \approx 3 \mathrm{A}{/tex}

232182.explanation:
The ionisation of K2HgI4 in aqueous solution is as follows:
K2[HgI4] {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} 2K+ + [HgI4]2-
van’t Hoff factor (i) for ionisation reaction is given as,
i = 1 + {tex}\alpha{/tex} (n - 1)
where,
n = number of ions,
a = degree of ionisation or dissociation
From above equation, it is clear that n = 3
i = 1 + 0.4 (3 - 1) [Given, % a = 40% or a = 0.4]
= 1.8

232184.explanation:
We know that,
Depression in freezing points ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf)
{tex}T_{f}^{\circ}-T_{f}=K_{f} \times m \times i{/tex}
where, Kf = molal depression constant 
m - molality = {tex}\frac{w_{\text {solute }} \times 1000}{M_{\text {solute }} \times w_{\text {solvent }}(\text { in } \mathrm{g})}{/tex}
i = van't Hoff factor
For diluted milk
{tex}\Delta T_{f_{1}}=K_{f} \times m_{1} \times i{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0-(0.2) \Rightarrow 0.2=K_{f} \times \frac{w_{\text {milk }} \times 1000}{M_{\text {milk }} \times w_{1}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)} \times 1{/tex}
For pure milk
{tex}\Delta T_{f_{2}}=K_{f} \times m_{2} \times i{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 0-(-0.5)=0.5=K_{f} \times \frac{w_{\text {milk }} \times 1000}{M_{\text {milk }} \times w_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)} \times 1{/tex}
So, {tex}\frac{0.2}{0.5}=\frac{K_{f}}{K_{f}} \times \frac{w_{\text {milk }} \times 1000}{M_{\text {milk }} \times w_{1}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)} \times \frac{M_{\text {milk }} \times w_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}{w_{\text {milk }} \times 1000}=\frac{w_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}{w_{1}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{w_{2}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)(\text { in pure milk })}{w_{1}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)(\text { in diluted milk })}=\frac{2}{5}{/tex}
i.e. 3 cups of water has to be added to 2 cups of pure milk.

232187.explanation:
Elevation in boiling point ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb) = Kb {tex}\times{/tex} m {tex}\times{/tex} i
Depression is a freezing point ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf) = Kf {tex}\times{/tex} m {tex}\times{/tex} i
where, m = molality
For the glucose solution (van’t Hoff factor, i = 1)
{tex}\Delta T_{b}^{1 m}=\Delta T_{f}^{2 m}=2 K{/tex}
so, {tex}K_{b} \times 1 \times 1=K_{f} \times 2 \times 1 \Rightarrow{/tex} Kb = 2Kf

232193.explanation:
Considering the expression of the depression in freezing point of a solution,
{tex}\Delta T_{f}=K_{f} \times m \times i{/tex}
{tex}T_{f}^{\circ}-T_{f}=K_{f} \times \frac{w_{B} \times 1000}{M_{B} \times w_{A}(\mathrm{ing})} \times i{/tex} ...(i)
Here, {tex}T_{f}^{\circ}=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, T_{f}=-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}{/tex}
wB =mass of ethylene glycol = 62 g
Mb =molar mass of ethylene glycol
{tex}\left(\begin{array}{lc}\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_2}&-\underset\vert{{\mathrm{CH}}_2}\\\mathrm{OH}&\;\;\;\mathrm{OH}\end{array}\right){/tex}
= 62 g mol-1
wA =mass of water in g as liquid solvent,
i =van’ t-Hoff factor = 1 (for ethylene glycol in water)
Kf = 1.86 K kg mol-1
On substituting in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}0-(-10)=1.86 \times \frac{62 \times 1000}{62 \times w_{A}} \times 1{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad w_{A}=\frac{1.86 \times 62 \times 1000}{10 \times 62}=186 \mathrm{g}{/tex}
So, amount of water separated as ice (solid solvent)
= 250 - wA = (250 - 186)g = 64 g

232196.explanation:
The solution with maximum freezing point must have minimum number of solute particles. This generalisation can be done with
the help of van’t Hoff factor (z) i.e.
Number of solute particles oc van’t Hoff factor (z)
Thus, we can say directly
Solution with maximum freezing point will be the one in which solute with minimum van’t Hoff factor is present
Now, for Co(H2O)6]Cl3 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} [CO(H2O)6]3+ + 3Cl-
van’t Hoff factor (z) is 4. Similarly for,
{tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right] \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \cdot \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{5} \mathrm{Cl}\right]^{2+}+2 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}{/tex} 'i' is 3
{tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2} \mathrm{]Cl} \cdot 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{4} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]^{+}+\mathrm{Cl}^-\right.{/tex} 'i' s 2
and for {tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}\right] \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}, 'i' is 1 as it does not showionisation. Hence, {tex}\left[\mathrm{CO}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{3} \mathrm{Cl}_{3}\right] \cdot 3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex} have minimum number of particles in the solution.
So, freezing point of its solution will be maximum.

232202.explanation:
Let the degree of association of acetic acid (CH3COOH) in benzene is {tex}\alpha{/tex}, then

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Total moles = {tex}1-\alpha+\frac{\alpha}{2}=1-\frac{\alpha}{2} \text { or } i=1-\frac{\alpha}{2}{/tex}
Now, depression in freezing point ({tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf) is given as
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = i Kfm ...(i)
where, Kf = molal depression constant or cryoscopic constant.
m = molality
{tex}Molality =\frac{\text { number of moles of solute }}{\text { weight of solvent (in kg) }}=\frac{0.2}{60} \times \frac{1000}{20}{/tex}
Putting the values in Eq. (i)
{tex}\therefore \quad 0.45=\left[1-\frac{\alpha}{2}\right](5.12)\left[\frac{0.2}{60} \times \frac{1000}{20}\right]{/tex}
{tex}1-\frac{\alpha}{2}=\frac{0.45 \times 60 \times 20}{5.12 \times 0.2 \times 1000}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 1-\frac{\alpha}{2}=0.527 \Rightarrow \frac{\alpha}{2}=1-0.527{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \alpha{/tex} = 94.6 %

232205.explanation:
{tex}-\Delta T_{f}=i k_{f} m_{2}{/tex}
{tex}=1 \times 2 \times \frac{34.5}{46 \times 500} \times 1000=3{/tex}
Vapour pressure curves shown in this option is in agreement with the calculated value of -{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf.  While the other options are wrong, vapour pressure decreases on cooling.

232210.explanation:
i.e. effective molarity = van’t Hoff factor x molarity
0.5 M C2H5OH (aq) i = 1
Effective molarity = 0.5

232212.explanation:
The elevation in boiling point is
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb = Kb {tex}\cdot \ m{/tex}: m = molality = {tex}\frac{n_{2}}{w_{1}} \times 1000{/tex} [n2 = Number of moles of solute, w1 = Weight of solvent in gram]
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad 2=0.76 \times \frac{n_{2}}{100} \times 1000{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad n_{2}=\frac{5}{19}{/tex}
Also, from Raoult’s law of lowering of vapour pressure :
{tex}\begin{aligned} &\frac{-\Delta p}{p^{o}}=x_{2}=\frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}+n_{2}} \approx \frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}} &\left[\because n_{1}>>n_{2}\right] \end{aligned}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-\Delta p=760 \times \frac{5}{19} \times \frac{18}{100}{/tex} = 36 mm of Hg
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} p = 760 - 36 = 724 mm of Hg

232214.explanation:
van’t Hoff factor (i) = 4 {3K++ [Fe(CN)6]3-
Molality = {tex}\frac{0.1}{329} \times \frac{1000}{100}=\frac{1}{329}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-\Delta T_{f}=i K_{f} \cdot m{/tex}
{tex}=4 \times 1.86 \times \frac{1}{329}=2.3 \times 10^{-2}{/tex}

(As % freezing point of water is 0°C)

232215.explanation:
Thermal decomposition of Mn compound (X), i.e. KMnO4 at 513 K results in compound F(i.e. K2MnO4 ), MnO2 and a gaseous product. MnO2 reacts with NaCl and concentrated H2O4 to give a pungent gas Z(i.e. Cl2). The reactions involved
are as follows:
{tex}\underset{(X)}{2{\mathrm{KMnO}}_4}\xrightarrow[\triangle]{513\;K}\underset{(Y)}{\;{\mathrm K}_2{\mathrm{MnO}}_4}+{\mathrm{MnO}}_2+{\mathrm O}_2(g){/tex}
{tex}{\mathrm{MnO}}_2+4\mathrm{NaCl}+4{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm{SO}}_4\longrightarrow{\mathrm{MnCl}}_2+4{\mathrm{NaHSO}}_4+2{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O+\underset{\underset{Pungent\;gas}{(Z)}}{{\mathrm{Cl}}_2(\mathrm g)}{/tex}

232217.explanation:
Molality = {tex}\left(\frac{20}{172}\right) \times \frac{1000}{50}=2.325 \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad-\Delta T_{f}=2=i K_{f} \cdot m{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad i=\frac{2}{1.72 \times 2.325}=0.5{/tex}

232220.explanation: The pair that has similar atomic radii is Mo and W. It is due to lanthanoid contraction. The factor responsible for lanthanoid contraction is the imperfect shielding of one electron by another in the same set of orbitals. Shielding of 4f is very less due to its diffused shape. As a result, nuclear charge increases. Hence, Mo and W have similar atomic radii.

232221.explanation:
The 3d-transition series is

In 1st ionisation, one electron will be removed from 4s2 subshell/orbital.
With an increase in atomic number (Z), i.e. with an increase in a number of protons in the nucleus, effective nuclear charge (Z*) also increases from Sc to Zn.
IE {tex}\propto{/tex} Z*
So, IE order of the given element will be,
Ti < Mn < Ni < Zn

232224.explanation:
Actinoids show a variety of oxidation states due to comparable energies of 5f, 6d and 7s energy levels.
In the actinoids family (5f -block), uranium (U), neptunium (Np), plutonium (Pu) and americium (Am) have highest possible oxidation states of +6, + 7, +7 and +6 respectively.

232226.explanation:
The spin only magnetic moment ({tex}\mu{/tex}) of each ion can be calculated as:
{tex}\mu=\sqrt{n(n+2)} \mathrm{B} \mathrm{M}{/tex}
[{tex}\because{/tex} n = No. of unpaired electron(s)] {tex}\Rightarrow \mu \propto n{/tex}, i.e. higher the number of the unpaired electron, higher will be the value of {tex}\mu{/tex}.

Metal ion Z n (for metal ion) M (BM) Nature
Ti2+ 22 2 (3d2) {tex}\sqrt8{/tex} Paramagnetic
V2+ 23 3 (3d2) {tex}\sqrt{15}{/tex} Paramagnetic
Ti3+ 22 1 (3d1) {tex}\sqrt3{/tex} Paramagnetic
Sc3+ 21 0 (3d0) 0 Diamagnetic

Thus, the correct order of spin only magnetic moments of given hydrated ions will be
Sc3+ < Ti3+ < Ti2+ < V2+

232227.explanation:
C6H5NH3Cl : i = 2;
Ca(NO3)2 : i =3
La(NO3)3 : i = 4;
C6H12O6 : i = 1
Lower the value of i, smaller will be the depression in freezing point, higher will be the freezing temperature, if molalities are equal. Hence, glucose solution will have highest freezing temperature.

232229.explanation:
K2SO4 : i = 3
NaCl : i - 2
Urea : i = 1
Glucose : i =1
Greater the value of i, greater the lowering in freezing point, lower will be the freezing temperature, if molarity in all cases are same. Therefore, K2SO4 solution has the lowest freezing point.

232230.explanation: The maximum number of possible oxidation states of actinoids are shown by neptunium (Np) and plutonium (Pu). These actinoids exhibit oxidation states of +3, +4, +5 and +6

232232.explanation:
Addition of Hgl2 to KI solution establishes the following equilibrium:
Hgl2 + 2KI {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} K2[HgI4]
The above equilibrium decreases the number of ions (4 ions on left side of reactions becomes three ions on right side), hence rises the freezing point.

232233.explanation:
The lanthanide ion that would show colour is Sm3+. Colour of a ompound depends on the number of electrons in 4 f-orbitals. Electronic configuration of given lanthanides are as follows:
Gd3+ = 4f7
Sm3+ = 4f5
La3+ =4f0
Lu3+ = 4f14
Gd3+ have half-filled 4 f-orbitals.
La3+ have no electron in 4 f-orbitals.
Lu3+ have fully-filled 4 f-orbitals.
Only Sm3+ contain 4f5. The electrons can easily undergoes excitation. That result in a formation of colour.

232234.explanation:
The correct order of atomic radii is

Eu > Ce > Ho > N

232245.explanation:
When MnO2(A) is fused with alkali in presence of air then potassium manganate (B) is formed. Potassium manganate (B) is of green colour which disproportionate in a neutral or acidic solution to produce potassium permanganate (C). Potassium permanganate (C) in presence of acidic medium oxidises iodide to iodate.
The reaction can be shown as:

  1. {tex}\overset{+4}{\underset{(A)}{2{\mathrm{MnO}}_{2\;}}}\frac{4\mathrm{KOH},\;{\displaystyle\overset0{{\mathrm O}_2}}}\Delta\overset{+6\;-2}{\underset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(B)\\Potassium\;manganate\\\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(Gree)}{2{\mathrm K}_2{\mathrm{MnO}}_4}}+\overset{-2}{2{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\overset{+\;6}{\underset{(B)}{3{\mathrm K}_2{\mathrm{MnO}}_4}}\overset{4\mathrm{HCl}}\longrightarrow\underset{\;\;\;\;\;\;\;\;(C)\\Potassium\\Permanganate\\(purple)}{\overset{+\;7}{2{\mathrm{KMnO}}_4}}+\overset{+\;7}{{\mathrm{MnO}}_2}+2{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O+4\mathrm{KCl}{/tex}
  3. {tex}\overset{+\;7}{\underset{(C)}{2{\mathrm{KMnO}}_4}}\overset{\mathrm{KI},{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}\longrightarrow\overset{+\;4}{\underset{(A)}{2{\mathrm{MnO}}_2}}+2\mathrm{KOH}+\overset{+5}{\underset{(\mathrm D)}{{\mathrm{KIO}}_3}}{/tex}

Thus, A and D are MnO2 and KIO3 respectively.

232249.explanation:
The most stable oxidation states in the compounds of the given transition metals of 3d-series are,
Sc :+3; Ti: +3, +4; V : +2, +3, +4, + 5; Cu: + 1, +2
The electronic configuration of Sc (Z = 21) is [Ar] 3d1, 4s2.
Due to the presence of only one 3 d-electron
(no pairing energy) and two 4s-electrons, they easily ionise to achieve most stable +3 oxidation state.

232251.explanation:
In the lanthanoid series, the atomic number of fourteen 4 f-block elements ranges from 58 (Ce) to 71 (Lu).
Ytterbium, Yb(Z = 70) has electronic configuration: [Xe]4 f146s2. So, the 71nth electron of lutetium, Lu(Z = 71) should enter into 5d orbital and its (here, Lu is 'X') electronic configuration will be : [Xe]4 f145d16s2. It happens so, because f-block elements have general electronic configuration,
(n - 2) f1 - 14(n -1)d1 - 10ns2. Therefore, the given option is correct.

232253.explanation: Lanthanoid contraction in the lanthanoid series takes place due to the presence of electron(s) in the 4 f-orbitals, f-orbitals have poor shielding effect. As a result, the effective nucleur charge will be more experienced by the 5d and 6s- electrons and it will cause contraction or decrease in both atomic and ionic radii.

= -2.2F + 2.303 RT
= 2.303 RT - 2.2 F

For the given 3d-transition metals,
{tex}\begin{array}{cccc} {\mathrm{V}} & {\mathrm{Fe}} & {\mathrm{Cu}} & {\mathrm{Zn}} \\ {3 d^{3} 4 \mathrm{s}^{2}} & {3 d^{6} 4 s^{2}} & {3 d^{10} 4 s^{1}} & {3 d^{10} 4 s^{0}} \\ {n=3} & {n=4} & {n=0} & {n=0} \end{array}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} n = no. of unpaired electrons]

So, absence of unpaired ^-electrons and larger size of Zn atoms, make the crystal lattice of Zn less closely packed
.

232302.explanation:
The half equations of the reaction are
{tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^- \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{2}{/tex}
The balanced half equations are
{tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}+8 \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{5e^-} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+2 e^{-}{/tex}
On equating number of electrons, we get
{tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^-+16 \mathrm{H}^{+}+10 e^{-} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+8 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{5C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-} \longrightarrow 10 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+10 e^{-}{/tex}
On adding both the equations, we get
{tex}2 \mathrm{MnO}_{4}^-+\mathrm{5C}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{4}^-+16 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}+2 \times 5 \mathrm{CO}_{2}+\frac{16}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}
Thus x, yand z are 2, 5 and 16 respectively.

After 30 min, [N2O5] = 2.75 mol L-1
{tex}\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ 2 \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}(g) \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}(g)+\mathrm{O}_{2}(g) \\t=0 \quad 3.0 \mathrm{M}\\ t=30 \quad 2,75 \mathrm{M}{/tex}
From the equation, it can be concluded that
{tex}\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\Delta t}=\frac{1}{4} \times \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{-\Delta\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\Delta t}=\frac{-(2.75-3.00) \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{L}^{-1}}{30} \Rightarrow \frac{0.25}{30}{/tex}
and {tex}\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=-2 \frac{\Delta\left(\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right)}{\Delta t} \Rightarrow \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta t}=-2 \times \frac{0.25}{30}{/tex}
= -1.667 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 mol L-1 min-1

T1 = (273 + 327) K = 600 K
k2 = 1 dm3 mol-1 s-1 at T2 = (273 + 527) K = 800 K
Now, {tex}\log \frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{T_{2}-T_{1}}{T_{1} T_{2}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{1}{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}=\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 10^{-3}}\left(\frac{800-600}{600 \times 800}\right){/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \log \frac{\left(10 \times 10^{3}\right)}{2.5}=\frac{E_{a}}{0.019} \times \frac{200}{48 \times 10^{4}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} log4 + 3log10 {tex}\simeq{/tex} Ea {tex}\times{/tex} 0. 022

= {tex}\frac{3.6}{0.022}{/tex} {tex}\simeq{/tex} 163.6 kJ mol-1

232324.explanation:
The expression for bacterial growth is
N = N0et
{tex}\frac{N_{0}}{N}=e^{-t}{/tex}
From 0 to 1 hour N' (t) = N0et
From 1 hour onwards, {tex}\frac{d N}{d t}{/tex} = -5N2
On differentiating the above equation from N ' to N we get
{tex}\int_\limits{e N_{0}}^{N}{/tex} N-2 dN = -5 {tex}\int_\limits{1}^{t}{/tex} dt [{tex}\because{/tex} At 1 hour, N' = eN0]
{tex}\left[\frac{1}{N}-\frac{1}{e N_{0}}\right]{/tex} = 5(t - 1)
Multiply both sides by N0, we get
{tex}\frac{N_{0}}{N}-\frac{1}{e}{/tex} = 5N0 (t - 1) or, {tex}\frac{N_{0}}{N}{/tex} = 5N0 (t - 1) + {tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{N_{0}}{N}{/tex} = 5N0t + [{tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex} - 5N0]
On comparing the above equation with equation of straight line, y = mx + c
We get m = 5N0, c = {tex}\frac{1}{e}{/tex}-5N0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Plot of {tex}\frac{N_{0}}{N}{/tex} vs t is shown aside.

232337.explanation:
Let the rate equation be k [A]x [B]y
From 1st values, 0.045 = k[0.05]x [0.05]y ...(i)
From 2nd values, 0.090 = k[0.10]x [0.05]y ...(ii)
From 3rd values, 0.72 = k[0.20]x [0.10]y ...(iii)
On dividing equations (i) by (ii), we get
{tex}\frac{0.045}{0.09}=\left[\frac{0.05}{0.10}\right]^x{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{0.05}{0.10}\right]^{1}=\left[\frac{0.05}{0.10}\right]^{x}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 1
Similarly on dividing Eq. (ii) by (iii) we get
{tex}\frac{0.09}{0.72}=\left[\frac{0.1}{0.2}\right]^{x}\left[\frac{0.05}{0.10}\right]^{y}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{0.01}{0.08}=\frac{0.1}{0.2}\left[\frac{0.05}{0.1}\right]^{y}{/tex}
0.25 = {tex}\left[\frac{0.05}{0.10}\right]^{y}{/tex}
0.25 = [0.5]y
[0.5]2 = [0.5]y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 2
Hence, the rate law for the reaction
Rate = k [A][B]2

232341.explanation:
The temperature dependence of a chemical reaction is expressed by Arrhenius equation,
{tex}k=A e^{-E_{a} / R T}{/tex} ...(i)
Taking natural logarithm on both sides, the Arrhenius equation becomes,
In k = In A - {tex}\frac{E_{a}}{R T}{/tex}
where, - {tex}\frac{E_{a}}{R}{/tex} is the slope of the plot and In A gives the intercept.
Eq. (i) at two different temperatures for a reaction becomes,
In {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{E_{a}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_{1}}-\frac{1}{T_{2}}\right){/tex} ...(ii)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} In the given problem,
T1 = 400K, T2 = 500 K
k1 = 10-5 s-1, k2 = ?
{tex}-\frac{E_{a}}{R}{/tex} (Slope) = - 4606
On substituting all the given values in Eq. (ii), we get
In {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{10^{-5}}{/tex} = 4606 {tex}\left(\frac{1}{400}-\frac{1}{500}\right){/tex}
In {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{10^{-5}}{/tex} = 2.303
{tex}\frac{k_{2}}{10^{-5}}{/tex} = 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}k2 = 10-4 s-1
Therefore, rate constant for the reaction at 500 K is 10-4 s-1

232350.explanation:
For zero order reaction,
[A0] - [At] = kt ...(i)
where, [A0] = initial concentration
[At] = final concentration at time ‘t'
k = rate constant
Also, for zero order reaction, t1/2 = {tex}\frac{\left[A_{0}\right]}{2 k}{/tex}
Given, t1/2 = 6 h and [A0] = 0.2 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 6 = {tex}\frac{0.2}{2 k}{/tex}
or, k = {tex}\frac{0.2}{2 \times 6}=\frac{1}{60}{/tex}
Now, from Eq. (i)
[A0] - [At] = kt

{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.5 - 0.2 = {tex}\frac{1}{60}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} t [{tex}\because{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{1}{60}{/tex}]
0.3 = {tex}\frac{1}{60}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} t
t = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 60 = 18 h

232352.explanation:
The temperature dependence of rate of a chemical reaction is expressed by Arrhenius equation as, k = {tex}A e^{-E_{\alpha} / R T}{/tex} ...(i)
where, A = Arrhenius factor or frequency factor or pre-exponential factor
R = Gas constant, Ea = Activation energy
Taking log on both sides of the Eq. (i), the equation becomes In k = In A - {tex}\frac{E_{a}}{R T}{/tex}

On comparing with equation of straight line (y = mx + c), the nature of the plot of Ink vs {tex}\frac{1}{R T}{/tex} will be:

  1. Intercept = C = In A
  2. Slope/gradient = m = - Ea = - y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}Ea = y

So, the energy required to activate the reactant, (activation energy of the reaction, Ea is = y)

From the equation, it is clear that k decreases exponentially with Ea . So, the plot-I is correct.
In the plot-II, k is plotted with temperature (in °C but not in K)
So, at 0°C, k {tex}\neq{/tex} 0 and k will increase exponentially with temperature upto 300°C. Therefore, the plot-II is also correct.

Order of the reaction (n) = 1

r1 = k {tex}\cdot{/tex} [A]

For the reaction, {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CHO}(g)\overset{\text{ Decomposes }}\longrightarrow{\mathrm{CH}}_4+\mathrm{CO}{/tex}

Its given, r1 = 1 torr/sec. when CH3CHO is 5% reacted i.e. 95% unreacted. Similarly, r2 = 0.5 torr/sec when CH3CHO is 33% reacted i.e., 67% unreacted.
Use the formula, r {tex}\propto{/tex} (a - x)m
where (a - x) = amount unreacted
so, {tex}\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\frac{\left(a-x_{1}\right)^{m}}{\left(a-x_{2}\right)^{m}}{/tex} or {tex}\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}=\left[\frac{a-x_{1}}{a-x_{2}}\right]^{m}{/tex}
Now putting the given values
{tex}\frac{1}{0.5}=\left(\frac{0.95}{0.67}\right)^{m} \Rightarrow{/tex} 2 = (1.41)m or m = 2

232363.explanation:
According to the Arrhenius equation {tex}k=A e^{-E_{a} / R T}{/tex}
where A = collision number or pre-exponential factor.
R = gas constant
T = absolute temperature
Ea = energy of activation
For reaction R1, k1 = {tex}A e^{-E_{a_{1}} / R T}{/tex} ...(i)
For reaction R2, k2 = {tex}A e^{-E_{a_{2}} / R T}{/tex} ...(ii)
On dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), we get
{tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=e^{-\frac{\left(E_{a_{2}}-E_{a_{1}}\right)}{R T}}{/tex} ...(iii)
[{tex}\because{/tex} Pre-exponential factor ‘A’ is same for both reactions]
Taking In on both the sides of Eq. (iii), we get
In {tex}\left(\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)=\frac{E_{a_{1}}-E_{a_{2}}}{R T}{/tex}
Given, {tex}E_{a_{1}}=E_{a_{2}}{/tex} + 10 kJ mol-1 = Ea2 + 10,000 J mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In {tex}\frac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}=\frac{10,000 \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}}{8.314 \mathrm{J} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{K}^{-1} \times 300 \mathrm{K}}=4{/tex}

232364.explanation:
For first order reaction, k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{a}{a-x}{/tex}
Given, t = 50 min, a = 0.5 M, a - x = 0.125 M
{tex}\therefore \quad k=\frac{2.303}{50} \log \frac{0.5}{0.125}{/tex} = 0.0277 min-1
Now, as per reaction 2H2O2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2H2O + O2
{tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{d t}=\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}{/tex}
Rate of reaction, {tex}-\frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}{/tex} = k [H2O2]
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}k [H2O2] ...(i)
When the concentration of H2O2 reaches 0.05 M,
{tex}\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{2} {/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0277 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.05 [from Eq. (i)]
or {tex}\frac{d\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{d t}{/tex} = 6.93 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol min-1

In the given options, the +R effect is shown by -CH3 and -OCH3 group (-OCH3 > -CH3). These group increases the electron density at o and /^positions. Groups such as -F and -NO2 shows -R-effect (-NO2 > -F). These group decreases the electron density at o and p- positions.
An increase in electron density at p-position makes the unshared electron pair of 'N’ more available and a decrease in electron density at p-position makes the unshared electron pair of 'N’ less available. Here, Ni2+ has d8 -configuration with Cl- as weak ligand.

Therefore, [Ni(H2O)6]2+ has octahedral geometry.

232439.explanation:
The compound used in the treatment of lead poisoning is EDTA. Medication occurs through chelation therapy. Calcium disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid chelates divalent metal ions such as Pb2+ from plasma and interstitial body fluids.
The metal displaces Ca and is chelated, mobilised and usually excreted. Less then 5% CaNa2EDTA is absorbed in the gastrointestinal tract and it possibly increases the absorption of Pb present in the tract. Therefore, it is not recommended for oral use. It is usually given intravenously.

In an aqueous solution, the basicity of 2o-amines (aliphatic) is maximum because of the thermodynamic stability of its conjugate acid.
{tex}\mathop {{\text{E}}{{\text{t}}_2}{\text{NH}}}\limits_{(B)} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \rightleftharpoons \mathop {{\text{E}}{{\text{t}}_2}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ \oplus {{\text{H}}_2}}\limits_{{\text{ Conjugate acid }}} + \mathop O\limits^ \oplus {\text{H}}{/tex}
{tex}{{\text{E}}{{\text{t}}_2}\mathop {\text{N}}\limits^ \oplus {{\text{H}}_2}}{/tex} is a sterically symmetric tetrahedral ion as it contains an equal number (two) of bulkier Et-group and small size H-atoms. Here, two H-atoms give additional stability through hydrogen bonding with H2O (solvent) molecules.

Aromatic amines (D) are always weaker bases than aliphatic amines, because of the conjugation of Ip of electrons of N (+R-effect) with the benzene ring.
So, the correct order is (D) < (C) < (A) < (B).

  3. [Fe(H2O)6]2+ and [Cr(H2O)6]2+ are the high-spin octahedral complexes of Fe2+ (3d6, n = 4) and Cr2+ (3d5, n = 5) ions and weak field ligand, H2Q respectively. So, spin-only magnetic moment = {tex}\sqrt{n(n+2)}{/tex} of the complexes.
    {tex}\left[\mathrm{Fe}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}, \quad \mu_{1}=\sqrt{4(4+2)}{/tex}
    (n =4), = {tex}\sqrt{24}{/tex} = 4.89 BM
    {tex}\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right)_{6}\right]^{2+}, \quad \mu_{2}=\sqrt{5(5+2)}{/tex}
    So, {tex}\mu_{1} \approx \mu_{2}{/tex}.Thus, statement (c) is correct.
  4. [Ni(NH3)4(H2O)2]2+ is also a high-spin octahedral complex of Ni2+ (3d8,n = 2)
    {tex}\mu=\sqrt{2(2+2)}=\sqrt{8}=2.83 \mathrm{BM}{/tex}
    Thus, statement (d) is correct.

232486.explanation:
The reaction o f alkyl halide with amine or amides is a nucleophilic substitution reaction. More nucleophilic nitrogen is more reactive with an alkyl halide. Compound (A) is benzamide and lone pair of ‘N ’ of it, is not available in this compound.

In compound (D), lone pair of ‘N ’ are available but in compound (C), the lone pair of ‘N ’ are not readily available due to the presence of electron-withdrawing group (-CN ) attach to a benzene ring.
In compound (B), i.e. phthalimide, ‘N ’ is not nucleophilic due to the presence of two polar groups attached to it that pulls lone pair towards them. Hence, the correct order is:
(B) < (A) < (C) < (D)

232488.explanation:
Complete removal of both the axial ligands (along the z-axis) from an octahedral complex leads to the following splitting pattern.

The single electron in the {tex}d_{x^2}-_{y^2}{/tex} orbital is being repelled by four ligands, while the electron m the {tex}d_{x^2}{/tex} orbital is only being repelled by two ligands. Thus, the energy of the {tex}d_{x^2}-_{y^2}{/tex} increases relative to that of {tex}d_{x^2}{/tex}. A more stable arrangement arises when both the eg electrons pair up and occupy the lower energy {tex}d_{x^2}{/tex} orbital. This leaves the {tex}d_{x^2}-_{y^2}{/tex} orbital empty.
Thus, four ligands can now approach along +x, -x , + y and - y directions without any difficulty as {tex}d_{x^2}-_{y^2}{/tex} orbital is empty.
However, ligands approaching along +z and -z directions meet very strong repulsive forces from filled {tex}d_{x^2}{/tex} orbitals. Thus, only four ligands succeed in bonding to the metal. A square planar complex is formed, the attempt to form an octahedral complex being unsuccessful.

For a diamagnetic complex, there should not be any unpaired electron in the valence shell of central metal.

In [Co(NH3)6]Cl3, Co (III) has d6-configuration in a strong ligand field, hence all the electrons are paired and the complex is diamagnetic.
In Na3[Co(ox)3], Co (III) has d6-configuration and oxalate being a chelating ligand, very strong ligand and all the six electrons remains paired in lower t2g level, diamagnetic.
In [Ni(H2O)6]Cl2, Ni (II) has 3d8-configuration and H2O is a weak ligand, hence

In K2[Pt(CN)4], Pt(II) has d8-configuration and CN- is a strong ligand, hence all the eight electrons are spin paired . Therefore, complex is diamagnetic.
In [Zn(H2O)6](NO3)2, Zn (II) has 3d10 configuration with all the ten electrons spin paired, hence diamagnetic.

232594.explanation:
Basic strength can be compared by the reaction of an alkanamine and ammonia with proton.

Basicity of an amine in aqueous solution depends upon the solubility of ammonium cation formed by accepting proton from water. The stability of ammonium cation depends upon the following factors.

  1. +I effect
  2. Steric effect
  3. Solvation effect

In first case, on increasing the size of alkyl group + I effect increases and the positive charge of ammonium cation gets dispersed more easily, Therefore, order of basicity is 2° amine > 1° amine > ammonia. In second case, substituted ammonium cation is also stabilised by solvation with water molecules. Greater the size of ion, lesser will be the solvation and less stabilised is the ion.

Considering both steric and solvation effect, it can be concluded that greater the number of H-atoms on the N-atom, greater will be the H-bonding and hence more stable is the ammonium cation. But in case of -C2H5 group, +I effect predominates over H-bonding. Therefore, order is 2° amine > 1° amine > ammonia i.e. (C2H5)2NH > C2H5NH2 > NH3

232831.explanation: Acidic strength is inversely proportional to pKa value. The acidity of phenols is due to greater resonance stabilisation of phenoxide ion relative to phenol. Therefore, any substituent which stabilises the phenoxide ion more by dispersal of negative charge will tend to increase the acidity of phenol. Electron withdrawing groups (-NO2) increases the acidic strength of phenol whereas electron-donating group (-OCH3) decreases the acidic strength of phenol. In case of -NO2 group attached to phenol, the dispersal of negative charge is more pronounced at o and p-position than at m-position. Thus, order of acidic strength of nitrophenol is: p-nitrophenol > o-nitrophenol and the correct order of the pKa values of give option is

232902.explanation:
Alkaline hydrolysis of an ester (carboxylic acid derivative) follows acyl SN2 mechanism.

Rate of SN2 mechanism depends on the polarity of group of — COOR group. Electron withdrawing group (-R > - I) increases the rate of SN2 reaction whereas electron donating group (+ R > + I) decreases the rate of SN2 reaction. Here, the nature of functional groups attached para to the benzene ring are:
{tex}-\mathrm{NO}_{2}>-\mathrm{Cl}>-\mathrm{OCH}_{3} \\ \ \ (-R) \ \ \quad(-I) \ \ \ \quad(+R){/tex}
so, the order of hydrolysis will be,
{tex}\begin{aligned} \mathrm{III}>\mathrm{II}>\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{IV}\\ (-R) \ \ (-I)\ \ \ \ \ \ (+R) \end{aligned}{/tex}

287671.explanation:
Boiling point elevation depends on the solvent, of course, but we're assuming water, and upon the concentration of the solute and how many particles into which the solute breaks. Colligative properties like boiling point elevation, depending on the total number of particles in solution. The solvent used here is water. So elevation in boiling point will be above a boiling point of water for all solutions.
ΔT=i × Kb​× m, where i = van Hoff's factor, Kb​ = boiling point elevation constant, m = molality
Higher ΔT will have a higher boiling point.
For 0.1 M KNO3​ (i)= (1+(n−1) α) =2 (α for salt is 1)
Similarly for 0.1 M BaCl2​ (i)=3  (α for salt is 1)
For 0.1 M Na3​PO4​ (i)=4  (α for salt is 1)
For 0.1 M K2​SO4​(i)=3  (α for salt is 1)
Thus, Na3​PO4​ has the highest boiling point.

Since both {tex}\mathrm{Al}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{3} and \mathrm \ {K}_{3}\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_{6}\right]{/tex} give the same number of ions, therefore they have the same van't Hoff Factor.

287696.explanation:

Weak electrolytes dissociate partially in aqueous solution while strong electrolytes dissociate completely e.g. Boric acid.
Nonelectrolytes are compounds that do not ionize at all in the solution e.g., CHCl3.

287726.explanation: A catalyst is a substance, which increases the rate of the reaction without itself being consumed at the end of the reaction, and the phenomenon is called catalysis. There are some catalysts which decrease the rate of reaction and such catalysts are called a negative catalyst. Obviously, the catalyst accelerating the rate will be a positive catalyst. However, the term positive is seldom used and the catalyst itself implies a positive catalyst. Catalysts are generally foreign substances but sometimes one of the products formed may act as a catalyst and such a catalyst is called "auto catalyst" and the phenomenon is called autocatalysis.

287727.explanation:
{tex}N=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} N_{0}{/tex}
{tex}N_{0}= Initial \ amount, N=Amount \ at \ 't' time n= No. \ of \ half-lives{/tex}
Given, {tex}\frac{N}{N_{0}}=\frac{1}{8} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n}=\frac{1}{8}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow n=3{/tex}
As by definition, {tex}n=\frac{\text { total time }}{\text { half life time }}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad half \ life \ time =\frac{96}{3}=32 minutes{/tex}

{tex}=58.2956 \mathrm{min} \approx 58.3 \mathrm{min}{/tex}

{tex}H_{2}+C l_{2} \quad \xrightarrow{hv } \quad 2 H C l {/tex}
Rate = K

Activation energy of backward reaction = b

⇒ Reaction is exothermic.

{tex}\ln \frac{{{{\left[ {{N_2}{O_5}} \right]}_0}}}{{{{\left[ {{N_2}{O_5}} \right]}_t}}} = kt{/tex}

287736.explanation:
Reactions that involve absorption of heat energy are called endothermic reactions. For such reactions. {tex}\sum { { H }_{ P } } >\sum { { H }_{ R } } {/tex}
In the given graphs  and the heat of products is more than the heat of reactants and thus they represent endothermic reactions. But in  only a small amount of energy is absorbed (less difference between the energy of reactants and products). Thus represents maximum activation energy.
Note: Activation energy is the excess energy that the reactant molecule must possess to cross the energy barrier.

287738.explanation:
A third-order reaction is a chemical reaction where the rate of reaction is proportional to the concentration of each reacting molecules. In these reactions, the rate is usually determined by the variation of three concentration terms.

  1. 2 NO + O2 ⇢ 2 NO2 
    R= k[NO]2 [O2]
    Order = 2 + 1 = 3
  2. 2 NO + Br2 ⇢ 2 NOBr 
    R= k[NO]2 [Br2]
    Order = 2 + 1 = 3
  3. 2 NO +Cl2 ⇢ 2 NOCl
    Rate, R= k[NO]2 [Cl2]
    Order = 2 + 1 = 3
