287921.explanation:
Rules to determine the hybridization of a coordination compound:

  1. Central metal ion release the electron equal to its oxidation state,
  2.  Metal ion arrange empty orbitals equal to its coordination number,
  3. These empty orbital arrange in a specific geometry,
  4. Hybridized orbitals of metal ion form bonds with the lone pair orbitals of the ligand.

Apply these rules for [CoCl4​]2−

287922.explanation:
The octahedral crystal field splitting (Δo) depends upon the field produced by the ligands and charge on the metal ion. For the given metal ion, some ligands are able to produce strong fields in which case the splitting will be larger whereas other produces weak fields and consequently result in small splitting of d orbitals. In general, ligands can be arranged in the series in the order of increasing field strength. A spectrochemical series is a list of ligands arranged in increasing field strength. A spectrochemical series is a list of ligands arranged in increasing their abilities to split the d orbital energies. 
I−<Br−<Cl−<F−<OH−<H2O<NH3<en<NO2−<CN−<CO. Note that the order in the spectrochemical series is the same regardless of what metal atom (or ion) is present. NO2-, CN- and CO are strong called strong field ligands because they cause a large splitting of the d orbital energy levels. On the other hand, the halide ions and hydroxide ions are week field ligands because they split the d orbitals to a lesser extent.

287923.explanation: Carbon monoxide is {tex}\pi{/tex}-acid ligand as it characterizes both metals to carbon and carbon to metal bonding. The latter is known as {tex}\pi{/tex} -acidic character.

287924.explanation: Ag+of AgCN is soluble in excess cyanide ion to form {tex}\left[\mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{CN})_{2}\right]^-{/tex}complex.

287925.explanation: The complex [Co(NH3)3Cl3] does not have optical isomers due to the presence of an element of symmetry. However, it exists in two geometrical isomers which are cis and trans or fac and mer isomers.

287926.explanation: CoCl2 is a weak Lewis acid, reacting with chloride ion to produce salt containing the tetrahedral [CoCl4]2- ion. CoCl2 is blue when anhydrous, and a deep magenta colour when hydrated, for this reason, it is widely used as an indicator for water.

287927.explanation:
The correct order for the wavelength of absorption in the visible region is [Ni(NO2)6]4-<[Ni(NH3)6]2+<[Ni(H2O)6]2+. The central metal ion in all the three complexes is the same. Therefore, absorption in the visible region depends on the ligands. The order in which the CFSE values of the ligands increases in the spectrochemical series is as follows :
{tex}H_2O < NH_3 < NO_2{/tex}

287928.explanation:
Pi-bonds are formed by lateral overlapping between two hybridized p-orbitals.
Now, in the case of [Ag(CN)2]–, two nitrile groups are having triple bonds between carbon and nitrogen are bonded to the silver atom to form the compound.
[N≡C-Ag-C≡N]–
A nitrile group has one sigma and two {tex}\pi{/tex}-bonds.
Hence [Ag(CN)2]– will have four {tex}\pi{/tex}-bonds.

287929.explanation:

V5+: 3d04s0 does not have an unpaired electron. Hence, {tex}{Na}_{3} \mathrm{VO}_{4}{/tex} is a colourless species.

287969.explanation:
Aldol condensation of formaldehyde and acetone gives:
{tex}\mathrm{CH}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COCH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{HOCH}_{2}-\mathrm{CH}_{2}-\mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_{3}{/tex}
Heating the product gives of methyl vinyl ketone:
{tex}H O C H_{2}-C H_{2}-C O-C H_{3} \stackrel{\Delta}{\longrightarrow} C H_{2}=C H-C O-C H_{3}{/tex}

287970.explanation:
{tex}\underset{(Blue)}{CH_3CHO}\;+2CuO\;\rightarrow CH_3COOH\;+\underset{(Red\;ppt)}{Cu_2O}\;{/tex}
Thus in this reaction, the precipitate of copper oxide {tex}\left(\mathrm{Cu}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right){/tex} is formed.

287972.explanation: ​​​Ethanoic acid is also known as Acetic acid (CH3COOH) and Methanecarboxylic acid.

287973.explanation:
The reaction is known as benzoin condensation.
{tex}\underset{Benzaldehyde}{2C_6H_5CHO}\;\xrightarrow{al.\;KCN}\;\underset{Benzoin}{C_6H_5CH(OH)COC_6H_5}{/tex}

287975.explanation: Vinegar is an aqueous solution of acetic acid (CH3COOH).

287977.explanation:
Cannizzaro reaction is given by aldehydes that do not contain an alpha hydrogen atom. In this reaction, a molecule of carbonyl compound is oxidized to carboxylic acid and another molecule is reduced to an alcohol.
Acetaldehyde does not give Cannizaro's reaction as it contains an alpha hydrogen atom. Instead, it undergoes the aldol condensation reaction.

287981.explanation:
Formation of iodoform is commonly used as a test for alcohol having −CH(OH)CH3, grouping, acetaldehyde, and ketones having at least one alkyl group as a methyl group. i.e., for detecting the presence of −CHOH.CH3:−COCH3, and CH3CHO. The esters (as given in the options) are hydrolyzed into alcohol or acids.
{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{COOC}}_2{\mathrm H}_5\xrightarrow[\mathrm{Heat}]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{COOH}+{\mathrm C}_2{\mathrm H}_5\mathrm{OH}{/tex}  
Ethyl alcohol so formed will give the iodoform test.
C2H5COOCH3 → C2H5COOH + CH3
Hence it will not give the iodoform test.

287982.explanation: NaHSO3 reacts with acetaldehyde to form an addition compound which is precipitated. Acetaldehyde can be recovered with dilute HCl. Acetophenone does not give the addition reaction with NaHSO3.

287995.explanation:
Hinsberg reagent (benzene sulphonyl chloride) is used to distinguish between primary, secondary, and tertiary amine.
Primary amine, such as methylamine ( a primary amine) will dissolve in an alkali solution after it has undergone a reaction with the Hinsberg reagent.
{tex}C_{6} H_{5} S O_{2} C l+C H_{3} N H_{2} \rightarrow C_{6} H_{5} S O_{2} N H C H_{3} \stackrel{K O H}{\longrightarrow} C_{6} H_{5} S O_{2} N K C H_{3}(\text {watersoluble}){/tex}
Secondary amine forms alkyl sulphonamide which does not form a salt with alkali and hence remains insoluble (solid) in alkali solution.
{tex}C_6H_5SO_2Cl\;+R_2NH\;\rightarrow\underset{Water\:insoluble,\;ether\:soluble}{C_6H_5SO_2NR_2}\;\xrightarrow{KOH}\;No\;action{/tex}
Tertiary amine does not react with Hinsberg reagent.
{tex}C_{6} H_{5} S O_{2} C l+R_{3} N \rightarrow \text { No reaction. }{/tex}

287996.explanation:
LiAlH4​/ether reduces aryl nitro compounds to azo compounds.
{tex}\underset{Nitrobenzene}{2C_6H_5NO_2}\;\xrightarrow{LiAlH_4}\;\underset{Azobenzene}{C_6H_5N=N-C_6H_5}{/tex}

287997.explanation:
When acetamide is treated with bromine and NaOH, methylamine is obtained.
The reaction is Hoffmann bromamide degradation reaction in which an amide is converted to an amine-containing one carbon atom less.
{tex}\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{CONH}_{2}+\mathrm{Br}_{2}+4 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{NH}_{2}+2 \mathrm{KBr}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{CO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}{/tex}

287998.explanation: Melting points are normally higher for primary amides due to the presence of hydrogen bonding.

287999.explanation:
{tex}R\overset O{\overset\parallel C}NH_2+Br_2\;+KOH\rightarrow RCONHBr\;+\;KBr\;+H_2O{/tex}
{tex}RCONHBr\;+KOH\;\rightarrow RNCO\;+KBr\;+\; H_2O{/tex}
{tex}RNCO\;+\;2KOH\;\rightarrow RNH_2\;+K_2CO_3{/tex}
{tex}RCONH_2\;+Br_2\;+4KOH\;\rightarrow RNH_2+2KBr\;+K_2CO_3\;+2H_2O{/tex}
Hence, RNC  does not act as an intermediate in Hofmann rearrangement.

288000.explanation:
Nitrosoamines are prepared from secondary amines with nitrous acid.
{tex}\mathrm{HO}-\mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{H}^{+} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{NO}^{+}{/tex}
{tex}R_{2} \mathrm{NH}+\mathrm{NO}^{+} \rightleftharpoons R_{2} \mathrm{NH}^{+} \mathrm{NO} \rightarrow R_{2} \mathrm{N}-\mathrm{NO}+\mathrm{H}^{+}{/tex}
Nitrosoamines are readily hydrolyzed to the amines by boiling with concentrated H2SO4 (or dilute HCl).
{tex}​​R_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{NO}+{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O\quad\xrightarrow[{Or\;H_2SO_4}]{HCl}\;{\mathrm R}_2\mathrm{NH}+{\mathrm{HNO}}_2{/tex}
The above reaction is known as Liebermann's nitroso reaction and it is used for the detection of secondary amine.

288001.explanation:
Acetamide in the presence of NaOH and heat changes to acetic acid while ethylamine does not undergo any such reaction.
{tex}\underset{Acetamide}{CH_3CONH_2}\;\xrightarrow{NaOH/\triangle}\;CH_3COOH\;+NH_3\uparrow{/tex}

288002.explanation:
This reaction is a chemical test for the detection of primary amines, in which the amine is heated with alcoholic potassium hydroxide and chloroform. If a primary amine is present, the isocyanide is formed. The reaction is known as carbylamine reaction.
{tex}R N H_{2}+C H C l_{3}+3 K O H \rightarrow R N^{+} \equiv C^{-}+3 K C l+3 H_{2} O{/tex}

288003.explanation:
The weakest base is CH3​NHCHO.
The lone pair of electrons cannot be easily donated to suitable Lewis acid as the nitro group is an electron-withdrawing group.
As amides are not basic in nature and in the given option amide structure and other compounds are amines.

288004.explanation: Acetophenone, benzonitrile, benzaldehyde, and benzoic acid all contain electron-withdrawing groups, therefore, in all these compounds the electron density over the phenyl ring is lower than in benzene. Thus, benzene undergoes electrophilic nitration most readily.

288005.explanation: (C2​H5​)2​NH (2o amine) is the strongest base. The basic nature of amines is due to the presence of lone pair of electrons on a nitrogen atom which is available for the bond formation with Lewis acid. Due to the +I effect, 2o amine is a better base than 1o amine and NH3​. In the case of aromatic amines, the lone pair on nitrogen atom involved in resonance, therefore, not available for bond formation, so aromatic amines are less basic.

288006.explanation: Aryl halides (ArX) do not undergo nucleophilic substitution because they have a strong C−X bond due to resonance.

288007.explanation:
Benzene can be converted to the p-toluidine as follows :

  1. Step 1: Friedel Craft alkylation reaction of benzene in the presence of anhydrous AlCl3, gives toluene.
  2. Step 2: Nitration of toluene gives p- nitro toluene.
  3. Step 3: Reduction Reaction of p- nitro toluene with Sn / HCl reduces nitro to the amino group, thus forming p-toluidine.

Note: If nitration is done first then the same product may not be formed as the Nitro group is a meta director.

288008.explanation:
Basicity of amines is due to the availability of an unshared pair (lone pair) of electrons on nitrogen. This lone pair of electrons is available for the formation of a new bond with a proton or Lewis acid.
Pyridine is less basic than triethylamine because lone pair of an electron in nitrogen in pyridine is delocalized.

288009.explanation:
{tex}C_6H_5CONHCH_3\;\xrightarrow[{HCl}]{Zn-Hg}\;C_6H_5CH_2NHCH_3{/tex}
This reaction is known as Clemenson reduction.

288010.explanation:
When reduced with a neutral reducing agent like zinc dust and aqueous ammonium chloride, nitrobenzene gives phenylhydroxylamine.
{tex}\underset{Nitrobenzene}{C_6H_5NO_2}\;+Zn\;+H_2O\;\;\xrightarrow[{Warm}]{NH_4Cl}\;\underset{Phenyl\;hydroxyl\;amine}{C_6H_5\overset H{\overset\vert N}-OH}\;+ZnO\\{/tex}

288015.explanation: Vitamin B12 contains cobalt, while haemoglobin contains iron and Chlorophyll contains magnesium.

288016.explanation:
The functional groups that are found in an amino acid are -COOH and NH3​ (amine group). So every amino acid has a central carbon with an H, R group (varies for each amino acid), a carboxylic group, and an amine group. These groups are joined to a single (aliphatic) carbon. The carbon directly attached to a carboxyl group is the alpha position, so the amino acids in proteins are all alpha-amino acids.
{tex}H_2N-\overset H{\underset R{\overset\vert{\underset\vert C}}}-COOH{/tex}

288017.explanation:
A disaccharide is a sugar (a carbohydrate) composed of two monosaccharides. It is formed when two sugars are joined together and a molecule of water is removed.
C12​H22​O11​ + H2​O ⟶ C6​H12​O6​ + C6​H12​O6​ give glucose and fructose.
All others cannot hydrolyze in water making them monosaccharides.

288018.explanation: A conjugated protein is a protein to which another nonprotein group (a prosthetic group), such as a carbohydrate, lipid group, etc., is attached. Some examples of conjugated proteins are lipoproteins, glycoproteins, phosphoproteins, hemoproteins, flavoproteins, metalloproteins, phytochromes, cytochromes, and opsins. In flavoproteins, the nonprotein part is the flavin molecule which is a derivative of riboflavin.

288019.explanation:
The inclusion complex is a host-guest addition compound.
The space available within the crystal lattice of the host molecule, i.e., amylose is in the form of tunnels and the size of the tunnels is such that it can easily accommodate guest molecules, i.e., iodine molecules to form an addition compound called the inclusion complex.
The blue colour, however, disappears on heating and reappears on cooling.

288021.explanation:
The primary structure of a protein refers to the linear sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide chain. The primary structure is held together by covalent bonds such as peptide bonds, which are made during the process of protein biosynthesis or translation.
The primary structure of proteins tells us about the sequence of amino acids in which they are linked.

288022.explanation: The bases of messenger RNA are complementary to those of the DNA strand. Again each adenine (A) of DNA these appear on RNA, the base uracil(U) opposite guanine (G) is cytosine (C) and vice versa and opposite thymine (T) is adenine (A). Thus the anticodon of GCA would be CGU.

288023.explanation: Enzymes with two active sites are called allosteric enzymes. One of these sites is the catalytic site for the concerned reaction. Another site may be the site for inhibition of catalysis by enzymes or to activate the enzyme further.

288024.explanation: α−Keratin is completely insoluble in cold or hot water. Haemoglobin, Ribonuclease, and Adenine are either soluble in cold or hot water.

288025.explanation: Carbogen is a mixture of carbon dioxide and oxygen in the ratio of 5% CO2 + 95% O2. It is used to stimulate breathing in the treatment of respiratory disease.

288026.explanation:
A rule of thumb for determining the D/L isomeric form of an amino acid is the corn rule.
The groups COOH, R, NH2, and H (where R is an unnamed- carbon main) are arranged around the chiral center carbon atom. Sighting from the hydrogen atom, if these groups are arranged counter-clockwise around the carbon atom, then it is the D-form. If clockwise, it is the L- form.
According to priority order:−NH2,−COOH,−CH2OH,−H.

288027.explanation:
Genetic code shows degeneracy i.e., the occurrence of more than 1 codon per amino acid. All the amino acids except methionine and tryptophan are coded by more than 1 codon. 
For example, valine is specified by GUU, GUC, GUA, GUG. 
This shows that the first two bases are common in all the four codons coding for valine. But the third base can be changed.

288028.explanation: Any amino acid has its lowest solubility at its isoelectric point and the isoelectric point is the pH at which the amino acid carries no charge.

288029.explanation: Thymine is one of the four nucleobases in the nucleic acid of DNA. The others are adenine, guanine, and cytosine. Thymine is also known as 5-methyl uracil, it is a pyrimidine nucleobase.

288030.explanation: Cellulose has many hydroxy groups (-OH) that allow cellulose microfibrils to form hydrogen bonds with water. So cellulose is hydrophilic. But cellulose is a structural polymer. Cellulose is made of very long microfibrils bonded to each other so cellulose can not fully mix with or move in the water. Thus cellulose is insoluble.

The polarity between the C−X bond increases by increasing the + I effect which increases by increasing the alkyl group by which X of C−X easily eliminates. In CH3CH2CH2Br the polarity decreases due to the presence of double bond presence of CO group (−I) and less number of the alkyl group.
{tex}C H_{3} C H_{2} C H_{2} B r+K O H \stackrel{a l c}{\rightarrow}{/tex} {tex}C H_{3} C H=C H_{2}+K B r+H_{2} O{/tex}

The acid reacts with the metal to give hydrogen gas.
Hence, in the above reaction, CH3​CH2​OH behaves as an acid.

{tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm{OH}\xrightarrow[{KOH}]{I_2}\underset{Iodoform}{CHI_3}+\mathrm{HCOO}^-\mathrm K^+{/tex}

292782.explanation: All the alcohols are water-soluble due to the formation of extensive hydrogen bonding between water molecules and alcohol molecules. Lower alcohols are highly soluble in water and their solubility decreases with an increase in the molecular weight.

292783.explanation:
The solution of ZnCl2​ in concentrated HCl is known as Lucas reagent.
Application: It is used to differentiate primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols.
On adding the alcohol to Lucas reagent, tertiary alcohol reacts immediately forming a ppt of alkyl chloride. If the ppt. appears after a few minutes, the alcohol is secondary. If no ppt, is obtained in cold the alcohol is primary.

292785.explanation: Trans configuration is more stable than cis configuration because in cis-configuration the H groups are thrown closely enough together to cause crowding or repulsion. A gain between 1,2 and 1,3-configurations, in 1-3, the OH groups are placed further apart to minimize the repulsion. Hence, more stable is 1,3-configuration.

292786.explanation: Ketone and esters react with a Grignard reagent to give tertiary alcohols and aldehyde react with Grignard to form secondary alcohols. The reaction with formaldehyde will produce a primary alcohol.

292787.explanation:
m-Methoxy phenol is the strongest acid.
The Methoxy group at the meta position exerts an only -I effect. Due to this, it withdraws electrons from all the nuclear positions of the benzene ring including the one holding the O−H group consequently, the electron density in the O−H bond decreases.
Hence, m-methoxy phenol becomes more acidic than o and p-methoxy phenols.

292801.explanation: The black substance obtained is a mixture of mercury and mercuric amino chloride.

292912.explanation: Cytochromes are conjugated proteins consisting of an apoprotein and a prosthetic group (heme). The heme consists of porphyrin with a central iron atom.  The name haemoglobin is the concatenation of heme and globin, reflecting the fact that each subunit of haemoglobin is a globular protein with an embedded heme (or haem) group; each heme group contains an iron atom, and this is responsible for the binding of oxygen. The most common types of haemoglobin contain four such subunits, each with one heme group.

292913.explanation: Methyl-α-D-glucoside and methyl-β-D- glucoside differ at C-1, hence are called anomers.

292919.explanation: When CHCl3 is exposed to sunlight and air, it slowly decomposes into phosgene and hydrogen chloride.

292924.explanation: The name hemoglobin is derived from the words heme and globin, reflecting the fact that each subunit of hemoglobin is a globular protein with an embedded heme group. Each heme group contains one iron atom, that can bind one oxygen molecule through ion-induced dipole forces. Hence Hb has four sub-units.

292925.explanation: Two possible binding sites in guanine.

292932.explanation:
Urotropine:
Formaldehyde forms hexamethylenetetramine with ammonia. 
{tex}\underset{Hexamethylene\;tetramine}{6HCHO\;+\;4NH_3}\;\rightarrow(CH_{12})N_4\;+6H_2O{/tex}
This is used as a urinary antiseptic in medicine, in name of 'Urotropine'.

292934.explanation: Aryl halides can be prepared by the decomposition of aryl diazonium salts in presence of copper halides solution dissolved in the corresponding halogen acid, the diazo group is replaced by a halogen atom. This reaction is known as Sandmeyer's reaction.

292936.explanation: Here ethanol reacts as a nucleophile attacking the carbocation generated by the heterolysis of the halide. Since p-methoxybenzyl bromide yields the most stable carbocation, it reacts most readily with ethanol.

292937.explanation:
The position taken up by a third group entering the ring depends on the nature of the two groups already present.
When both groups show o-and p-directing nature, the directive power of each group is generally in the following order:
{tex}\mathrm{O}^{-}>\mathrm{NH}_{2}>\mathrm{NR}_{2}>\mathrm{OH}>\mathrm{OMe}>\mathrm{NHAc}>\mathrm{Me}>\mathrm{X}{/tex}
In the case of compound  A 

the arrows are used to indicate the possible positions that may be taken up by an incoming group.

Due to the presence of bulky group, the steric effect works, and the favourable product will be this.

292938.explanation:
Phenol on reaction with carbon dioxide in the presence of a base like NaOH gives Salicylic acid. It is Kolbe's Reaction.
Phenol on reaction with chloroform in the presence of a base like NaOH gives Salicylaldehyde. It is Reimer-Tiemann reaction.
Both are shown in the figure.

292941.explanation: Ketone reacts with HCN to form an addition product, known as cyanohydrin compound.

292945.explanation: Lithium (EN=1.0) is less electronegative than Mg (EN=1.2) therefore, organolithium compounds are more nucleophilic than Grignard reagents. As a result, organolithium compounds add more reactive CO2, as well as less reactive resonance stabilized lithium salt of carboxylic acid thus, formed to produce ketones.

292946.explanation: In Wolff-Kishner reduction carbonyl group of aldehydes and ketones is converted into a methylene group. Aldehydes or ketones are heated with hydrazine and KOH or potassium tertiary butoxide in ethylene glycol to forms alkanes.

292947.explanation: This reaction is called the Rosenmund reaction.

337386.explanation: Alkyl halides can be obtained by treating alcohol with haloacids. The reactivity of tertiary alcohols with the haloacids is the highest and primary alcohols are the lowest. Formation of alkyl chloride using primary and secondary alcohols requires the usage of a catalyst ZnCl2, where dry hydrogen chloride gas is passed through a solution of alcohol or by heating a mixture of alcohol and concentrated aqueous halogen acid. Reaction with tertiary alcohols does not require a catalyst and can be carried out by using tertiary alcohols. the reaction is conducted by simply shaking with concentrated HCl at room temperature.

337388.explanation: Production of alkyl chlorides from alkanes can be carried out by chlorination under the presence of UV light. Chlorine molecule(Cl2) under the influence of UV light forms free radicals, which react with alkanes to form a mixture of isomeric monohaloalkane and polyhaloalkanes.

337399.explanation: SN1 reactions are generally carried out mainly in polar protic solvents (like H2O) and they follow first-order kinetics. This means that the rate of reaction depends only on one reactant. This reaction favours tertiary alkyl halides because of the high stability of the formed carbocation. The carbocation forms when the molecule is polarized in water to form a carbocation and halide ion attacked by the nucleophile. The reactivity of the halides are R–I> R–Br>R–Cl>>R–F. Hence, (CH3)3C—I will undergo the reaction most readily.

337401.explanation: First, we need to identify the longest carbon chain. Once we do that, the actual structure should read CH3—CH2—CH(CH3)—CH2—Br. –Br, the functional halide group is attached to the first carbon atom (1- Bromo), so we start the numbering from that position. The methyl group branch is bond to the second carbon atom in the chain(2-methyl). The number of carbons in the unbranched parent chain is four, thus giving the name butane. The IUPAC is named 1-Bromo-2-methylbutane.

337406.explanation:
A mixture containing two enantiomers in equal proportions but with zero optical activity because the opposite optical rotations of the two enantiomers cancel out each other rotation is called a racemic mixture. 
Options (a) is a chiral carbon atom and it will undergo an SN1 substitution mechanism to give a racemic mixture.
Option (b) is not an asymmetric carbon.
Option (c) contains a secondary carbon asymmetric atom, which has less reactivity towards an SN1 substitution mechanism.

343561.explanation:
Halogen exchange reactions are those reactions in which one halide replaces another. This reaction is known as the Finkelstein reaction. In

  1. Halogen (-X) is replaced by iodine(presence of dry acetone).
  2. Addition of hydrogen halide on the alkene.
  3. Halogen replaces the alcoholic group.
  4. Halogen replaces the hydrogen of the benzoic ring.

343569.explanation: Asymmetric/chiral carbon atom is that in which all of its four valencies with four different groups or atoms (can not be superimpose). In molecules (i), (ii), and (ii), all have asymmetric carbon as each carbon has satisfied all four valencies with four different groups of atoms.

351307.explanation:
For the equation
5Br–(aq) + BrO–3(aq) + 6H+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3Br2(aq) + 3H2O(l)
Rate law expression can be written as
{tex}\quad \frac{-1}{5} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{BrO}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{-1}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{+1}{3} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br} \dot{\mathrm{O}}_{3}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{-5}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}} =\frac{5}{6} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}

An asymmetric carbon needs to be surrounded by four species different from each other through covalent bonds. Hence, the atoms b and c are asymmetric.

y = mx + c

386920.explanation:
Due to the polar nature of alkyl halides and the increase in molecular weight compared to their parent alkanes, the boiling points of alkyl halides are higher than that of their parent alkanes. The boiling points of alkyl halides depend on the molecular mass and the size of the halogen atom (decrease from I to F). With the increase in size, mass, and the number of electrons in halogen atoms, the magnitude of Van Der Waals forces increase and the boiling point also increases. The boiling point of alkyl halides reduces in the order RI > RBr > RCl > RF.
Therefore, the order of increasing order of boiling points should be Butane < 1-Chlorobutane < 1-Bromobutane < 1-Iodobutane.

430916.explanation: Starch is a polymer of {tex}\alpha{/tex} − D − (+) − glucose and consists of two components-Amylose and Amylopectin. Natural starch has approximately 10 - 20% of amylose and 80 - 90% of amylopectin. Amylose is water-soluble component which gives blue color with iodine. Chemical amylose (polysaccharide) is a long straight (unbranched) chain having only {tex}\alpha{/tex} − D − (+) − glucose units joined together by α-glycosidic linkages involving C1 of one glucose and C4 of the next.it can have 100 - 3000 D - glucose units i.e., its molecular mass can range from 10,000 to 5000,000. Amylopectin is a branched-chain polysaccharide insoluble in water which does not give blue color with iodine. It is composed of chains of 25 − 30D - Glucose units joined by {tex}\alpha{/tex} − D − glycosidic linkages between C − 1 of one glucose unit and C − 4 of the next glucose unit (similar to amylose). However, these chains are connected with each other by 1, 6-linkage.

430917.explanation: The colour of the starch complex is so deep, that it can be detected visually when the concentration of the iodine is as low as 0.00002 M at 20 °C.

430976.explanation:
351K
Ethanol has a higher boiling point than methoxymethane due to the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding, which requires more energy to break. In contrast, methoxymethane experiences only weak dipole-dipole interactions. Among the given options, 351K is the correct boiling point for ethanol, while 455K corresponds to phenol’s boiling point.

430983.explanation:
Alcohol
Of compounds having similar molecular masses, alcohols will certainly have the highest boiling point due to the presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding which is lacking in hydrocarbons, haloalkanes and ethers.

436311.explanation:
For liquid state:
{tex}P_{M}=P_{A}^{\prime}+P_{B}^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}P_{M}=P_{A}^{0} \times \frac{1}{4}+P_{B}^{0} \times \frac{3}{4}{/tex}
or {tex}P_{A}^{\circ}+3 P_{B}^{0}=4 P_{M}{/tex}
Also from Raoult’s law and Dalton’s law of partial pressure
{tex}P_{A}^{\prime}=P_{A}^{0} X_{A}=P_{M} X_{A}^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}P_{A}^{\circ} \times \frac{1}{4}=\frac{P_{A}^{0}+3 P_{B}^{0}}{4} \cdot X_{A}^{\prime}{/tex}
Similarly {tex}P_{B}^{\circ} \times \frac{3}{4}=\frac{P_{A}^{0}+3 P_{B}^{0}}{4} \cdot X_{B}^{\prime}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \frac{X_{A}^{\prime}}{X_{B}^{\prime}}=\frac{P_{A}^{0}}{P_{B}^{0}} \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{3} \times \frac{1}{3}=\frac{1}{9}{/tex}

or {tex}\Delta T_{f}=\frac{0.15 \times 1.86}{0.512}=0.545{/tex}
Now on diluting the solution to double
{tex}\Delta T_{f} \propto \frac{1}{\text { weight of solvent }}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} freezing point = -0.272°C

While KCI gives only two particles (ions)

{tex}p_{A}^{\circ}=p_{M} \cdot Y_{A}{/tex} and {tex}p_{B}=p_{M} \cdot Y_{B}{/tex}
(where YA and YB are mole fraction of A and B in vapour phase)
{tex}\therefore \ \frac{p_{A}^{\circ}}{Y_{A}}=\frac{p_{B}^{\circ}}{Y_{B}}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{p_{A}^{\circ} \cdot X_{A}}{Y_{A}}=\frac{p_{B}^{\circ} \cdot X_{B}}{Y_{B}}=\frac{p_{B}^{\circ}\left(1-X_{A}\right)}{\left(1-Y_{A}\right)}{/tex}
or {tex}\frac{p_{B}^{\circ}}{X_{A}^{\circ}}=\frac{p_{A}^{\circ}}{Y_{A}^{\circ}}+\left(p_{B}^{\circ}-p_{A}^{\circ}\right){/tex}
or {tex}\frac{1}{X_{A}}=\frac{p_{A}^{\circ}}{p_{B}^{\circ}} \cdot \frac{1}{Y_{A}}+\frac{\left(p_{B}^{\circ}-p_{A}^{\circ}\right)}{p_{B}^{\circ}}{/tex}
This is a straight line equation Y = mx + c

{tex}p_{B}^{\prime}=p_{B}^{\circ} \cdot X_{B}=37 \cdot X_{B}{/tex}
or {tex}7 p_{A}^{\prime}=37 \cdot X_{B}{/tex} ...(ii)

{tex}\because{/tex} [A0] = 4[B0]

{tex}\therefore{/tex} n1 - n2 = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n2 = n1 - 2
Also T = n1 {tex}\times{/tex} t1/2 A and T = n2 {tex}\times{/tex} t1/2 B
{tex}\therefore \frac{n_{1} \times t_{1 / 2} A}{n_{2} \times t_{1 / 2} B}{/tex} = 1
{tex}\therefore \ \frac{n_{1}}{n_{2}}=\frac{15}{5}{/tex} or n1 = 3n2

or [H+] = {tex}\frac{K_{a}}{\alpha}=\frac{K_{a}}{\sqrt{K_{a} / C}}=\sqrt{K_{a} \cdot C}{/tex} 
or log H+ = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}[log Ka + log C]

The rate of the reaction decreases by a factor of 4 if the concentration of reactant B is doubled.

{tex}4=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^y \Rightarrow y=-2{/tex}
Hence order of reaction w.r.t. B is -2

441698.explanation:
Electronic configuration of the given elements are:
Mn {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d5 4s2
Cr {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d5 4s1
Ti {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d2 4s2
V {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 1s2 2s2 2p6 3s2 3p6 3d3 4s2
In general, ionization potential (both 1st and 2nd) increases from left to right across the period due to increase in effective nuclear charge. On this basis, the second IP values should exhibit the trend:
Mn > Cr > V > Ti
But the actual observed order is :
Cr > Mn > V > Ti
Practically, only chromium is exceptional and rest others show the normal trend. This exceptional behaviour of chromium is due to the stable configuration (3d5) that it achieves after the loss of first electron.

  • 3d2 4s2 can show a maximum of 4 oxidation states.
  • 3d3 4s2 can show a maximum of 5 oxidation states.

441702.explanation: The common stable oxidation state of all the lanthanoids is +3. The oxidation state of +2 and +4 are also exhibited by some of the elements. These oxidation states are only stable in those cases where stable 4f0, 4f7 or 4f14 configurations are achieved.

Ions which have unpaired electrons exhibit colour in solution. Ti3+ has an outer electronic configuration of 4s03d1, i.e., 1 unpaired electron.
Thus its solution will be coloured.
Sc3+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} d0
In case of La3+, 4f0 configuration is present and in Lu3+, 4f14 is present. So, there is no possibility of f-f transition, hence these ions do not appear coloured.

441717.explanation:
The order can be explained using the idea of spin correlation. Spin correlation refers to lowering of energy for like (parallel) spins. Spin correlation leading to decrease in repulsion for electrons of like spins than for electrons of different spins is called exchange energy.
Spin correlation and its exchange energy gives an electronic configuration a special stability which is greatest for half-filled electronic configurations. Mn2+ (d5) gets stabilisation due to half-filled configuration.
In Fe2+ (d6) the placing of one extra electron in a subshell destabilises. Placing of 2 electrons in Co2+(d7) destabilises it more. Cr2+(d4) has one vacant subshell. Fe2+ gets more stabilisation compared to Cr2+ through exchange energy.
So the order is as follows : Mn > Fe > Cr > Co

ln {tex}\frac{\mathrm{C}_2}{\mathrm{C}_1}{/tex}

The emf of cell is +0.20 V. So, cell reaction is possible. (as {tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -nEF = -Ve spontaneous)
The half-cell reaction are
At negative pole:
A {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} A+ + e- (oxidation)
At positive pole:
B+ + e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B (reduction)
Hence cell reaction will be
A + B+ {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A+ + B, {tex}E^{\circ}_{\text {cell }}{/tex} = +0.20 V

Now for CH3COOH {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} CH3COO- + H+
{tex}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]=\frac{K_{a}\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COO}^{-}\right]}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} E1 + E2 = {tex}-\frac{0.059}{1}\left[\log \frac{K_{a} y}{x}+\log \frac{K_{a} x}{y}\right]{/tex}
= - 0.0592 [2 log Ka]

log Ka = {tex}-\frac{E_{1}+E_{2}}{0.118}{/tex}
or pKa = {tex}\frac{\left(E_{1}+E_{2}\right)}{0.118}{/tex}

{tex}\propto{/tex} number of unpaired electrons

In [Fe(en)(py)(NH3)2]2+
Fe+2 = [Ar]3d6 (4 unpaired electrons)

In [Co(ox)2(OH)2]-
Co+5 = [Ar]3d4 (4 unpaired electrons)
OX and OH are weak field ligands.
In [Ti(NH3)6]3+
Ti+3 = [Ar]3d1 (one unpaired electron)
Hence complex [Co(OX)2(OH)2]- has maximum number of unpaired electrons, thus show maximum paramagnetism.

444480.explanation:
Since {tex}\log (1)=0{/tex} :
{tex} E=E^0-0.059^* 0=E^0{/tex}

  • The problem statement implies that the oxidation potential is being asked, so we need to find the difference in the EMF before and after the equivalence point.
  • The Nernst equation for {tex}{Fe}^{2+} \rightleftharpoons {Fe}^{3+}+e^{-}{/tex}is:

{tex} E=E_{F e^{3+} / F e^{2+}}^0-\frac{0.059}{1} \log \frac{F e^{3+}}{F e^{2+}}{/tex}

  • At {tex}50 \%{/tex} titration, {tex}[{Fe}^{2+}]=[{Fe}^{3+}{/tex}
  • So, {tex}\log \frac{F e^{3+}}{F e^{2+}}=\log 1=0{/tex}
  • Therefore, {tex}E=E_{F^{3+} / F_e 2^{2+}}^0-0.059^* 0=E_{F_e^{3+} / F_e^{2+}}^0{/tex}
  • Since the problem asks for the EMF of the oxidation electrode formed at {tex}50 \%{/tex} titration, we need to find the potential difference between the {tex}{Fe}^{2+}{/tex} and {tex}{Fe}^{3+}{/tex} redox couple.
  • The EMF at {tex}50 \%{/tex} titration is 0.059 V .

Final Answer: The EMF of the oxidation electrode formed at 50% titration is 0.059 V .

At cathode, 
{tex}\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_{2}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}{/tex}
At the anode, two reactions are possible as following:
{tex}\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+\mathrm{e}^{-} ; \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\mathrm{o}}=1.36 \mathrm{V}{/tex}......(i)
{tex}2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}_{2}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{e}^{-} ; \mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{0}=1.23 \mathrm{V}{/tex}.......(ii)
The reaction at the anode with a lower value of Eo​​​​​cell should be preferred, but oxidation of O2 is a kinetically slow process and needs high voltage thus reaction (i) takes place.

478350.explanation: Gem-dihalides are dihaloalkanes that have two halogen atoms of the same type attached to the same carbon atom in a molecule. The common naming system of gem-dihalides (geminal halide) is alkylidene dihalides. Ethylidene dichloride thus is a gem-dihalide. The chemical formula of ethylidene dichloride is C​​​​​​3​H6Cl​​​2.

482775.explanation: Aluminum chloride (AlCl​​​3 ) is a Lewis acid catalyst and works in the same way as FeCl3 does. Benzene(C​​​6H6 )is converted into chlorobenzene by chlorination of benzene in the presence of AlCl3. The reaction occurs by an electrophilic substitution reaction. Cl2 forms a coordination complex with AlCl3, forming Cl+AlCl4- complex, which gives a slight positive charge to Cl, and AlCl4- is negatively charged. This Cl+ then reacts with the aromatic double bond of the benzene ring to form an additional product, followed by deprotonation to form chlorobenzene and AlCl3 and HCl as the side products.

512011.explanation:
This problem can be solved by determining the order of reaction w.r.t. each reactant and then writing rate law equation of the given equation accordingly as
R = {tex}\frac{d [C]}{d t}{/tex} = k[A]x[B]y
where, x = order of reaction w.r.t A
y = order of reaction w.r.t B
1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k(0.1)x(0.1)y
1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k(0.1)x(0.2)y
2.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k(0.2)x (0.1)y

512220.explanation:
In a galvanic cell, the salt bridge does not participate chemically in the cell reaction. The ions of the electrolyte present in the salt bridge neither react with the ions of the electrode solutions, neither get oxidized nor get reduced at the electrodes.

The electrolysis of alumina by Hall and Heroult's process is carried by using a fused mixture of alumina (Al2O3) and cryolite (Na3AlF6, sodium hexafluoroaluminate) along with minor quantities of aluminum fluoride (AlF3) and fluorspar (CaF2). The addition of cryolite and fluorspar increases the electrical conductivity of alumina and lowers the fusion temperature.

512558.explanation:
According to Faraday's law,
Weight of X deposited (W1) {tex}\propto{/tex} Equivalent weight of X [E1]
Weight of Y deposited (W2) {tex}\propto{/tex} Equivalent weight of Y [E2]
{tex}\therefore\frac{{W}_{{1}}}{{W}_{{2}}}=\frac{{E}_{{1}}}{{E}_{{2}}}{/tex} ...(1)
{tex}\therefore \frac{{W}_{1}}{{W}_{2}}=\frac{\text { Atomic mass / V alency of } {X}}{\text { Atomic mass / V alency of } \mathbf{y}}{/tex} ...(2)
From Equation 2,
{tex}\frac{{W}_{1}}{{W}_{2}}=\frac{\frac{2}{{2}}}{\frac{1}{{1}}}=\frac{1}{{1}}{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Atomic mass ratio of X and Y = 2 : 1]
{tex}\therefore \frac{{W}_{{1}}}{{W}_{2}}=\frac{{1}}{{1}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass ratio = 1 : 1

512591.explanation:
Single electrode potential cannot be determined experimentally by taking a single electrode. Therefore, this single electrode is combined with a second single electrode and the emf of the cell is determined by using a potentiometer. The second electrode is an electrode of a known potential and is called a reference electrode.
Calomel Electrode:
A saturated calomel electrode Hg/Hg2Cl2(s), KCl (solution) is now used as a secondary reference electrode. The potential of the saturated calomel electrode is 0.2422 volts (SRP).
So calomel electrode is reversible with respect to Cl- ion.
Similarly, the quinhydrone electrode is reversible with respect to H+ ion.

512646.explanation:
The order of decrease in reducing character of three metals is Pb > Cu > Ag.

  • Pb has maximum reducing power. Hence, Pb can reduce both copper ions and silver ions.
  • Ag has a minimum reducing power. Hence, it can neither reduce copper ions nor reduce lead ions.
  • The reducing power of Cu is higher than that of Ag but lower than that of Pb. Hence, Cu can reduce silver ions but Cu cannot reduce lead ions.

512745.explanation:
These are characteristics of the first-order reaction.
When the rate of the reaction depends on the first power of the reactant concentration in the rate equation, then the reaction is said to follow first-order kinetics. The change in the concentration of only one reactant will affect the rate of the reaction. The unit of the rate constant for the first-order reaction is s-1. This implies that the rate constant does not depend on the concentration of the reactant. The time taken for half the reactant to get reacted, the half lifetime of the first-order reaction is independent of the concentration. This means that the half-life period of the first-order reaction is constant.

512795.explanation:
Cathode: (P2) Cl2 + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cl-
Anode: 2Cl- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2e- + Cl2 (P1)
(P2) Cl2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cl2(P1)
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{{0 . 0 5 9 1}}{{2}}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{P}_{1}}{{P}_{2}}{/tex}
As for concentration cell {tex}{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\text {o }}{/tex}is zero.
Ecell = {tex}\frac{-0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{P}_{1}}{{P}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -nF Ecell
For a reaction to be spontaneous, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G is to be negative. Thus Ecell must be positive.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ecell = -In {tex}\frac{{P}_{1}}{{P}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ecell = {tex}\frac{0.0591}{2}{/tex} log {tex}\frac{{P}_{2}}{{P}_{1}}{/tex}
For Ecell to be positive;
P2 > P1

512815.explanation:
A chemical reaction occurs as a result of collisions between reacting molecules. Therefore, the reaction rate is given by the total number of effective collisions. More is the no. of effective collisions, more will be reaction rate.
Effective collisions are collision between two reactants with the appropriate orientation & with sufficient energy to overcome the activation energy barrier.

0.2 = k(0.6)x(0.3)y ...(iii)
Dividing (ii) by (i):
{tex}\frac{0.4}{0.1}=\frac{(0.6)^{y}}{(0.3)^{y}}{/tex}

Rate law will be:

512882.explanation:

The activation energy (Ea) = Threshold energy - Average kinetic energy of molecules.

512896.explanation:
For any neutral species a, the order of radius of corresponding anion neutral species and the anion is,
a- > a > a+
Explanation: For any anionic species the no of electrons is greater than the no of protons. Hence the effective nuclear attractive force towards the valence electrons will be less than the neutral atom. Hence size will be maximum.
For any cationic species, the no of electrons is lesser than the no of protons. Hence the effective nuclear attractive force towards the valence electrons will be greater than the neutral atom. So the atomic size or radius will be minimum.
So the correct sequence of radius or size will be, I- > I > I+.

512943.explanation:
Activation energy: Ea​ is the energy that must be possessed by the molecules in excess to the average energy at a given temperature to enter a chemical reaction.
Relation between activation energy and enthalpy of a reversible reaction:
If the reaction is endothermic in forward direction, then Ea(backward) ​= Ea(forward) ​+ {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
If the reaction is exothermic in forward direction then Ea(backward)​ = Ea(forward) ​+ {tex}\Delta{/tex}H
For an endothermic reaction, where {tex}\Delta{/tex}H represents the enthalpy of the reaction in kJmol−1, the minimum value for the energy of activation will be slightly more than {tex}\Delta{/tex}H.

513311.explanation:
The higher values of specific heat of the water in comparison to other liquids are due to H-bonding. Water is a polar molecule because oxygen bears a partial negative charge and hydrogen bears a partial positive charge. This results in extensive hydrogen bonding in water molecules between slightly negative oxygen and slightly positive hydrogen. This is the case for water because any heat energy given to water will go towards breaking those hydrogen bonds. Because the heat is spent breaking hydrogen bonds, it doesn't increase the kinetic energy of the molecules themselves very easily.
Thus, the temperature of the water doesn't increase as much as most other fluids - i.e. it has high specific heat. H-bonding in H2O increases forces of attraction among molecules and develops abnormal properties.

513865.explanation:
Electrolytes are chemicals that break into ions (ionize) when they are dissolved in water. The positively-charged ions are called cations, while the negatively charged ions are called anions. Substances can be categorized as strong electrolytes, weak electrolytes.
A strong electrolyte is a solution/solute that completely, or almost completely, ionizes or dissociates in a solution. These ions are good conductors of electric current in the solution.
Weak electrolytes only partially break into ions in water. Weak electrolytes include weak acids, weak bases, and a variety of other compounds.
KCl is a strong electrolyte and hence mains a linear relation. Whereas, NH4​OH is a weak electrolyte and hence has a curve nature.

513908.explanation:
Impurities affect surface tension appreciably. It is observed that impurities which tend to concentrate on the surface of liquids, compared to its bulk lower the surface tension.
Substances like detergents and soaps (CH3​(CH2​)11​{tex}OSO_3^-Na^+{/tex}) decrease the surface tension sharply.
Those like alcohol (eg.CH3​OH, C2​H5​OH) lower the surface tension slightly. This can also be related to the fact that CH3​OH has a smaller dielectric constant. The dielectric constant is directly proportional to surface tension. So, on adding CH3​OH in water, overall dielectric constant decreases and surface tension decreases.
Inorganic impurities present in the bulk of a liquid such as KCl tend to increase the surface tension of water.

514387.explanation:
At high altitudes, the boiling of water takes place below 100oC because the atmospheric pressure is low. The boiling occurs at a lower temperature than the boiling point if the atmospheric pressure is lower than 76 cm Hg.
At high altitude, the external pressure on the water is decreased, therefore, it will take less energy to break the water molecules free from their bonded energy. If it takes less energy, then it will take less heat, as this is the energy we are introducing. If less heat is required, then less temperature is required, then the water will boil at a lower temperature.

514393.explanation: On mixing 10 mL of carbon tetrachloride with 10 mL of benzene the total volume of the solution is 20 mL. As there is no changes in interparticle forces as both carbon tetrachloride and benzene are non-polar.

514412.explanation: The addition of glycol lowers the freezing point of water in the radiator so that the cold winter temperatures wouldn't burst the lines and thus, the glycol-water mixture is used as antifreeze in radiators of cars.

514419.explanation:
The ice melts at a temperature lower than its usual melting point when the pressure increases. This is because ice is less denser than water. When water freezes, the molecules arrange themselves in a hexagonal lattice which maximizes this stability. This hexagonal structure is the reason that snowflakes have six sides. The lattice is also less dense than the random arrangement in liquid water. Increasing the pressure on ice causes it to melt.
Vice ​{tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Vwater​ and thus, increase in pressure favours forward reaction.

514426.explanation:

Due to higher Kf​ of camphor (40K molality−1) about 20 times more than Kf​ (1.86) of water, the depression is 20 times more in the case of camphor used as a solvent than water.

The increased pressure inside the cooker increases the boiling point of water above 100oC so more cooking is done before the water actually starts to boil. So ultimately food is cooked faster.

514539.explanation: High vapour pressure leads to the weak intermolecular forces of attraction in a liquid. Weaker are the intermolecular forces of attractions, more is the tendency for evaporation, more is vapor pressure, lower is the boiling point.

514547.explanation:
Osmosis is the diffusion of a fluid through a semipermeable membrane. When a semipermeable membrane (animal bladders, skins of fruits and vegetables) separates a solution from a solvent, then only solvent molecules are able to pass through the membrane. The osmotic pressure of a solution is the pressure difference needed to stop the flow of solvent across a semipermeable membrane. The osmotic pressure of a solution is proportional to the molar concentration of the solute particles in solution.
{tex}\pi=\frac{{n R T}}{{V}}{/tex} = MRT
Here {tex}\pi{/tex} is osmotic pressure, R is gas constant, T is temperature, M is the molarity of solute, n is a number of moles of solute present.
When mole fraction of solvent decreases, moles of solvent will decrease, and no. of moles of solute will increase.
Osmotic pressure is directly proportional to the number of moles of solute.
Osmotic pressure will increase with the decrease of the mole fraction of solvent.
The boiling point will increase with the decrease of the mole fraction of solvent.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tb ​= i.m.Kb
Vapour pressure will decrease with the decrease of the mole fraction of solvent.
pA ​= xA ​{tex}\times{/tex} pA​0

514559.explanation:
Maxima or minima obtained in the temperature, the composition curve of a mixture of two liquids indicate an azeotropic mixture.
Azeotropes are defined as the mixtures of liquids which boil at a constant temperature, like a pure liquid and possess the same composition of components in liquid as well as in vapour phase. Azeotropes are also called constant boiling mixtures because the whole of the azeotropes change into vapour state at the constant temperature and their components cannot be separated by fractional distillation.
Azeotropes are of two types:

  1. Minimum boiling azeotrope
  2. Maximum boiling azeotrope

514580.explanation:
According to kinetic theory, average kinetic energy {tex}(\epsilon)=\frac{3}{2} k_{B} T{/tex}.
Where, kB​ is Boltzmann's constant. Thus kinetic energy is directly proportional to temperature.
When the liquid is in equilibrium with its vapour pressure at its boiling point, the molecules in two phases have same kinetic energy as they are at same temperature.

514590.explanation: Alcohol involves H-bonding, thus have the highest boiling point; also a molar mass of CH4​ > molar mass of H2​. Greater is the molar mass of covalent compound higher is its boiling point. SO the boiling point of methane is higher than hydrogen.

514602.explanation: Osmotic pressure is the best method for the determination of molar mass of proteins and polymers. Because a small amount of solute will produce a much larger change in this quantity than in the boiling point, freezing point, or vapor pressure. even a 106 molar solution would have a measurable osmotic pressure. Molecular weight determinations are very frequently made on proteins or other high molecular weight polymers. These substances, owing to their large molecular size, tend to be only sparingly soluble in most solvents, so measurement of osmotic pressure is often the only practical way of determining their molecular weights.

514611.explanation: Vapour pressure of pure benzene decreases by the addition of naphthalene. Naphthalene is non -volatile in nature so it decreases the vapor pressure of benzene. Addition of a non-volatile solute always lowers the vapor pressure.

514652.explanation:
The plots of {tex}\frac{1}{X_{A}} \text { vs. } \frac{1}{Y_{A}}{/tex} (where, XA and YA are the mole fraction of liquid A in liquid and vapour phase respectively) is linear with slope and intercepts equal to {tex}\frac {P_{A}^{0}}{P_{B}^{0}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\left(P_{B}^{0} \ - \ P_{A}^{0}\right)}{P_{B}^{0}}{/tex} respectively.
{tex}{P}_{{A}}^{\prime}={P}_{{A}}^{0} {X}_{{A}}{/tex} and {tex}{P}_{{B}}^{\prime}={P}_{{B}}^{0} {X}_{{B}}{/tex}
{tex}{P}_{{A}}^{\prime}={P}_{{M}}^{0} {X}_{{A}}{/tex} and {tex}{P}_{{B}}^{\prime}={P}_{{M}}^{0} {X}_{{B}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{P_{A}}{Y_{A}}=\frac{P_{B}}{Y_{B}}{/tex}
or
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0} \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{B}}^{0}\left(1-\mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{A}}\right)}{\left(1-\mathrm{Y}_{\mathrm{A}}\right)}{/tex}
or
{tex}\frac{{P}_{{B}}^{0}}{{X}_{{A}}}=\frac{{P}_{{A}}^{0}}{{Y}_{{A}}}+\left({P}_{{B}}^{0}-{P}_{{A}}^{0}\right){/tex}
or
{tex}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{{\text{X}}_{\text{A}}}}}{\text{ = }}\frac{{\text{1}}}{{{{\text{Y}}_{\text{A}}}}}{{\cdot}}\frac{{{\text{P}}_{\text{A}}^{\text{0}}}}{{{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}^{\text{0}}}}{\text{ + }}\frac{{\left( {{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}^{\text{0}}{\text{ - P}}_{\text{A}}^{\text{0}}} \right)}}{{{\text{P}}_{\text{B}}^{\text{0}}}}{/tex}
or
y = mx + C
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Slope = m = {tex}\frac {P_{A}^{0}}{P_{B}^{0}}{/tex} and intercept C = {tex}\frac{\left(P_{B}^{0} \ - \ P_{A}^{0}\right)}{P_{B}^{0}}{/tex}

{tex}\frac{\Delta {H}}{{T}}{/tex} = constant (Trouton's rule)
{tex}\therefore {K}_{{b}}=\frac{{R T}^{2} {M}}{\Delta {H}} \propto {M T}{/tex}

515055.explanation:
Crystallization is the process of formation of solid crystals precipitating from a solution, melt or more rarely deposited directly from a gas.
In an Unsaturated solution, the rate of dissolution of a solute in a volatile liquid solvent is greater than the rate of crystallization. In a supersaturated solution, the rate of dissolution of a solute in a volatile liquid solvent is less than the rate of crystallization. At equilibrium, the rate of dissolution of a solute in a volatile liquid solvent is equal to rate of crystallization.

515078.explanation: The low concentration of oxygen in the blood and tissues of people living at high altitudes is due to low atmospheric pressure. 515133.explanation: Raoult's law is an important law of thermodynamics. It states that the partial pressure of each component of an ideal mixture of the liquids will be equal to the vapour pressure of the pure component multiplied by its mole fraction in the mixture. Here solution-i will follow Raoult's law because the solution formed after the mixing will be ideal. And hence it will not show any deviation as the intermolecular forces after mixing will also be the same.

Favourable conditions for solution formation typically involve a negative value of Hsolution; this arises because the hydration process exceeds the lattice energy in the solute.
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hsolution = {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hhydration + {tex}\Delta{/tex}Hlattice energy
{tex}\Delta{/tex}Hhydration = −ve

516021.explanation: A ruby is a crystal of alumina, aluminum oxide (Al2O3) containing a trace of chromium (III) ions replacing some of the aluminum ions. In ruby, each Al3+ ion and Cr3+ ion is surrounded by six oxide ions in an octahedral arrangement. The origin of the color of emeralds is similar to that of the color of rubies. However, the bulk of an emerald crystal is composed of beryl, beryllium aluminum silicate (Be3Al2(SiO3)6) instead of the alumina which forms rubies. The color is produced by chromium (III) ions, which replace some of the aluminum ions in the crystal. In emeralds, the Cr3+ is surrounded by six silicate ions, rather than the six oxide ions in ruby. Therefore, the color (green) of emeralds is different from that of ruby.

516026.explanation: Vitamin K plays an important role in coagulation, better known as blood clotting. Clotting is a process that helps prevent excessive bleeding both inside and outside the body. When the blood doesn't clot, excessive or prolonged bleeding can occur. It can also lead to spontaneous or sudden bleeding in the muscles, joints, or other parts of the body. Vitamin K deficiency is much more common in infants. Hence, continuous bleeding from an injured part of the body is due to a deficiency of vitamin K.

xB = {tex}\frac{n_{\text {toluene }}}{n_{\text {solution }}}=\frac{3.5}{3}{/tex} = 0.7
Total V.P. of solution = (0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 74.7 + 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 22.3) torr
= (22.4 + 15.6) torr = 38 torr

525876.explanation: The vapor pressure of a liquid is independent of the pressure of surroundings. Any solution boils when its vapor pressure equals the surrounding atmospheric pressure. The atmospheric pressure decreases with increase in altitude. Due to this, the vapor pressure equals the atmospheric pressure in a shorter period of time when compared to at the solution being present at sea level. Therefore, the solution will vaporize quickly as the bowling point will reached faster.

525877.explanation:
Depression in freezing point, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = i {tex}\times{/tex} Kf {tex}\times{/tex} m
van’t Hoff factor, i = {tex}\frac{1-\alpha+n \alpha}{1}{/tex}, where n = no. of ions produced by complete dissociation of 1 mole of HX.
HX {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} H+ + X- {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} i = {tex}\frac{1-0.2+2 \times 0.2}{1}{/tex} = 1.2
[For 20% ionisation, {tex}\alpha=\frac{20}{100}{/tex} = 0.2]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.86 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.2 = 0.45 [{tex}\because{/tex} m = 0.2]
Hence, freezing point of solution is 0 - 0.45 = -0.45°C

(A) > (C) > (B)

525880.explanation: The conductivity of an electrolyte is the conductance of 1 cm3 of the given electrolyte. It increases with the increase in the concentration of electrolyte due to an increase in the number of ions per unit volume. Molar conductivity ({tex}\lambda_m{/tex}) is the conductance of a solution containing 1 mole of the electrolyte. It increases with the decrease of concentration due to an increase in the total volume having one mole of electrolyte. Thus, interionic attraction increases and the degree of ionization decreases. Therefore, (S1) is wrong and (S2) is correct.

2F of current deposits = 1 mol
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.1 F of current deposits = {tex}\frac{0.1}{2}{/tex} = 0.05 mol

According to the reaction: PbSO4 {tex}\to{/tex} Pb4+ + 2e-
We require 2F for the electrolysis of 1 mol of PbSO4

{tex}\because{/tex} Q = It

= {tex}\frac{12 \times 96500}{100 \times 3600}{/tex} hour = 3.2 hours

CH3-CH2-CH=CH2 + Br2 {tex}\xrightarrow{{hv}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| }\limits_{\;Br} H - CH = C{H_2}{/tex}

526010.explanation: It is a general case that symmetrical substituted octahedral complexes are less deeper in color than tetrahedral complexes. So [CoCl4]2- is deep blue in color.

526012.explanation: In dye test, phenolic — OH group of p-naphthol is converted to — O- which activates the ring towards electrophilic aromatic substitution.

526013.explanation:
[Pt(NH3)2Cl(NO2)] and [Pt(NH3)4ClBr]2+ are the Ma4bc and Ma2bc type of complexes.
Each shows two geometrical isomers i.e., cis and trans isomers.

526014.explanation:
Alkyl fluorides are more conveniently prepared by heating suitable chloro- or bromo-alkanes with organic fluorides such as AsF3, SbF3, CoF2, AgF, Hg2F2, etc. This reaction is called the Swarts reaction.
CH3Br + AgF {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} CH3F + AgBr
2CH3CH2Cl + Hg2F2 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2CH3CH2F + Hg2Cl2

526015.explanation:
{tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex}  {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{(propanal)} {/tex}
Generally, aldehydes are more reactive than ketones in nucleophilic addition reactions.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of reaction with alcohol to form acetal and ketal is
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - C{H_2} - CHO}\limits_{(propanal)} {/tex} > {tex}C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{/tex}

526016.explanation: Maganese exhibits the large number of oxidation states. The most common oxidation states of Mn are +2,+3,+ 4, +6, and +7.

526020.explanation: The correct order of increasing bond length is CH3F < CH3Cl < CH3Br < CH3I

526021.explanation:
The pentaerythritol is typically produced via a basecatalyzed reaction of acetaldehyde with excess formaldehyde. The aldol condensation of three moles of formaldehyde with one mole of acetaldehyde is followed by a crossed Cannizzaro reaction between pentaerythritol, the intermediate product, and formladehyde to give the final pentaerythritol product and sodium formate as a byproduct. These reactions are shown below
CH3CHO {tex}\xrightarrow[{\left( {three{\text{ }}Aldol{\text{ }}condensation} \right)}]{{{\text{ 3HCHO/O}}{{\text{H}}^{\text{ - }}}{\text{ }}}}{/tex} C(CH2OH)3CHO
C(CH2OH)3CHO {tex}\mathop {\xrightarrow[{O{H^ - }}]{{{\text{ HCHO }}}}}\limits_{\left( {Cannizaro's{\text{ }}reaction} \right)} {/tex}  C(CH2OH)4 + HCOONa

526025.explanation: [MA2B2] will not exhibit optical isomerism in both conditions.

526026.explanation:
For a given alkyl group, the order of reactivity is
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{Cl}>\mathrm{R}-\mathrm{F}}{\text { increasing bond energy }}{/tex}
decreasing halogen reactivity.
This order depends on the carbon-halogen bond energy; the carbon-fluorine bond energy is maximum and thus fluorides are least reactive while carbon iodine bond energy is minimum hence iodides are most reactive.

526027.explanation:
TIPS/Formulae: In the formation of nitrile, the number of carbon atoms in the parent chain increases by one.
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}Cl}\limits_{\ Propyl\, chloride} + alc.KCN\xrightarrow{{{{100}^o}C}}C{H_3}C{H_2}C{H_2}CN {/tex}

526028.explanation:
Cannizzaro’s reaction is a disproportionation reaction of aldehyde in which one molecule of aldehyde reduces to alcohol whereas the other oxidizes to salt of carboxylic acid
C6H5CHO + KOH {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C6H5CH2OH + C6H5COOK

526030.explanation:
KMnO4 reacts with H2SO4 to form Mn2O7 which is highly explosive substance.
2KMnO4 + H2SO4 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} K2SO4 + Mn2O7 + H2O

Co3+ with strong field ligand forms a complex of the low magnetic moment.
{tex}\Delta_{t}=\left(\frac{4}{9}\right) \Delta_{0}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \Delta_{t}=\frac{4}{9} \times{/tex} 18000 = 8000 cm-1

526034.explanation: Toluene has electron-donating methyl group. Hence, reacts fastest while others have either electron-withdrawing groups (i.e. - COOH or - NO2 etc.) or no substituent.

526035.explanation: Only acetaldehyde and methyl ketones give an iodoform test.

526037.explanation: The element with outer electron configuration 3d54s2 is Mn which exhibits oxidation states from + 2 to + 7.

526039.explanation:
The reaction proceeds via free radical mechanism.
As 2° free radical is more stable than 1°, so CH3CH2CH(Br)CH3 would be formed.

526040.explanation:
The square planar complex of the type [Mabcd]n{tex}\pm{/tex}, where all four ligands are different, has 3 geometrical isomers. But if one of the ligands is ambidentate, then 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6 geometrical isomers are possible. But if two ligands are ambidentate,
then 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 12 geometrical isomers are possible.
In the given example, {tex}NO_2^-{/tex} and SCN- are ambidentate ligands.

526041.explanation:
CH3CH2 - C {tex}\equiv{/tex} N {tex}\xrightarrow{{LiAl{H_4}}} {/tex} CH3CH3 - CH2 - NH2
NaBH4 does not reduce R-CN.

526044.explanation:
Seliwanoff reagent {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} [Resorcinol + Conc. HCl]
It is used to distinguish between aldoses and ketoses. Ketoses show red colour whereas aldoses show light pink colour with Seliwanoff Reagent.

526047.explanation:
Addition of HBr to 2-pentyne gives two structural isomers (I) and (II)
CH3-C{tex}\equiv{/tex}C-CH2CH3 {tex}\xrightarrow{{HBr}}{/tex} {tex}C{H_3}C(Br)\mathop { = C}\limits_{(I)} HC{H_2}C{H_3}{/tex}{tex} + C{H_3}CH = \mathop {C(Br)}\limits_{(II)} C{H_2}C{H_3}{/tex}
Each one of these will exist as a pair of geometrical isomers. Thus, there are two structural (I and II) and four configurational isomers cis-and trans-of I and II.

526048.explanation: Complexes having only one type of ligands are examples of the homoleptic complex.

526049.explanation: Primary, secondary and tertiary amines are distinguished by the Hinsberg reagent test. The Hinsberg reagent is an alternative name for benzene sulphonyl chloride (C6H5SO2Cl).

526050.explanation:
TIPS/Formulae:
Both compounds do not contain {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen hence undergo Crossed Cannizzaro reaction.
Initially OH- attacks at the carbonyl carbon of HCHO than that of PhCHO because carbonyl carbon of HCHO is

  1. more electrophilic than thal of C6H5CHO
  2. less sterically hindered

to give hydroxymethoxide which acts as a hydride donor in the next step to give sodium formate.

526052.explanation: Glucose exists in the cyclic form in which the aldehyde group is not free, therefore it does not give Schiff’s test.

526054.explanation: It does not show optical and geometrical isomerism (ii) and (iii) shows only geometrical isomerism.

526055.explanation:
The reaction between alkyl halides, aryl halides, and sodium in presence of dry ether to give substituted aromatic compounds is known as Wurtz fitting reaction.
C6H5Cl + 2Na + ClCH3 {tex} \to \mathop {{C_6}{H_5}C{H_3}}\limits_{Toluene} + 2NaCl{/tex}

526057.explanation:
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}{\text{ }}}\limits_{keto\,form} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^{\mathop {:O}\limits^{..} H} - C{H_2}{\text{ }}}\limits_{enolic\,form} {/tex}
No. of {tex}\sigma {/tex} bonds in enolic form : 3 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 = 9
No. of {tex}\pi{/tex} bonds in enolic form : 1
No. of lone pairs of electrons in enolic form = 2

526060.explanation:
Gluconic acid is obtained by partial oxidation of glucose by a mild oxidizing agent e.g. Tollen’s reagent, Fehling solution, Br2 water.
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{{({\text{CHOH}})}_4}}\limits_|^| }\limits_{\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{Glucose}}} }^{{\text{CHO}}} \xrightarrow{{{\text{B}}{{\text{r}}_2}{\text{ water}}}}\mathop {\mathop {{{({\text{CHOH}})}_4}}\limits_|^| }\limits_{\mathop {{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{OH}}}\limits_{{\text{Gluconic acid}}} }^{{\text{COOH}}}{/tex}
Gluconic acid can not form hemiacetal or acetal.

526062.explanation: The two possible isomers for the given octahedral complex are [M(NH3)5SO4]Cl and [M(NH3)5Cl] SO4. They respectively give chloride ion (indicated by precipitation with BaCl2) and SO4 ion (indicated by precipitation with AgNO3). Hence the type of isomerism exhibited by the complex is ionisation isomerism.

526063.explanation:
Grignard reagents react with compounds containing active hydrogen to form hydrocarbons corresponding to alkyl (or aryl) part of the Grignard reagent.
C6H5MgBr + Me3COH {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} C6H6 + Me3COMgBr

526064.explanation:
{tex}{\text{R}} - \underbrace {{\text{N}}{{\text{H}}_2}}_{{\text{Mol}}{\text{.Mass }} = 16} + C{H_3} - C - Cl\xrightarrow[{ - HCl}]{}R - \underbrace {NH - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}_{{\text{Mol}}{\text{.Mass }} = 58} {/tex} 
Now since the molecular mass increases by 42 unit as a result of the reaction of one mole of CH3COCl with one-NH2 group and the given increase in mass is (390-180) 210. Hence the number of - NH2 groups = {tex}\frac{210}{42}{/tex} = 5

TIPS/Formulae: Iodoform test is given by compounds having -COCH3, CHOHCH3 and CH3CH2OH.
Thus diethyl ketone does not give this test.

526070.explanation:
The compound shows linkage isomerism because the ligand in the compound is an ambidenate ligand that can bond at more than one atomic site.
i.e., {tex}^\bullet_\bullet NCS^-{/tex} and {tex}^\bullet_\bullet SCN^-{/tex}

526071.explanation:
The correct order of relative basicity of amines in the gas phase is 3o > 2o > 1o > NH3
The alkyl group releases an electron and thus, tends to disperse the positive charge of the alkylammonium ion and therefore stabilises it. Since, {tex}NH_4^+{/tex}(from NH3) has no such alkyl group, it is not stabilized to such an extent as alkylammonium ion.

526072.explanation: The given reaction is an example of electrophilic substitution. Further, CH3 group in toluene is o, p-directing.

526073.explanation: The compound containing {tex}\alpha{/tex}-H atom (CH3CHO) does not undergo Cannizzaro's reaction. The other three reactions {tex}\alpha{/tex}-hydrogen.

526075.explanation:
Chrome alum is K2SO4 {tex}\cdot{/tex} Cr2(SO4)3 {tex}\cdot{/tex} 24H2O
Sodium peroxide (Na2O2) will act as an oxidizing agent. It will oxidise Cr3+ to Cr6+ and Fe2+ to Fe3+
{tex}C{r^{3 + }}\, \to \mathop {\,CrO_4^{2 + }}\limits_{yellow} {/tex}
{tex}F{e^{2 + }}\, \to \,\mathop {Fe{{(OH)}_3} \downarrow }\limits_{brown\,residue}{/tex}
Hence, the filtrate will be yellow in colour and the residue will be brown in colour.

526076.explanation:
{tex}{\text{ }}\mathop {{\text{Glucose}}}\limits_{\mathop {{\text{stored in the}}}\limits_{{\text{form of Glycogen}}} } {\text{ }}\xrightarrow[{{\text{(does not need oxygen; need only enzymes)}}}]{}{\text{ }}\mathop {\mathop {{\text{Pyruvic acid}}}\limits_ \downarrow }\limits_{{\text{Lactic acid}}} \xrightarrow{{{{\text{O}}_2}}}{\text{C}}{{\text{O}}_2} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}{/tex}
During vigorous exercise sufficient oxygen is not available to meet the energy demand, so energy is derived through the conversion of pyruvic acid to lactic acid.

Central atom

526173.explanation:
The required reaction is
Ag+ + 2NH3 {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} [Ag(NH3)2]+; K = ?
From the given equations, we have
k1 = {tex}\frac{\left[\text{Ag}\left(\text{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}}{\left[\text{Ag}^{+}\right]\left[\text{NH}_{3}\right]} ; \text k_{2}=\frac{\left[\text{Ag}\left(\text{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}}{\left[\text{Ag}\left(\text{NH}_{3}\right)\right]^{+}\left[\text{NH}_{3}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The value of K is given by
K = k1 {tex}\times{/tex} k2 = 6.8 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = 1.08 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5

526177.explanation:
Manganate: {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}

Paramagnetic, green in colour, Tetrahedral & contains p{tex}\pi{/tex}-d{tex}\pi{/tex} bond
Permanganate: {tex}\mathrm{MnO}_{4}^{-}{/tex}

Diamagnetic, purple in colour, Tetrahedral & contains p{tex}\pi{/tex}-d{tex}\pi{/tex} bond
Manganate ion is paramagnetic while permanganate ion is diamagnetic.

526179.explanation:
NOTE: Colour is due to d-d transitions. Coloured compounds contain partly filled d-orbital.
The oxidation state of copper in various compounds is + 1 and + 2. In CuF2 it is in +2 oxidation state. In +2 state its configuration is Cu2+ = 1s22s22p63s23p63d9
i.e., 
It has one unpaired electron due to this it is coloured.
(NOTE: CuF2 possesses blue colour in crystalline form)

526181.explanation:
The Colour of transition metal ion salt is due to the d-d transition of unpaired electrons of the d-orbital. Metal ion salt having the similar number of unpaired electrons in d-orbitals shows similar colour in the aqueous medium.

526216.explanation:
In the given substituted benzene rings, the substituents methoxy (-OCH3) and amino (-NH2) are strongly activating groups, while methyl (-CH3) is weakly activating and chloro (-Cl) is a deactivating group towards electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.
Since among methyl and methoxy group, methoxy group is more reactive than methyl group, (C) is more reactive than (D). Although the amino group is a strongly activating group, it gets protonated in presence of acid to form anilinium ion {tex}( - \mathop N\limits^ +{H_3}){/tex} which is strongly deactivating. Hence, (A) is less reactive than (C) and (D).
Chloro group is also deactivating group but less deactivating than {tex}( - \mathop N\limits^ + {H_3}){/tex}. Thus order is (A) < (B) < (D) < (C).
Note: The activating groups increase the electron density on the benzene ring and thus increases the rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction. The deactivating groups decrease the electron density on the benzene ring and thus decrease the rate of electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction.

528370.explanation:
The elementary reaction, A2 {tex} \underset{k_{-1}}{\stackrel{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}}{/tex} 2A
follows opposing or reversible kinetics,

  1. Rate of the reaction,
    r = rforward - rbackward 
    = {tex}k_{1}\left[A_{2}\right]-k_{-1}[A]^{2}{/tex} ...(i)
  2. Again, rate of the reaction can be expressed as,
    r = {tex}-\frac{d\left[A_{2}\right]}{d t}=+\frac{1}{2} \frac{d[A]}{d t}{/tex} 
    So, the rate of appearance of A, i.e.
    {tex}\frac{d[A]}{d t}{/tex} = 2r = {tex}2 k_{1}\left[A_{2}\right]-2 k_{-1}[A]^{2}{/tex} [from eq.(i)]

528405.explanation:
Ct = C0e-kt ; k = {tex}\frac{\ln 2}{\frac {t_1} 2}{/tex}
(Ct)A = (C0)A {tex}e^{-k_At}{/tex} ; kA = {tex}\frac{\ln 2}{300}{/tex}
(Ct)B = (C0)B {tex}e^{-k_Bt}{/tex} ; kB = {tex}\frac{\ln 2}{180}{/tex}
{tex}\frac{\left(C_{t}\right)_{B}}{\left(C_{t}\right)_{A}}=\frac{\left(C_{0}\right)_{B}}{\left(C_{0}\right)_{A}} \times e^{\left(k_{B}-k_{A}\right) t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4 = {tex}e^{\left(k_{B}-k_{A}\right) t}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 In 2 = {tex}\left[\frac{\ln 2}{180}-\frac{\ln 2}{300}\right] t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2 In 2 = In 2 {tex}\left[\frac{1}{180}-\frac{1}{300}\right] t{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2=\left(\frac{120}{180 \times 300}\right) t \Rightarrow t=\frac{2 \times 180 \times 300}{120}{/tex} = 900 sec

528411.explanation:
Rate (R) = k[A]a [B]b
Exp I {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.1]a[0.1]b
Exp II {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 24.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.1]a [0.2]b
Exp III {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.2]a [0.1]b
ExpIV {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 72 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[X]a[0.2]b
Exp V {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 288 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 = k[0.3]a[Y]b
From Exp I & II,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{24 \times 10^{-3}}=\frac{{k}[0.1]^{{a}}[0.1]^{{b}}}{{k}[0.1]^{{a}}[0.2]^{{b}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}=\left(\frac{0.1}{0.2}\right)^{b} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{b}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} b = 2
Similarly, from exp I & III we get
{tex}\frac{1}{2}=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{a} \Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1
From Exp. II&IV,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{24 \times 10^{-3}}{72 \times 10^{-3}}=\frac{{k}[0.1]^{{a}}[0.2]^{{b}}}{{k}[{X}]^{{a}}[0.2]^{{b}}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}=\left(\frac{0.1}{X}\right)^{a} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}=\left(\frac{0.1}{X}\right)^{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X = 0.3
From Exp. I &V,
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{288 \times 10^{-3}}=\left(\frac{0.1}{0.3}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{0.1}{{Y}}\right)^{{b}}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{48}=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{1}\left(\frac{0.1}{Y}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{3}{48}=\left(\frac{0.1}{Y}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{16}=\left(\frac{0.1}{Y}\right)^{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)^{2}=\left(\frac{0.1}{Y}\right)^{2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}=\frac{0.1}{Y}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Y = 0.4

= {tex}\frac{0.909 \times 20 \times 3600 \mathrm{C}}{96500\left(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{e}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 112g mol-1

543798.explanation:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ag+ is undergoing reduction and hence, acting as an oxidizing agent.

543799.explanation:

{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{o}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex}
= 0.75 - (-1.66) = +0.91 V
Since, {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} is positive, the reaction is spontaneous. Hence, the coating of Ag2S will be removed and Ag will be deposited at the cathode.

543809.explanation:
The required disproportionation reaction (2Cu+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu2+ + Cu) can be obtained by using the following reactions.
Cu2+ + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu+; {tex}E_{C{u^{2 + }}|C{u^ + }}^o{/tex} = 0.15 V ...(i)
Cu2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu; {tex}E_{C{u^{2 + }}|C{u }}^o{/tex} = 0.34 V ...(ii)
Multiplying eq. (i) by 2 we get,
2Cu2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cu+ ...(iii)
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{\circ}{/tex} = -nFEo = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 0.15
Cu2+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu ...(iv)
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{\circ}{/tex} = -nFEo = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} 0.34
Subtract the eq. (iv) from (iii)
Cu2+ + Cu {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cu+
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{3}^{\circ}{/tex} = -nFEo = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} F {tex}\times{/tex} Eo
Also {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{3}^{\circ}=\Delta \mathrm{G}_{1}^{\mathrm{o}}-\Delta \mathrm{G}_{2}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex}
-1EFo = (-2F {tex}\times{/tex} 0.15) - (-2F {tex}\times{/tex} 0.34)
Eo = 0.38 V
This is the value for the reaction Cu2++ Cu {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cu+
But the required reaction is just the reverse of it (i.e., 2Cu+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cu2+ + Cu)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} = + 0.38 V

543810.explanation: An inert electrode does not participate in the cell reaction. It only provides a surface for the oxidation or reduction reaction and for the conduction of electrons.

543811.explanation:
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = -nFEcell
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.23 = -44,390 J mol-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{/tex} = -2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.19 = -36,670 J mol-1
{tex}\Delta{/tex}G = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - T{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - 288{tex}\Delta{/tex}S and
{tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_{40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - 313{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
-44,390 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - 288{tex}\Delta{/tex}S ...(i)
-36,670 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - 313{tex}\Delta{/tex}S ...(ii)
Subtracting (ii) from (i),
-7720 = 25{tex}\Delta{/tex}S
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}S = -308.8 J K-1 mol-1
Substituting this value in Eq (i),
-44,390 = {tex}\Delta{/tex}H - (288 {tex}\times{/tex} -308.8)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\Delta{/tex}H = -1333324.4 J mol-1 = -133 kJ mol-1

543812.explanation: Measurement of the standard reduction potential of an electrode is done by coupling it with SHE and maintaining the concentration of its oxidized and reduced species at unity. Here, SHE acts as the anode and silver electrode acts as the cathod.

543813.explanation:
E1 = {tex}E_{1}^{o}-\frac{0.059}{a}{/tex}log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{M}]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}+}\right]}{/tex}
E2 = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{2}^{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{0.059}{\mathrm{~b}}{/tex}log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{N}]}{\left[\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{b}+}\right]}{/tex}
At equivalence point,
E1 = E2 = Eequiv and
{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{M}][\mathrm{N}]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}+}\right]\left[\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{b}+}\right]}{/tex} = 1
Eequiv = {tex}E_{1}^{o}-\frac{0.059}{a}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{M}]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} a Eequiv = a{tex}E^o_1{/tex} - 0.059 log{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{M}]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}+}\right]}{/tex} ...(i)
Eequiv = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{2}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{\mathrm{~b}}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{N}]}{\left[\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{b}+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} b Eequiv = b{tex}E^o_2{/tex} - 0.059 log{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{N}]}{\left[\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{b}+}\right]}{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii),
Eequiv (a + b)
= a{tex}E^o_1{/tex} + b{tex}E^o_2{/tex} - 0.059 log {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{M}][\mathrm{N}]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{\mathrm{a}^{+}}\right]\left[\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{b}+}\right]}{/tex}
Eequiv = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{aE}_{1}^{0}+\mathrm{bE}_{2}^{\circ}}{\mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b}}\left(\because \log \frac{[\mathrm{M}][\mathrm{N}]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^{a+}\right]\left[\mathrm{N}^{\mathrm{b}+}\right]}=0\right){/tex}
