543820.explanation: The strongest oxidizing agent will have a higher positive value of standard reduction potential.

543821.explanation:
Zinc acts as an anode (oxidation takes place) and silver acts as a cathode (reduction takes place). For cell reaction to be spontaneous, the standard cell potential of the reaction should be positive.
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^{0}{/tex}
= +0.80 V - (-0.76 V) = +1.56 V
Thus,
anode reaction is Zn {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + 2e-
cathode reaction is 2Ag+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Ag
Net reaction is:
{tex}\mathop {Zn(s)}\limits^0 {/tex} + 2Ag+(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+(aq) + 2{tex}\mathop A\limits^0 g {/tex}(s)

543822.explanation: Metals with negative standard reduction potential (E°) values can displace hydrogen from dilute acid solutions.

543823.explanation:
4HBrO + 4H+ + 4e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Br2 + 4H2O;
{tex}E_{\frac{{HBrO}}{{B{r_2}}}}^o{/tex} = 1.595 (cathode)
HBrO + 2H2O {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}BrO_3^ - {/tex} + 5H+ + 4e-;
{tex}E_{\frac{{BrO_3^ - }}{{HBrO}}}^o{/tex} = 1.5 V (anode)
5HBrO {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Br2 + {tex}BrO_3^ - {/tex} + H+ + 2H2O
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\mathrm{o}}=\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{HBrO} / \mathrm{Br}_{2}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{BrO}_{3} / \mathrm{HBrO}}^{\circ}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = 1.595 - 1.5 = 0.095 volts
Here, {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} > 0. Hence, the reaction is spontaneous.
Alternatively, according to the Latimer diagram, a species disproportionates into its neighbours if the potential on the right is more positive than that on the left. Hence, HBrO is the correct answer.

Using Equilibrium constant, K = {tex}{10^{\left( {\frac{{n \times {E^o}}}{{0.059}}} \right)}}{/tex} at 298 K
When n = 1, K = {tex}{10^{\left( {\frac{{{E^o}}}{{0.059}}} \right)}}{/tex} OR K = {tex}{10^{(16.9 \times {E^o})}}{/tex}
When n = 2, K = {tex}{10^{\left( {\frac{{{2E^o}}}{{0.059}}} \right)}}{/tex} OR K = {tex}{10^{(33.8 \times {E^o})}}{/tex}
When n = 3, K = {tex}{10^{\left( {\frac{{{3E^o}}}{{0.059}}} \right)}}{/tex} OR K = {tex}{10^{(50.8 \times {E^o})}}{/tex}
K = {tex}10^{\left(\frac{E^{6}}{0.059}\right)}{/tex} (At 298 K)
K = {tex}10^{\left(\frac{E^{6}}{0.059}\right)}{/tex} = 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 1010

543828.explanation:
The cell reaction is Zn + Cu2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + Cu

When [Zn2+] is increased and [Cu2+] is decreased, the term log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex} increases.
Thus, E1 > E2

543829.explanation:
The cell reaction is Zn + Cu2+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Zn2+ + Cu
Ecell = {tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{cell}}^{\mathrm{o}}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\left[\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ecell - {tex}E_{cell}^o{/tex} = {tex} - \frac{{0.059}}{2}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{{{c_1}}}{{{c_2}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.059 = {tex}-\frac{0.059}{2} \log \frac{\mathrm{c}_{1}}{\mathrm{c}_{2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} log{tex}\frac {c_1}{c_2}{/tex} = -2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Taking antilog on both sides, {tex}\frac {c_1}{c_2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{100}{/tex}

543831.explanation:
For H2 electrode, 2H+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2(g)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}{E_{\frac{{{H^ + }}}{{{H_3}}}}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{E}^{\circ}-\frac{0.059}{2} \times \log \frac{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
Now, E° = 0 for H2 electrode.
To make the potential of H2-electrode,
{tex}{E_{\frac{{{H^ + }}}{{{H_3}}}}}{/tex} = 0,
0 = {tex}0-\frac{0.059}{2} \times \log \frac{P_{H_{2}}}{\left(10^{-7}\right)^{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\frac {0.059}{2}{/tex}log {tex}\frac{{{P_{{H_2}}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{/tex} = 0
log{tex}\frac{{{P_{{H_2}}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{P_{{H_2}}}}}{{{{10}^{ - 14}}}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}{P_{{H_2}}}{/tex} = 10-14 atm

543832.explanation:
For the given hydrogen electrode,
Pressure of H2 = 1 bar and pH = 1.2

{tex}{E_{\frac{{{H^ + }}}{{{H_2}}}}} = \frac{{0.059}}{2}{/tex}log10[H+]2
= 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} log10[H+]
= 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} [-pH]
EH = 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} (-pH) = 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} (-1.2) = - 0.0708 V

543833.explanation:

[H+] is 1 M and pressure of H2 {tex}\neq{/tex} 1 bar (or 1 atm)
{tex}{E_{\frac{{{H^ + }}}{{{H_2}}}}} = - \frac{{0.059}}{2} \times {\log _{10}}{P_{{H_2}}}{/tex}
EH = {tex}-\frac{0.059}{2} \times \log _{10} \mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex}
= {tex} - \frac{{0.059}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} log10 10
= {tex} - \frac{{0.059}}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = -0.0295 V

543834.explanation:
Anodic reaction : H2(P1) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2H+ + 2e-
Cathodic reaction : 2H+ + 2e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} H2(P2)
Eanode = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{/tex}
Ecathode = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
Ecell = Eanode + Ecathode
= {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \text { ln } \frac{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{\mathrm{P}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{RT}}{2 \mathrm{~F}} \ln \frac{\mathrm{P}_{1}}{\mathrm{P}_{2}}{/tex}

    Resistance (R) = 50{tex}\Omega{/tex},

    = 1.4 S m-1 {tex}\times{/tex} 50{tex}\Omega{/tex} = 70 m-1
  2. For given 0.5 M solution, resistance (R) = 280{tex}\Omega{/tex}

    The molar conductivity ({tex}{ \wedge _m}{/tex})

At anode: 2Cl- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Cl2 + 2e-
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2F (i.e., 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C) of electricity is required to produce 1 mol of Cl2 gas.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.2 F (i.e., 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 C) of electricity will be required to produce 0.1 mol of CI2 gas.
Now, Q = I {tex}\times{/tex} t
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 96500 = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} t
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = 6433.3 seconds = 107.2 minutes {tex}\approx{/tex} 110 minutes

543858.explanation: i - b, ii - a, iii - d, iv - c

543862.explanation: zinc has higher negative electrode potential than iron

543864.explanation:
Charge on one oxide ion (O2-)
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C
({tex}\because{/tex} One unit negative charge = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 C)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Charge on 1 mol of oxide ion
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 {tex}\times{/tex} 6.022 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023
= 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 C

543865.explanation:
Half-reactions can be represented as:
4Al3+ + 12e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4Al
3C {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 3C4+ + 12e-
Number of electrons involved in change = 12

543866.explanation: The oxidation number of S in Na2S2O3 is +2 and in K2SO4, it is +6. The oxidation number of Mn in KMnO4 is +7 and in MnO2, it is +4. The oxidation number of H remains the same.

543868.explanation:
The standard reduction potential of Mg2+ |Mg and Cu2+| Cu electrodes are -2.36 V and +0.34 V respectively.
Here, Cu is cathode and Mg is anode.
{tex}\therefore E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=E_{\mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Cu}}^{\circ}-{E}_{\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \mid \mathrm{Mg}}^{\circ}{/tex}
= 0.34 - (-2.36)
= 2.70 V

543870.explanation: Zinc electrode (E° = -0.76 V) acts as anode whereas calomel electrode (E° = 0.280 V) acts as cathode.

543871.explanation:
Under standard conditions (1 M KCl, 25 °C) the standard electrode reduction potential of the calomel electrode is 0.280 V and that of copper is +0.34 V.
Therefore, copper acts as a cathode, and the calomel electrode acts as an anode.
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\mathrm{E}_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}-\mathrm{E}_{\text {anode }}^{\circ}{/tex}
= 0.34 - 0.280 = +0.06 V

543872.explanation: In calomel electrode, either oxidation or reduction occurs so it can be coupled with any electrodes.

543873.explanation: From the values of standard electrode potential, the order of reducing power is Al > Cu > Ag > Hg

543874.explanation:
Mg, Al and Ca have lower reduction potentials than water which has E° = -0.83 V, therefore Mg2+, Al3+ and Ca2+ cannot be reduced and during the electrolysis of their aqueous salt solution, water will get reduced.
However, copper having a positive electrode potential value (more than hydrogen) can be obtained by electrolysis of an aqueous solution of its salt.

543876.explanation:
The standard e.m.f. for the reaction
X(s) + Y2(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} X2+ + 2Y- ....(i)
will be same (i.e., n volt) as that of
{tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}X(s) + {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}Y2(l) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}X2+ + Y- ....(ii)
This is because E° is an intensive property. But, {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° is an extensive property that depends on the amount of substance. Thus, the magnitude of {tex}\Delta{/tex}G° for reaction (i) will be twice that of reaction (ii) [i.e., 2 {tex}\times{/tex} (-m) kJ] as the amounts have been doubled.

543877.explanation:
Cr2{tex}\mathrm{O}_{7}^{2-}{/tex} + 14H+ + 6e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Cr3+ + 7H2O
According to Nemst equation,
1.067 = 1.33 - {tex}\frac {0.059}{6}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}\right]^{2}}{\left[\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{14}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -0.263 = 0.00983 log{tex}\left(\frac{\left(15 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{2}}{4.5 \times 10^{-3} \times\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{14}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 26.7 = log {tex}\frac{0.05}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{14}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 26.7 = log 0.05 - 14 log [H+]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -log[H+] = {tex}\frac {28.001}{14}{/tex} = 2

543878.explanation:
The half cell reaction is:
Mn{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 8H+ + 5e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Mn2+ + 4H2O
Suppose that only the H+ concentration deviates from 1 M to 10-5 M.
{tex}\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{MnO}_{4}, \mathrm{Mn}^{2+}}{/tex} = {tex}E_{MnO_4^ - .M{n^{2 + }}}^o - \frac{{0.059}}{5}{/tex}log {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{Mn}^{2+}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{MnO}_{4}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{8}}{/tex}
E - Eo = -0.0118 log {tex}\frac{1}{1 \times\left(10^{-5}\right)^{8}}{/tex} = -0.472 volt
The couple {tex}\frac{{MnO_4^ - }}{{M{n^{2 + }}}}{/tex} has thus moved down the electrochemical series by 0.472 volt from its standard value, i.e., to a position of less oxidizing power.

543879.explanation:
For hydrogen electrode,
Pressure of H2 is 1 bar (or 1 atm) and [H+] {tex}\neq{/tex} 1 M
{tex}\mathrm {E}_{\mathrm{H}^{+} / \mathrm{H}_{2}}=\frac{0.059}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} log10[H+]2
EH = 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} (-pH)
Now, Ecell - EHydrogen (RHS) - EHydrogen (LHS)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} ECell = -0.059 pH(RHS) - [-0.059 pH(LHS)]
= -0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} 2.21 + 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} 3.32
= 0.059(3.32 - 2.21)
= 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.11
= 0.0655 volt

543880.explanation:
pH is 7 for a neutral solution.
As pH = -log10 [H+]
[H+] = 10-7M
Eo = {tex}\frac {0.059}{n}{/tex}log{tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{NO}_{3}^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {0.059}{1}{/tex}log{tex}\frac {1}{(10^{-7})^2}{/tex}
= 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} (log 1 - log 10-14)
= 0.059 {tex}\times{/tex} (14) = 0.826 V

543881.explanation:
Molar conductivity increases with dilution.
Molar conductivity {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{Molarity}{/tex}
Hence, lesser the molarity, higher the molar conductivity.

543883.explanation:
Cell constant
= specific conductance {tex}\times{/tex} resistance
= 0.00141 {tex}\times{/tex} 4.2156 = 0.005944 cm-1
The cell constant value remains same as the same cell is used.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Specific conductance of HCl solution
= {tex}\frac {cell \ constant}{resistance}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {0.005944}{1.0326}{/tex}
= 0.00575 {tex}\Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm-1

543884.explanation:
Specific conductance
= {tex} \frac {equivalent \ conductance}{1000}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} Concentration
= {tex}\frac{95.2 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{2} \mathrm{eq}^{-1}}{1000 \mathrm{~cm}^{3} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1 eq L-1
= 0.0952 {tex}\Omega^{-1}{/tex} cm-1
Conductance = {tex}\frac {specific \ conductance}{cell \ constant}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.0952 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}}{0.33 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}}{/tex} = 0.288 {tex}\Omega^{-1}{/tex}
Resistance = {tex}\frac {1}{conductance}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {1}{0.288 \Omega^{-1}}{/tex}
= 3.47 {tex}\Omega{/tex}
Now,
V = IR
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I = {tex}\frac {V}{R}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {6V}{3.47 \Omega}{/tex} = 1.73 A

543885.explanation: For weak electrolytes, the variation of {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} and {tex}\sqrt C{/tex} is very large. Therefore, {tex}\wedge_m^o{/tex} cannot be obtained by extrapolating the graph to {tex}\sqrt C{/tex} = 0.

543887.explanation:
{tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}\right)}^{\mathrm{o}}=2 \times \lambda_{\left(\mathrm{H}^{+}\right)}^{o}+\lambda_{\left(\mathrm{so}_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{o}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 349.6 + 160
= 859.2 S cm2 mol-1
Then factor for H2SO4 is 2.
{tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{e}}^{\circ}=\frac{859.2}{2}{/tex} = 430 S cm2 eq-1

543889.explanation:
{tex}\wedge_{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}^{\mathrm{o}}{/tex} = {tex}\lambda_{m}^{0}{/tex}(H+) + {tex}\lambda_{m}^{0}{/tex}(OH-)
= 350 + 200 = 550 S cm2 mol-1
Degree of dissociation ({tex}\alpha{/tex}) = {tex}\frac{ \wedge_{m}}{\wedge_{m}^{0}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\wedge_m{/tex} = {tex}\alpha \times \wedge_{m}^{0}{/tex} = 1.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-9 {tex}\times{/tex} 550 S cm2 mol-1
= 1.045 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 S cm2 mol-1

Potash alum = K2SO4{tex}\cdot{/tex}Al2(SO4)3{tex}\cdot{/tex}24H2O

2 mol of Al3+ and 4 mol of {tex}\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}{/tex}
{tex} \wedge _{m[{K_2}S{O_4} \cdot A{l_2}{{(S{O_4})}_3} \cdot 24{H_2}O]}^o{/tex}
= {tex}2 \lambda_{m\left(K^{+}\right)}^{\circ}+2 \lambda_{m\left(A l^{3+}\right)}^{\circ}+4 \lambda_{m\left(s o_{4}^{2-}\right)}^{0}{/tex}
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 74 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 190 + 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 162
= 1176 S cm2 mol-1

543892.explanation:
W = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{I}(\mathrm{A}) \times \mathrm{t}(\mathrm{s})}{96500\left(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} mole ratio {tex}\times{/tex} M
5.28 g = {tex}\frac{2 \times 130 \times 60 \mathrm{C}}{96500\left(\mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{e}^{-}\right)} \times \frac{1 \mathrm{~mol}}{2 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{e}}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} M g mol-1
M = 65.3 g mol-1
The molar mass corresponds to zinc.

543893.explanation:
According to Faraday’s second law of electrolysis,
mass of product deposited {tex}\propto{/tex} equivalent weight
Eeq(Al3+) = {tex}\frac {27}{3}{/tex} = 9, Eeq(Ag+) = {tex}\frac {108}{1}{/tex} = 108
Eeq(Zn2+) = {tex}\frac {65.4}{2}{/tex} = 32.7, Eeq(Au+) = {tex}\frac {197}{3}{/tex} = 65.6
Hence, the correct decreasing order of amount of different metals deposited is:
Ag > Au > Zn > Al

543894.explanation:
Electrolysis of aqueous NaCl:
Anode: {tex}Cl_{(aq)}^ - \to \frac{1}{2}C{l_{2(g)}} + {e^ - }{/tex}
Cathode: H2O(l) + e- {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac {1}{2}{/tex}H2(g) + {tex}\mathrm{OH}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}{/tex}
According to Faraday's 2nd law of electrolysis,
{tex}\frac{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{\text { Mass of } \mathrm{H}_{2}}=\frac{\text { Equivalent weight of } \mathrm{Cl}_{2}}{\text { Equivalent weight of } \mathrm{H}_{2}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{852 \mathrm{~g}}{\text { Massof } \mathrm{H}_{2}}=\frac{35.5}{1}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of H2 = 24g
According to ideal gas equation,
PV = nRT
{tex}\therefore \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}} \mathrm{RT}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{H}_{2}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{12 \times 0.0821 \times 300}{1}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} n = {tex}\frac {24}{2}{/tex} = 12 mol)
= 295.56 L

= 11.52 S cm2 mol-1

{tex} =6 \times 10^{-4} g{/tex}

This means that {tex}6 \times 10^{-4} g{/tex} of urea is present in {tex}1 L{/tex} of solution.
Molarity of urea solution

{tex} =\frac{w \times 1000}{M \times \text { volume }(m L)} {/tex}
{tex} =\frac{0.06 \times 1000}{100 \times 60}=0.01 M {/tex}

{tex}\therefore 0.01 M{/tex} glucose solution is isotonic with {tex}0.06 \%({w} / {V}){/tex} solution of urea.

545343.explanation:
Assuming dilute solution,
{tex}\frac{\Delta \mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{P}_{\mathrm{A}}^{0}}=\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}}=\frac{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{B}}} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{M}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{A}}}{/tex}

For 0.05 M solution,
{tex}\pi_1{/tex} = 0.05 {tex}\times{/tex} 0.0821 {tex}\times{/tex} 300
{tex}\pi_1{/tex} = 1.2315 atm

The movement of solvent molecules occurs from dilute to concentrated solution, i.e. from solution B to solution A. Thus to prevent osmosis pressure must be applied to the concentrated solution (A).
Magnitude of external pressure
= 1.2315 - 0.12315 = 1.10835 {tex}\approx{/tex} 1.108 atm

= {tex}\frac{0.218}{0.222}+\frac{0.218 \times 60}{1000}{/tex}
= 0.982 + 0.013
= 0.995 {tex}\approx{/tex} 0.993 g mL-1

When ice separates out at -3.124oC, the molality of solution changes. The new molality (mx) is

WA = {tex}\frac{254.8 \times 1000}{342 \times \mathrm{m}_{x}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{254.8 \times 1000}{342 \times 1.68}{/tex}
= 443.5 g
Thus, amount of ice separated,
745.2 - 443.5 = 301.7 {tex}\approx{/tex} 302 g

= {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}}}{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{A}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}+\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}}}=\frac{1}{2.58} \approx{/tex} 0.39

545527.explanation:
1. Oxalate ion:
    2Mn{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 5C2{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 16H+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Mn2+ + 8H2O + 10CO2
    2 moles of KMnO4 oxidizes 5 moles FeC2O4
  2. Nitrite ion:
    2Mn{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 5N{tex}\mathrm{O}_{2}^{-}{/tex} + 6H+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Mn2+ + 5N{tex}\mathrm{O}_{3}^{-}{/tex} + 3H2O
    2 moles of KMnO4 oxidizes 5 moles of Fe(NO2)2
  3. Ferrous ion:
    2Mn{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 10Fe2+ + 16H+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Mn2+ + 10Fe3+ + 8H2O
    1 mole of KMnO4 oxidizes 5moles of FeSO4
  4. Sulphite ion:
    2Mn{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{-}{/tex} + 5S{tex}\mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}{/tex} + 6H+ {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 2Mn2+ + 5S{tex}\mathrm{O}_{4}^{2-}{/tex} + 3H2O

2 moles of KMnO4 oxidizes 5 moles of FeSO3.
Therefore, among these, FeSO4 requires least amount of acidified KMnO4 for complete oxidation.

545529.explanation:
Let us assume that the total mass of the solution is 100 g.
26 g of methanol = {tex}\frac{26}{32}{/tex} = 0.8125 mole
36 g of ethanol = {tex}\frac{36}{46}{/tex} = 0.7826 mole
38 g of propanol = {tex}\frac{38}{60}{/tex} = 0.6333 mole
Total moles = 0.8125 + 0.7826 + 0.6333
= 2.2284
xmethanol = {tex}\frac{0.8125}{2.2284}{/tex} = 0.3646
xethanol = {tex}\frac{0.7826}{2.2284}{/tex} = 0.3512
xpropanol = {tex}\frac{0.6333}{2.2284}{/tex} = 0.2842
The increasing order of mole fraction is propanol < ethanol < methanol, iii < ii < i.

545531.explanation:
16.9 % (w/v) AgNO3 solution means 100 mL of solution contains 16.9 g of AgNO3
5.8 % (w/v) NaCl solution means 100 mL of solution contains 5.8 g of NaCl

  AgNO3 + NaCl {tex}\to{/tex} AgCl{tex}\downarrow{/tex} + NaNO3
ln 100 mL 16.9 g   5.8 g   -   -
ln 1 L 169 g   58 g   -   -
  {tex}\equiv{/tex} 1 mol   {tex}\equiv{/tex} 1 mol        
ln 50 mL 0.05 mol   0.05 mol   0.05 mol   -

When 0.05 mol of AgNO3 reacts with 0.05 mol of NaCl, 0.05 mol of AgCl is formed
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The mass of the precipitate formed
= 0.05 mol {tex}\times{/tex} 142.8 g mol-1
= 7.14 g {tex}\approx{/tex} 7 g

545532.explanation:
The molar solution has 1 mole or molar mass of solute in 1000 mL of its solution
Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{B}}}{\text { Volume in } \mathrm{L}}{/tex}
where nB = {tex}\frac{\text { Mass of solute }}{\text { Molar mass of solute }}{/tex}
The value of molarity should be 1 for molar solution.

  1. Molar mass of C2H5OH
    = (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) + (5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) + (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) + (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1)
    = 46 g
    {tex}\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}=\frac{4.6}{46}{/tex} = 0.1 mole
    Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{C}_{2} \mathrm{H}_{5} \mathrm{OH}}}{\text { Volume in } \mathrm{L}}=\frac{0.1}{1}{/tex} = 0.1 M
  2. Molar mass of CaCl2 = 40 + (2 {tex}\times{/tex} 35.5)
    = 111 g
    {tex}\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}=\frac{110.98}{111}{/tex} = 0.999 {tex}\approx{/tex} 1 mole
    Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CaCl}_{2}}}{\text { Volumein } \mathrm{L}}=\frac{1 \mathrm{~mole}}{1 \mathrm{~L}}{/tex} = 1 mole
  3. Molar mass of CH3OH
    = (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 12) + (3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) + (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 16) + (1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1) = 32 g
    {tex}\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}}=\frac{0.23}{32}{/tex} = 0.0072 mole
    Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{OH}}}{\text { Volume in } \mathrm{L}}=\frac{0.0072}{\mathrm{l}}{/tex}
    = 0.0072 M
  4. Molar mass of NaCl = 23 + 35.5
    = 58.5 g
    nNaCl = {tex}\frac{0.585}{58.5}{/tex} = 0.01 mole
    Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{Nacl}}}{\text { Volume in } \mathrm{L}}=\frac{0.01}{\mathrm{0.1}}{/tex} = 0.1 M

545533.explanation: In-ground state, elements that have one electron in 5d orbital (5d1) are La, Ce, Gd and Lu. In all other lanthanoids, 5d orbitals are empty. In-ground state, gadolinium has one electron in 5d orbital.

545535.explanation:
M1 = 0.1 M, M2 = 0.2 M
V1 = 100 mL V2 = 25 mL
Resulting molarity of the two mixtures (solutions), M = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}}{/tex}
Resulting Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{M}_{1} \mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{M}_{2} \mathrm{~V}_{2}}{\mathrm{~V}_{1}+\mathrm{V}_{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{(0.1\times100)+(0.2\times25)}{100 + 25}{/tex}
= 0.12 M

545536.explanation:
In-ground state, lanthanoids that have half-filled 4f orbital (4f7) are Eu and Gd.
In-ground state, lanthanoids that have completely filled 4f orbital (4f14) are Yb and Lu.
In-ground state, lanthanoids that have half-filled 4f orbital (4f7) are Eu and Gd.

545538.explanation:
xethanol = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\text {ethanol }}}{\mathrm{n}_{\text {ethanol }}+\mathrm{n}_{\text {water }}}=\frac{0.1}{1}=\frac{10}{100}{/tex}
If a solution is 100 moles, it contains 10 moles ethanol and 90 moles water.
Mass of ethanol = 10 {tex}\times{/tex} 46 = 460 g
Mass of water = 90 {tex}\times{/tex} 18 = 1620 g
Total mass of solution = 1620 + 460 = 2080 g
Volume of solution = {tex}\frac{2080}{0.99}{/tex} = 2101 mL
= 2.101 L
Molarity = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{n}_{\text {ethanol }}}{\text { Volume of solution }}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{10}{2.101}{/tex} = 4.76 {tex}\approx{/tex} 4.8 M

545539.explanation:
Lanthanoid hydroxides are stronger base than Al(OH)3 and weaker than Ca(OH)2. Basicity decreases from La(OH)3 to Lu(OH)3 as the ionic size decreases from La3+ to Lu3+. Thus, they follow the order.
Al(OH)3 < Lu(OH)3 < Ce(OH)3 < Ca(OH)2

545541.explanation:
Number of moles of NaCl in 1 L = 3 mole
Mass of NaCl in 1 L = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 58.5 = 175.5 g
Mass of 1 L solution
= 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} Density of solution
= 1000 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.25 = 1250 g
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of water in the solution = 1250 - 175.5
= 1074.5 g
= 1.0745 kg
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Molality = {tex}\frac{\text { Number of moles of solute }}{\text { Mass of solvent in } \mathrm{kg}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{1.0745}{/tex} = 2.79 m

545542.explanation:
Lanthanoid ions that have the configuration of [Xe] 4fo are La3+ and Ce4+
Lanthanoid ions that have the configuration of [Xe] 4f7 are Eu2+{tex}\cdot{/tex}Gd3- and Tb4+
Lanthanoid ions that have the configuration of [Xe] 4f14 are Yb2+ and Lu3+
Eu2+ has a half-filled f orbital.
Therefore, the electronic configuration of Eu2+ ion is [Xe] 4f7

545543.explanation:

Mole fraction of solute, xB = {tex}\frac{n_B}{n_A+n_B}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{55.55+1}{/tex}

Molality = {tex}\frac{\text { moles of solute }}{\text { mass of solvent }(\mathrm{kg})}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1.11}{0.030}{/tex} = 37 m
Specific gravity is the unitless quantity. It is the density with reference to standard density.

545545.explanation: The most stable and most common oxidation state of lanthanoids is +3. Hence, Sm2+, Eu2+ and Yb2+ ions in +2 state tend to change to +3 state by loss of electrons and those in +4 state tend to change to +3 state by gain of electron.

But HCl is only 70% concentrated
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Mass of solution = {tex}\frac{18.23}{70}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 100 = 26 g

545548.explanation: In general, the earlier members of lanthanoids have reactivity similar to calcium but, with increasing atomic number, their reactivity is similar to aluminium.

545554.explanation:
Due to lanthanoid contraction, there is a regular decrease in the ionic size (M3+) with an increase in atomic number.
Therefore, the correct order in ionic radii is:
Tm3+ < Pm3+ < Pr3+ < La3+

545557.explanation:
The size of Ln3+ cation decreases with an increase in the atomic number, due to lanthanoid contraction. This decreases the ionic character of the M-OH bond and increases the covalent character of the M-OH bond.
Hence, correct order is Ce(OH)3 < Pr(OH)3 < Tm(OH)3 < Lu(OH)3

545572.explanation:
In-ground state, elements that have empty 5f orbital (5f0) are Ac and Th.

545606.explanation: When a solid solute is added to the solvent, some solute dissolves and its concentration increases in solution (i.e., dissolution). Some solute particles in solution collide with the solid solute particles and get separated out of solution (i.e., crystallization). In the state of dynamic equilibrium, these two processes occur at the same rate.

Let molar mass of P be = x g

Then, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf (in P) = {tex}\frac{3 K_f}{x(97)}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1000

{tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf (in Q) = {tex}\frac{2 K_f}{79(98)}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1000
Since {tex}\Delta{/tex}Tf is equal for two solutions,

x = {tex}\frac{3}{97}\times \frac{98}{2} \times{/tex} 79

= 1.24 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 {tex}\times{/tex} 55.55
= 6.89 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol

545621.explanation: NaCl is a single compound. It is not a solution. Aqueous NaCl solution is a solution of solid in liquid. Pumice stone is a solution of gas in solid. Manganin is a solution of solid in solid (solid solution).

545632.explanation: [Cr(NH3)5SO4]Cl will ionize to give Cl- ions which then react with AgNO3 to form white precipitate of AgCl.

545634.explanation: 1 mol CrCl3{tex}\cdot{/tex}5H2O reacts with excess of AgNO3 to give 2 mol of AgCl. This shows that one molecule of complex gives two chloride ions in the solution. Hence, the formula of the complex is: [Cr(H2O)5Cl]Cl2.

{tex}\mathop {NaCl}\limits_x \to \mathop {N{a^ + }}\limits_x + \mathop {C{l^ - }}\limits_x {/tex}

Ag+ + 2Cl- {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} [AgCl2]-

AgCl(s) {tex} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} Ag+ +Cl-

Kf = {tex}\frac{\left[\mathrm{AgCl}_{2}\right]^{-}}{\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{sp} \cdot\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 105 = {tex}\frac{10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-10}\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [Cl-] = {tex}\frac{10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-10} \times 3.0 \times 10^{5}}{/tex}
= 3.3 M

= 193.1 g
Mass of NaCl is 10 L = 193.1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 = 1931 g

545649.explanation: [Pt(NH3)2Cl2] is a neutral complex, since the coordination entity does not carry any charge.

545653.explanation: Acetate, cyanide, and ammonia are monodentate ligands. They do not form chelates. Oxalate is a bidentate ligand and thus, it forms a chelate.

545654.explanation:
Charge on the ligand {tex}C_2 O^{2-}_4{/tex} is -2 and charge on the complex ion is -3.
Let the oxidation number of Co be x.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + 3 {tex}\times{/tex} (-2) = -3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = +3
Coordination number is 6 because {tex}C_2 O^{2-}_4{/tex} is a bidentate ligand.

545655.explanation:
[M(en)2(C2O4)]Cl
The complex has two molecules of en and one {tex}C_2 O^{2-}_4{/tex} ion. Thus, the complex has three bidentate ligands in all.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Coordination number
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} number of bidentate ligands
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 = 6
Let the oxidation number of metal ‘M’ in the complex be ‘x’.
The charge on complex ion, en and {tex}C_2 O^{2-}_4{/tex} are +1, 0 and -2 respectively.
Hence, x + 0 - 2 = +1
x = 3
{tex}\therefore{/tex} The sum of coordination number and oxidation number of the metal M = 6 + 3 = 9

545660.explanation: [Co(en)2Cl2]+ is optically active in cis isomer. Trans isomer contains a plane of symmetry and is therefore optically inactive. The resolution of cis-isomer in d and l-form is possible.

545661.explanation:
EAN = Z - X + Y
Z = atomic number of (Fe) metal = 26
X = number of electrons lost during oxidation of metal to metal ion = 3
Y = number of electrons donated by ligands = (6 {tex}\times{/tex} 2) = 12
{tex}\therefore{/tex} EAN = 26 - 3 + 12 = 35

545664.explanation: [Ni(CO)4] is tetrahedral due to sp3 hybridization. It contains no unpaired electrons. Hence, it is diamagnetic.

545668.explanation:
Fe2+ = [Ar] 3d6
In presence of a strong field ligand (CN-), all six electrons will be placed in low energy {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{/tex}orbitals.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Electronic configuration = {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{6} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0}{/tex}
K4[Fe(CN)6] is low spin complex that contains no unpaired electron in d-orbitals.

545670.explanation:
V2+ = [Ar] 3d3
For octahedral complexes in which central metal atom/ion has electronic configurations d1, d2, d3, d8, d9 and d10 there is only one possible arrangement of electrons which irrespective of the strength of the ligand and hence, the high spin and low spin configurations cannot be distinguished.
Since the metal ion has d3 configuration, there is only one possible arrangement of electrons.
{tex}\therefore{/tex}Electronic configuration = {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{3} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{0}{/tex}

545671.explanation:
Crystal field stabilization energy (CFSE) = - 4 Dq x (number of electrons in t2g orbitals) + 6 Dq x (number of electrons in eg orbitals)

Complex Outer electronic configuration CFSE
[Mn(H2O)6]3+ 3d4{tex}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{3} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{1}\right){/tex} -4Dq(3) + 6Dq(1) = -6 Dq
[Fe(H2O)6]3+ 3d5{tex}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{3} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{2}\right){/tex} -4Dq(3) + 6Dq(2) = 0
[Co(H2O)6]2+ 3d7{tex}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{5} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{2}\right){/tex} -4Dq(5) + 6Dq(2) = -8 Dq
[Co(H2O)6]3+ 3d6{tex}\left(\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}^{4} \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}^{2}\right){/tex} -4Dq(4) + 6Dq(2) = -4 Dq

The complex with least value of CFSE is the most stable i.e. [Co(H2O)6]2+.

545673.explanation:
Optical isomerism
Stereoisomers have the same chemical formula and binds but have different spatial arrangement. Optical isomerism is a type of stereoisomerism.

545674.explanation:
Crystal field splitting energy ({tex}\Delta{/tex}E) = {tex}\frac {hc}{\lambda}{/tex}
{tex}\Delta{/tex}E = {tex}\frac{\left(6.63 \times 10^{-34} \mathrm{Js}\right)\left(3.00 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\right)}{\left(498 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}\right)}{/tex}
= 3.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J/ion
= (3.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-19 J/ion) {tex}\times{/tex} (6.02 {tex}\times{/tex} 1023 ions/mol)
= 240198 J mol-1 {tex}\approx{/tex} 240 kJ/mol

545675.explanation:
The strength of ligands is in order: en > NH3 > H2O.
Ligand strength {tex}\propto \frac{1}{\lambda}{/tex}
Therefore, the wavelength of absorption will be:
[Co(en)3]3+ < [Co(NH3)6]3+ < [Co(H2O)6]3+

545676.explanation:
The increasing order of energies of light is:
red < green < blue < violet.
The higher the energy of the light absorbed, the higher is the ligand strength.
Hence, the increasing order of ligand strength is b < d < c < a.

545677.explanation:
In [Cr(NH3)6]3+, chromium has electronic configuration 3d3 4s0 hence d-d transitions are possible, which gives colour in visible light.
But Sc in [Sc(H2O)3(NH3)3]3+, Ti in [Ti(en)2(NH3)2]4+ has 3d0 4s0 and Zn in [Zn(NH3)6]2+ has 3d10 4s0 configuration, hence, these complexes are colourless.

545678.explanation: The electronic configuration of Co3+ has four unpaired electrons in [CoF6]3- so it is paramagnetic. [Zn(NH3)4]2+ [Co(NH3)6]3+ and [Ni(CO)4] does not have unpaired electrons. Hence, they are diamagnetic.

545679.explanation:
Complex Oxidation state of metal Number of unpaired electrons
[MnCl4]2- +2 5
CoCl4]2- +2 3
[NiCl4]2- +2 2
[ZnCl4]2- +2 0

545680.explanation:
[Fe(CO)5]
EAN = 26 - 0 + 10
EAN = 36
Since the given complex has only one central metal atom and it follows EAN rule, it is mononuclear.

545681.explanation: In metal carbonyls, the donation of a pair of electrons from the filled d-orbital of metal to vacant antibonding {tex}\pi{/tex}* orbital of CO ligand (back bonding) reduces the bond order of C-O bond from the triple bond to double bond. This results in an increase in C-O bond length from 1.128 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex} to 1.15 {tex}\mathop A\limits^o {/tex}. Among these four options, the central metal ion Fe2- in [Fe(CO)4]2- has maximum electron density and back bonding will be more pronounced in this complex. Thus, C-O bond length in [Fe(CO)4]2- is the longest.

545784.explanation: If the d-electrons are asymmetrically arranged, they will repel some ligands in the complex more than the others. This results in distortion. In the case of unevenly occupied {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{/tex} orbitals, the Jahn-Teller distortion is very weak since the {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{/tex} set does not point directly at the ligands and therefore, the energy gain is much less while. The {tex}\mathrm{d}_{x^{2}-y^{2}}{/tex} and {tex}\mathrm{d}_{z^{2}}{/tex} have lobes pointing towards ligands. Hence, the Jahn-Teller distortion is strong. In d8 type complex, electrons are symmetrically distributed. Hence, Jahn-Teller distortion is not observed.

However, due to the stronger +1 effect at o-position (than at p-position), o-cresol is a weaker acid than p-cresol.

546103.explanation:
Alcohols and phenols can be distinguished by the following tests,

  1. Reaction with ferric chloride: Phenols react with neutral ferric chloride solution to give blue, violet or green colouration whereas alcohols do not react.
  2. Bromine water test: Phenols react with bromine water to give a white precipitate of 2, 4, 6-tribromophenol while alcohols do not.
  3. Phenols dissolve in aqueous NaOH by forming water soluble sodium phenoxide and are reprecipitated/regenerated on acidification with HCl. Alcohols show no acidic character in aqueous solution, thus, alcohols do not react with aqueous NaOH.
  4. Coupling reaction: Phenols react with diazonium salts in basic solution to form yellow or orange coloured dyes but alcohols do not react.

546107.explanation: In ethers, two alkyl groups are attached to oxygen atom whereas in alcohols, one alkyl group and one H-atom are linked to oxygen. Hence, ethers are different from its corresponding isomeric monohydric alcohols with respect to the absence of replaceable active hydrogen. Ethers and their corresponding monohydric alcohols are functional isomers of each other, hence they contain a same number of carbon atoms and an oxygen atom which is sp3 hybridized.

546170.explanation: The actual electronic configuration of niobium (Z = 41) is [Kr] 4d4 5s1 and not [Kr] 4d5 5s0 even though in the latter case, it achieves half-filled stability. This can be explained based on two energy concepts: repulsion energy (caused by the opposite spin of an electron in the same orbital) and exchange energy (due to the shifting of the electron in the same degenerate orbitals accompanied by the release of energy). The observed electronic configuration is the lowest energy state while the electronic configuration with the half-filled d-subshell is not possible as it requires high energy to shift two 5s-electrons to 4d-subshell.

Aldehydes and ketones are reduced to respective hydrocarbons when treated with hydrazine followed by heating with KOH/NaOH in high boiling solvents. This is known as Wolff-Kishner reduction. The CO group of acetone is reduced to CH2, and propane is obtained.

546959.explanation: i - c, ii - e, iii - f, iv - a, v - d

546961.explanation: {tex}\mathop {\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - C \equiv C - C{H_3}}\limits_{But - 2 - yne} }\limits_{({C_4}{H_6})} }\limits_{(X)} \xrightarrow{{HBr}}\left[ {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{Br} = CH - C{H_3}} \right]\xrightarrow{{HBr}}\mathop {\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{Br}^{Br} - C{H_2} - C{H_3}}\limits_{2,2 - Dibromobutane} }\limits_{(Y)} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{ - BaB{r_2}}]{{aq.\,Ba{{(OH)}_2}}}\left[ {C{H_3}\mathop { - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{OH}^{OH} - C{H_2} - }\limits_{Unstable{\text{ }}diol{\text{ }}} C{H_3}} \right]\xrightarrow[{ - {H_2}O}]{}C{H_3}\mathop {\mathop { - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_2} - }\limits_{Butan - 2 - one} }\limits_{(Z)} C{H_3}{/tex}

546963.explanation: i - b, ii - d, iii - e, iv - a

546964.explanation: Zwitter ion is a doubly charged ion in which -COOH group loses a proton and exists as -COO- whereas -NH2 accepts a proton and exists as {tex} - \mathop N\limits^ + {H_3}{/tex}

546966.explanation:
One mole of maltose on hydrolysis yields two moles of glucose.
{tex}\mathop {\mathop {{C_{12}}{H_{22}}{O_{11}}}\limits_{Maltose} }\limits_{(1\;mole)} + {H_2}O \to 2\mathop {\mathop {{C_6}{H_{12}}{O_6}}\limits_{Glu\cos e} }\limits_{(2\;moles)} {/tex}

546967.explanation: Triaminobenzene [C6 H3(NH2)3] is an example of primary amine. 1-Methylcyclohexylamine is an example of primary amine. C3H9N can represent all three types i.e., primary, secondary and tertiary amines.

546972.explanation:
In the aqueous phase, methylamines follow the basicity order as follows:
(CH3)2NH > CH3NH2 > (CH3)3N
This irregularity is due to the steric factor and solvation effect. Alkylamines are more basic than ammonia and arylamines are less basic than ammonia.

546976.explanation:
Four isomeric amines are possible for C3H9N:
{tex}\underset {\text{Propan-1-amine}}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2}{/tex} {tex}\underset {\text{Propan-2-amine}}{CH_3-\overset {\overset {CH_3}|}CH-NH_2}{/tex} {tex}\underset {\text{N-Methylethanamine}}{CH_3-CH_2-NH-CH_3}{/tex} {tex}\underset {\text{N,N-Dimethylmethanamine}}{CH_3-\overset {\overset {CH_3}|}N-CH_3}{/tex}

546978.explanation: Substituted aniline containing electron releasing groups at para position is more basic than meta isomer followed by aniline and lastly by ortho isomer which is the least basic.

546979.explanation:
Essential amino acids are the amino acids which body cannot synthesize and must be supplied from diet.
Essential amino acids: PVT TIM HALL
PTV: Phenylalanine, Threonine, Valine,
TIM: Tryptophan, Isoleucine, Methionine,
HALL: Histidine, Arginine, Lysine, Leucine
Isoleucine, tryptophan, lysine and methionine are essential amino acids.

546984.explanation: Benzylamine is more basic than aniline. m-Nitroaniline is more basic when compared to p-nitroaniline. The tendency for hydration increases from tertiary to primary amines.

546989.explanation: {tex}\underset {\underset {(P_1)}{\text{Diazobenzene}}}{C_6H_5 - N = N - C_6H_5} \xleftarrow [{\text {dry ether}}]{LiAlH_4}{/tex} {tex}\underset {{\text{Nitrobenzene}}}{C_6H_5 - NO_2} \xrightarrow {{Fe}/{conc.HCl}}\underset {\underset {(P_2)}{\text{Aniline}}}{C_6H_5-NH_2}{/tex}

546990.explanation: Benzyl bromide (C6H5CH2Br) is best suited for the alkylation of benzylamine through SN1 mechanism. This is because, the carbocation formed from benzyl bromide i.e., C6H5{tex}CH_2^+{/tex} is resonance stabilized.

546991.explanation: Each carbon that is bonded to side-chain must be bonded to two functional groups (i.e., -CO- and -NH-) in a peptide chain.

546997.explanation:
The given tripeptide upon hydrolysis gives three amino acids. The hydrolysis breaks the peptide (CO-NH) bonds,
{tex}\mathop {{H_2}N - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - OH,}\limits_{Glycine} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {H - \mathop {\mathop N\limits_| }\limits_H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{C{H_3}} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - OH}\limits_{Ala\min e} ,{/tex} {tex}H - \mathop N\limits_{\mathop |\limits_H } - \mathop {\mathop {{\text{ }}C}\limits_| H - }\limits_{\mathop {\mathop {{\text{ }}C}\limits_| H - }\limits_{Valine} C{H_3}} COOH{/tex}

546999.explanation: The {tex}\alpha{/tex}-D-glucopyranose molecules in the main chain of amylopectin are linked together by 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 4{tex}\alpha{/tex} glycosidic linkages between C-1 of one glucopyranose unit and C-4 of another. The C-1 of the terminal glucopyranose further gets linked to C-6 of another glucopyranose unit in the next chain, through 1 {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} 6 {tex}\alpha{/tex} glycosidic linkage, resulting in a highly branched structure.

547003.explanation: In tertiary structure, the polypeptide chain is coiled by forming weak bonds such as hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, disulphide bridges and electrostatic forces.

547009.explanation: A metal ion associated with an enzyme is called an activator. A non-protein organic molecule associated with an enzyme is called a coenzyme, on its surface to bind the substrate. All proteins do not have specific active sites.

547012.explanation:
{tex}\underset {(A)}{\underset {(C_5H_{11}N)}{\underset {Pent-2-en-1-amine}{CH_3-CH_2-CH=CH-CH_2-NH_2}}}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow [{\Delta}]{alc. {KOH}/{CHCl_3}}\underset {Pent-2-ene isocyanide}{CH_3-CH_2-CH=CH-CH_2-NC} \xrightarrow {\ {H_2}/{Pt}}{/tex} {tex}\underset {(2^\circ Amine)}{\underset {Product (C_6H_{15}N)}{\underset {N-Methylpentanamine}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-NH-CH_3}}}{/tex}
Compound A can decolorize bromine in CCI4 due to the presence of a double bond, (which can be between any two carbon atoms) and it gets reduced by catalytic hydrogenation.

547014.explanation: i - c, ii - d, iii - a, iv - b

547017.explanation:
{tex}CH_3 - \underset {\underset {\underset {\text{Acetamide}}O}{||}}C - NH_2 \xrightarrow { {Br_2}/{4KOH_{(aq)}}} \underset {\text{Methanamine}}{CH_3-NH_2}{/tex} + 2KBr + K2CO3 + 2H2O
This reaction is known as Hoffmann’s hypobromite reaction.

547018.explanation: {tex}\mathop {2C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - }\limits_{Ethanoyl\,chloride} Cl + \mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}C{H_2})}_2}}\limits_{Dibenzyl\,cadmium} Cd\xrightarrow[{ether}]{{dry}}{/tex} {tex}2C{H_3}\mathop { - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_{||} }\limits_O - C{H_2} - }\limits_{Benzyl\,methyl\,ketone} {C_6}{H_5} + CdC{l_2}{/tex}

547019.explanation: Tocopherol (Vitamin E) is a fat-soluble vitamin and it can be stored in our body in the liver and adipose tissues.

547023.explanation: Reaction of phthalimide with alkyl or benzyl halides in the presence of alcoholic KOH or aqueous NaOH solution is called as Gabriel phthalimide synthesis. Aromatic primary amines like aniline are not prepared by using this method because nucleophilic substitution of aryl halides with phthalimide anion is not possible.

547024.explanation: In RNA, thymine is replaced by uracil.

547025.explanation:
Alkyl nitrile on treatment with diisobutyl aluminum hydride and subsequent hydrolysis gives the corresponding aldehyde.
Cyclohex-2-enol can oxidise to form cyclohex-2-en-1-one, only in presence of Sarrets reagent. The commercial method for the preparation of benzaldehyde involves side-chain chlorination of toluene to form benzal chloride as the intermediate.

547029.explanation: Intermolecular association due to hydrogen bonding is more in 1° amines than 2° amines.

547030.explanation: The nucleotides of DNA are joined between 5' and 3' carbons of sugar by phosphodiester bonds. The double helix structure of DNA is stabilized by hydrogen bonds which can be denatured to two individual strands by heating or changing the pH of protein.

547031.explanation:
Allylamine reacts with (and decolorizes) bromine in carbon tetrachloride instantly due to the presence of a carbon-carbon double bond in the aliphatic chain.
{tex}\underset {\text{Allylamine}}{CH_2=CH-CH_2-NH_2+Br_2} \xrightarrow {CCl_4} {/tex}{tex}\underset {\underset {Br}|}CH_2-\underset{\underset{Br}|}CH_2-CH_2-NH_2{/tex}
Propylamine does not react with (decolourizes) bromine in carbon tetrachloride.
{tex}\underset {\text{Propylamine}}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2+Br_2} \xrightarrow {CCl_4} {/tex} No reaction if the mixture is not heated or irradiated.

547036.explanation: Single nucleotide of RNA contains one cyclic pentose (furanose) which has 4 chiral carbons.

547037.explanation: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{HCN}}}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{CN}^{OH} - H}\limits_{Acetaldehyde\,cyabihydrin} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O}}}]{{{{\text{H}}^{\text{ + }}}}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{COOH}^{OH} - H}\limits_{Lactic\,acid} {/tex}

547038.explanation:
The order of basicity of aliphatic amines in the gaseous phase follows the expected order:
Tertiary amine > Secondary amine > Primary amine > Ammonia

547044.explanation: Arylamines are generally less basic than alkylamines because the nitrogen lonepair electrons are delocalised by interaction with the aromatic ring {tex}\pi{/tex} electron system.

547047.explanation:
At very low pH, the -NH2 groups will be protonated while -COOH will not ionize. The structure of lysine at very low pH is
{tex}{H_3}\mathop N\limits^ + {/tex}-CH2-CH2-CH2-CH2-{tex}\mathop {\mathop C\limits_| H}\limits_{^ + NH_3} {/tex}-COOH
The overall charge is +2.

547049.explanation:
Urotropine i.e., hexamethylenetetramine is formed by the reaction of six molecules of formaldehyde and four 8. molecules of ammonia.
{tex}\mathop {6HCHO}\limits_{Formaldehyde} {/tex} + 4NH3 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{{(C{H_2})}_6}{N_4}\,}\limits_{Urotropine} + \,6{H_2}O{/tex}

547050.explanation:
The electron-withdrawing ability of the -OH group decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom (reducing basicity) but the effect decreases as the distance between N and -OH group increases in a chain. Hence, the correct decreasing order of basicity is,
ii = CH3CH2NH2
i = HO(CH2)3NH2
iv = HO(CH2)2NH2
iii = HO(CH2)NH2

547054.explanation: {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^{C{H_3}} = CH - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}\limits_{\mathop {4 - Methylpent - 3 - en - 2 - one}\limits_{(Mesityl\,oxide)} } {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{N{H_3}}} {/tex} {tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{N{H_2}}^{C{H_3}} - C{H_2} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - C{H_3}}\limits_{\mathop {4 - Amino - 4 - methylpen tane - 2 - one}\limits_{(Diacetone\,amine)} } {/tex}

547056.explanation: A chlorine atom has both -I effect and +R effect. -I effect is dominant over +R effect, therefore, all the three o-, m- and p-chloroanilines are weaker bases than aniline.

547059.explanation:
Schiff base has general structure R2C = NR and acetaldehyde on reaction with benzylamine give (CH3)2C = NCH2C6H5.
Nitration of hexamethylenetetramine (urotropine) yields RDX or cyclonite.
Acetone reacts with ethylene glycol to form a cyclic ketal. Benzophenone, being bulky does not react with sodium bisulphite.

547060.explanation: Benzenamine is the weakest base (pKb = 9.38) followed by N-Methylbenzenamine (pKb = 9.30), ethanamine (pKb, = 3.29) while N,N-diethylethanamine (pKb = 3.25) is the strongest base of all the mentioned compounds.

547062.explanation:
Glycine form a zwitter ion.
{tex}HOOC - \mathop {C{H_2}}\limits_{Glycine} - N{H_2} \rightleftharpoons {/tex} {tex}\mathop {\mathop O\limits^ \ominus OC - C{H_2}}\limits_{Zwitter\;ion} - \mathop N\limits^ \oplus {H_3}{/tex}

547065.explanation: The coupling reaction is an electrophilic aromatic substitution reaction, and hence arenes containing electron releasing groups show greater ease of coupling than those containing electron-withdrawing groups.

547066.explanation: (CH3)3N is a tertiary amine and tertiary amines do not undergo benzoylation reaction as they lack a hydrogen atom on N.

547070.explanation:
(A) {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}CHO}\limits_{Benzaldehyde} \xrightarrow{{{K_2}C{r_2}{O_7}/dil.{H_2}S{O_4}}}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic\,acid} \xrightarrow{{Ca{{(OH)}_2}}}{/tex} {tex}\mathop {{{({C_6}{H_5}COO)}_2}}\limits_{Calcium{\text{ }}benzoate} Ca\xrightarrow[{CaC{O_3}}]{{\Delta ,\,Dry\,distillation}}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}CO{C_6}{H_5}}\limits_{Benzophenone}{/tex}
(B) {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}CHO}\limits_{Benzaldehyde} \xrightarrow{{{K_2}C{r_2}{O_7}/dil.{H_2}S{O_4}}}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COOH}\limits_{Benzoic acid} {/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{{C_5}{H_5}N - \Delta }]{{SOC{l_2}}}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}COCl}\limits_{Benzoyl{\text{ }}chloride} \xrightarrow{{{C_6}{H_6}/Anhy\,AlC{l_3}}}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}CO{C_6}{H_5}}\limits_{Benzophenone} {/tex}
(D) {tex}\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}CHO}\limits_{Benzaldehyde}\xrightarrow[{(ii)\,{H_2}O/{H^ + }}]{{(i)\,{C_6}{H_5}MgBr,\,dry\,ether}} {/tex} C6H5CH(OH)C6H5 {tex}\xrightarrow[{C{H_2}C{l_2}}]{{PCC}}\,\mathop {{C_6}{H_5}CO{C_6}{H_5}}\limits_{Benzophenone} {/tex}

547071.explanation:
Acetone on treatment with methyl magnesium iodide in the presence of dry ether followed by hydrolysis gives tertiary butyl alcohol.
{tex}\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop {{C^{\delta + }}}\limits^| }\limits^{C{H_3}} }\limits_{Acetone} = \mathop O\limits^{\delta - } + \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{\delta - } {H_3} - \mathop {Mg}\limits^{\delta + } Cl}\limits_{\mathop {Methyl}\limits_{Magnesium\,chloride} }{/tex} {tex} \xrightarrow{{dry\,ether}}\left[ {C{H_3}\mathop { - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - }\limits_{Adduct} OMgCl} \right] {/tex}{tex}\xrightarrow[{{H_2}O}]{{{H^ + }}}\,\mathop {C{H_3} - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_{C{H_3}}^{C{H_3}} - OH + }\limits_{tert - Butyl\,alcohol} Mg(OH)Cl {/tex}

547072.explanation: Quaternary ammonium hydroxides on heating forms least substituted alkene as the major product.

547074.explanation:
Essential amino acid: PTV TIM HALL
PTV: Phenylalanine, Threonine, Valine,
TIM: Tryptophan, Isoleucine, Methionine,
HALL: Histidine, Arginine, Lysine, Leucine
Essential amino acid = PTV TIM HALL
Here, A = arginine and not alanine.
So, among the given options, alanine is a non-essential amino acid.

547077.explanation: Fehling solution A is an aqueous copper sulphate solution while Fehling solution B is Rochelle salt dissolved in NaOH.

547079.explanation: m-Nitroaniline is obtained due to the protonation of aniline in an acidic medium. The protonated anilinium ion, {tex}-NH_3^+{/tex} is meta directing.

547080.explanation: In the primary structure of a protein, the amino acids are linked by peptide bonds.

547082.explanation: The increasing s-character of {tex}\beta{/tex}-carbon atom in the amine increases its electron-withdrawing capacity, thereby decreasing its basicity. Hence, the decreasing order of basicity is, (CH3CH2)2NH > H2C = CHCH2NH2 > (H2C = CH)2NH > HC {tex}\equiv{/tex} CCH2NH2.

547085.explanation:
{tex}\underset {\text{Ethanamine}}{CH_3-CH_2-NH_2}{/tex} + NaNO2 + 2HCl {tex}\xrightarrow {0-5 {^\circ C}}\underset {\text{Ethyldiazonium chloride}}{[CH_3-CH_2N_2^+Cl^-]}{/tex} + NaCl + 2H2O
[CH3 - CH2{tex}N_2^+{/tex} Cl-] + H2O {tex}\longrightarrow \underset {\text{Ethanol}}{CH_3-CH_2-OH}+HCl+N_2\uparrow{/tex}

547088.explanation: Schiff s reagent is a dilute solution of para-rosaniline hydrochloride along with sulphur dioxide.

547090.explanation:
The tripeptide molecules possible are as follows:
A-B-C
A-C-B
B-A-C
B-C-A
C-A-B
C-B-A

547091.explanation: Secondary amines on reaction with nitrous acid at low temperature forms yellow oily N-nitroso amines. Here, N-methylaniline is a secondary amine while N, N-dimethylaniline and N, N-diphenylaniline are tertiary amines while phenylmethanamine is a primary amine.

547094.explanation: Both aldehydes answer the Schiff's reagent test, react with Grignard reagent and they also undergo oxidation to the corresponding carboxylic acids with K2Cr2O7/dil. H2SO4. Only acetaldehyde (methyl ketone) containing CH3CO- group undergoes haloform reaction with NaOH and I2. HCHO does not show this reaction. Hence, the iodoform test can be used to distinguish between formaldehyde and acetaldehyde.

547095.explanation: {tex}\underset {\text{Ethanenitrile}}{CH_3-C\equiv N} \xrightarrow [{\text {ethanol}}]{ {[\text H]}/{\text {Na in}}}{/tex} {tex}\underset {\text{Ethanamine}}{CH_3-CH_2-NH_2} \xrightarrow [{\Delta}]{CHCl_3, KOH(alc.)}{/tex} {tex}\underset {\text{Ethyl isocyanide}}{CH_3-CH_2-NC}{/tex}

547097.explanation:
Number of amino acids in a tetrapeptide = 4
Number of peptide linkages in a linear polypeptide or protein = n - 1 (where, n is the number of amino acids present in the polypeptide)
E.g. No. of peptide linkages in a linear polypeptide having 15 amino acids = 15 - 1 = 14
Number of peptide bonds = 3

547100.explanation: Both propan-1-ol (i) and formaldehyde (iv) neither contain CH3CO group nor {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ - }}\mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits^{\text{|}} }\limits^{{\text{OH}}} {\text{H - }} {/tex} group.

547101.explanation: Secondary amines react with Hinsberg reagent to give an alkali-insoluble product.

547102.explanation: Maltase catalysis the conversion of maltose (C12H22O11) into glucose.

547107.explanation: Fluoroborates are diazonium salts which are highly stable compounds at room temperature. Diazonium salts are readily soluble in water under cold conditions but react with water when warmed. Diazonium salts can conduct electricity only in solution state.

547113.explanation:
In this reaction, replacement of diazonium group by -F takes place in the presence of fluoroboric acid (HBF4) and the reaction is known as Balz-Schiemann reaction.
{tex}\underset {\text{Arene diazonium salt}}{Ar-N_2^+X^-}{/tex} + NaNO2 + 2HCl {tex}\xrightarrow {273K-278K} \underset {\text{Diazonium fluoroborate}}{Ar-N_2^+BF_4^-} \xrightarrow {\Delta} \underset {\text{Aryl fluoride}}{Ar - F}{/tex} + BF3 + N2{tex}\uparrow{/tex}

547116.explanation: Vitamins are required in the diet for normal health, growth, and maintenance of the body.

547119.explanation: Arene diazonium salts are obtained from primary amines.

547122.explanation: i - b, ii - c, iii - a, iv - d

547124.explanation: Glycogenolysis is a process in which glycogen into glucose. Epinephrine or adrenaline stimulates glycogenesis raising the level of blood glucose.

547128.explanation: In the Cannizzaro reaction, the transfer of hydride to a carbonyl group is the fastest step in the mechanism.

547129.explanation: {tex}\underset {\underset {(A)}{\text{N-Ethylformamide}}}{CH_3-CH_2-NH-\overset {\overset {\text O}{||}}C-H}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow [{\text C_5\text H_5 \text N}]{\text{POCl}_3}\underset {\underset {(B)}{\text{Ethyl isocyanide}}}{CH_3-CH_2-N \equiv C}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow {523 \text K}\underset {\underset {(Ethyl cyanide)}{\text{Cyanoethane}}}{CH_3-CH_2-C \equiv N}{/tex}

547147.explanation:
The first oxidation product of secondary alcohol is ketone.
The pistachio odour of ice cream is due to the presence of acetophenone in it.
The ketonic group is always present within a carbon chain.

547149.explanation:
At the isoelectric (pH) point, all the amino acids in the solution will be in zwitter ion form. The isoelectric point of lysine is the average of pKa ({tex}\alpha{/tex}-NH3) and pKa (R group).
pH = {tex}\frac {8.95+10.53}{2}{/tex} = 9.74
Between pH 9 and 9.7, some of the amino acids in the solution will be in zwitter ion form. However, at the isoelectric point, all the amino acids will be in zwitter ion form.

547152.explanation: Osteomalacia (soft bones and joint pain in adults) is caused due to deficiency of vitamin D.

547154.explanation:
{tex}H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits_|^| }\limits_H^H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^H = \mathop {\mathop C\limits^| }\limits^H - \mathop {\mathop C\limits^{||} }\limits^O - H{/tex}
But-2-en-1-al
It has 10{tex}\sigma{/tex} bonds and 2{tex}\pi{/tex} bonds.

547155.explanation: i - c, ii - d, iii - a, iv - e

547156.explanation: Glycogen is a polysaccharide which on hydrolysis gives thousands of glucose units.

547164.explanation: {tex}\underset {\text{1-Chlorobutane}}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-Cl} \xrightarrow [{\text{dry ether}}]{\text {Mg}}{/tex} CH3 - CH2 - CH2 - CH2MgCl {tex}\xrightarrow {\text{CNCl}} \underset {\text{Butyl cyanide}}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-CN} \xrightarrow [{\text{dry ether}}]{\text{CH}_3-\text{MgBr}}{/tex} {tex}CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-\underset {\underset {CH_3}|}C=NMgBr \xrightarrow {H_3O^+}{/tex}{tex}\underset {\text{Hexan-2-one}}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-CH_2-\underset {\underset {CH_3}|}C = O}{/tex}

547166.explanation: i - c, ii - d, iii - a, iv - b

547167.explanation: Galactose is a monosaccharide while lactose, sucrose and maltose are disaccharides.

547171.explanation: the sugar component in RNA is ribose and the sugar component in DNA is 2'-deoxyribose

547172.explanation: The configuration of sugar is related to the configuration of glyceraldehyde. The configuration of D-sugars is related to the configuration of D-glyceraldehyde. The configuration of L-sugars is related to the configuration of L-glyceraldehyde.

547176.explanation: In DNA, adenine and thymine are joined by two hydrogen bonds (i.e., A = T) while cytosine and guanine are joined by three hydrogen bonds (i.e., C {tex}\equiv{/tex} G).

547184.explanation: {tex}\underset {1-Nitropropane}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NO_2} \xrightarrow [{conc. HCl}]{Sn/}{/tex} {tex}\underset {Propanamine}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH_2} \xrightarrow [{\Delta}]{CHCl_3+alc.KOH}{/tex}{tex}\underset {\text{Propyl isocyanide}}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NC} \xrightarrow [{LiAlH_4}]{\text{dry ether}} \underset {N-Methylpropylamine}{CH_3-CH_2-CH_2-NH-CH_3} {/tex}

547185.explanation: Benedict’s test is given by reducing sugars. Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar. Hence, it does not give Benedict’s test.

547187.explanation: Glucose and gluconic acid on reaction with dil. HNO3 get oxidised to saccharic acid. This confirms that the primary alcohol group is present in glucose and in gluconic acid.

547189.explanation:
{tex}\overset 3 CH_2=\overset 2 CH-\overset 1 CH_2-\underset {\underset H |}N-CH_3{/tex}
N-methylprop-2-en-1 -amine

Compound X is an aldehyde i.e., CH3CHO

547200.explanation:
All the given reactions can be categorised as elimination reactions.

  1. (CH3)3C-CH(OH)-CH3 {tex}\xrightarrow[{\mathop {{\text{1,2 - Methyl}}\,{\text{shift}}}\limits_{{\text{( - }}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O)}}} }]{{{\text{Conc}}{\text{.}}\,{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{4}}}}} {/tex} (CH3)2C = C(CH3)2
  2. (CH3)2CH-CH(Br)-CH3 {tex}\xrightarrow{{{\text{Alc}}{\text{.}}\,{\text{KOH}}}}\,{/tex} (CH3)2C = CH-CH3
  3. (CH3)2CH-CH(Br)-CH3 {tex}\xrightarrow{{{{{\text{(C}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{)}}}_{\text{3}}}\,{{\text{O}}^{\text{ - }}}{{\text{K}}^{\text{ + }}}}}\, {/tex} (CH3)2CH-CH = CH2
  4. (CH3)2C(OH)-CH2CHO {tex}\xrightarrow{\Delta }\,{/tex} (CH3)2C = CHCHO

Saytzeff rule: In dehydrohalogenation or in dehydration, the alkene formed by the elimination has a greater number of alkyl groups attached to the doubly bonded carbon atom.
Reaction (i): Involves the 1,2-methyl shift, as carbocation formed during the course of reaction undergoes rearrangement to form a more stable highly substituted alkene. Thus, forms a Saytzeff product.
Reaction (ii): Involves a simple dehydrohalogenation reaction, which is in accordance with the Saytzeff rule.
Reaction (iii): It’s an example of an elimination reaction in presence of bulky base potassium tert-butoxide. In this case, base abstracts hydrogen from the less hindered site of a substrate molecule which is in contrast with the Saytzeff rule.
Reaction (iv): The product of dehydration is highly substituted {tex}\alpha{/tex}, {tex}\beta{/tex}-unsaturated compound. Thus, only reaction (iii), will not produce Saytzeff product.

The reaction can be reversed and the additional products can be decomposed back to the original aldehydes by the action of mineral acids or the aqueous alkalies. Thus, the reaction is used in the purification and separation of aldehydes from other non-carbonyl compounds.

547395.explanation: Due to ortho effect, o-phenylenediamine is the weakest base even weaker than aniline. Since NH2 group has a strong electron-donating resonance effect (+R effect) and a weak electron-withdrawing inductive effect (-1 effect), therefore, both p-phenylenediamine and m-phenylenediamine are stronger bases than aniline. However, when -NH2 group is present at m-position, it can exert only its -I effect since +R effect does not operate at m-position. This decreases the electron density on the N-atom and hence m-phenylenediamine is a weaker base than p-phenylenediamine. Thus, the relative basicity of o-, m- and p-phenylenediamine w.r.t. aniline decreases in order.

547397.explanation: Unlike methyl group which has only electron-donating hyperconjugation effect, -OCH3 group has both electron-donating resonance effect (i.e., +R effect) as well as electron-withdrawing inductive effect (i.e., -I effect). When -OCH3 group is present at m-position, it can exert only its -I effect since resonance effect does not operate at m-position. This decreases the electron density on the nitrogen atom to such an extent that m-anisidine becomes a weaker base than o-anisidine. Among p-anisidine and o-anisidine, o-anisidine is a weaker base aniline due to ortho effect while p-anisidine is a stronger base than aniline due to +R effect of -OCH3 group. Thus, the overall basicity of o, m- and p-anisidines relative to aniline follows the order:

547398.explanation:
The priorities at C2 and C3 as per Cahn-Ingold-Prelog convention;

At C2, the direction is anticlockwise whereas at C3 it is clockwise, however the lowest priority group -H is on front. Moving -H at the back of the page will change the direction. At C2, it will become clockwise while at C3, it will become anticlockwise. Lowest priority comes forward, so assignment rules are backwards. The stereochemical designation for clockwise is R and that for anticlockwise is S.

547658.explanation:
Reactivity of an alkyl halide in {tex}\mathrm{S}_{\mathrm{N}} 2{/tex} mechanism is in the following order:
CH3X > 1° alkyl halide > 2° alkyl halide > 3° alkyl halide.

547659.explanation:
Alkaline hydrolysis of tertiary butyl bromide follows SN1 mechanism.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of reaction {tex}\propto{/tex} [(CH3)3C - Br]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate is doubled if the concentration of tertiary butyl bromide is doubled. In SN1 mechanism, the reaction rate does not depend on the concentration of nucleophiles.

547660.explanation: Electron deficient chemical species act as electron acceptors hence termed as Lewis acid or an electrophile. A nucleophile is a negatively charged chemical species or a species carrying at least one lone pair of electrons.

547661.explanation: The given reaction follows SN1 mechanism. Hence, the racemic mixture is formed i.e., ({tex}\pm{/tex}) pentan-2-ol.

547663.explanation:
For any SN1 reaction reactivity is decided by the ease of dissociation of alkyl halide,
R - X {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} R+ + X-
Higher the stability of R (carbocation), higher would be reactivity of SN1 reaction. Stability of carbocation follows the order,
CH3−CH2−{tex}\mathop C\limits^ + {/tex}H2 < CH3−{tex}\mathop C\limits^ + {/tex}H−CH2−CH3 < p−H3CO−C6H4−{tex}\mathop C\limits^ + {/tex}H2
Hence, the correct increasing order of reactivity of the given alkyl halides for SN1 reaction is II < I < III.

547664.explanation: A compound in option ({tex}{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_|^| }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_3}{{\text{H}}_7}}^H - {\text{Br}}{/tex}) i.e., {tex}{\text{C}}{{\text{H}}_3} - \mathop {\mathop {\text{C}}\limits_|^{{|_*}} }\limits_{{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5}}^{{{\text{C}}_2}{{\text{H}}_5}} - {\text{Br}}{/tex}  contains a chiral carbon (carbon marked with '*'). In SN1 the attack of the nucleophile may occur from both the sides (front and back) with almost equal ease to form a 1 : 1 mixture of both the enantiomers, giving a racemic mixture.

547665.explanation: Chloroethene (i.e., vinyl chloride) is the least reactive towards nucleophilic substitution because -Cl is directly attached to sp2 hybridised C-atom (vinylic C-atom).

547667.explanation: p-orbital of fluorine and sp2 hybrid orbital of C-atom of benzene

547668.explanation: C-I bond energy is the least and hence the reaction is the fastest with benzene in presence of anhydrous AlCl3.

547669.explanation: Haloarenes are electron-rich and hence they repel the incoming nucleophile which is also electron-rich resulting in lesser reactivity towards nucleophilic substitution reactions. For example, the substitution of halogen in haloarenes by -OH, -CN or -NH2 group is a nucleophilic substitution reaction.

547670.explanation: It has been found that presence of electron withdrawing groups like —NO2, -COOH, -CN at o- and/or p - position with respect to halogen atom greatly activates haloarenes to undergo nucleophilic displacement reactions.

547673.explanation: Any substance which when added to a chemical reaction, inhibits it, is called a catalytic inhibitor. In CHCl3 when one percent ethanol is added, it stops the formation of carbonyl chloride. So, ethanol acts as a catalytic inhibitor.

547674.explanation: Grignard’s reagent is an example of the organometallic compound having a sigma bond while ferrocene, cobaltocene, and ruthenocene are organometallic {tex}\pi{/tex} complexes.

(CH3)3C - Br + NaOH(aq) {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} (CH3)3C - OH + NaBr
Rate {tex}\propto{/tex} [(CH3)3C - Br]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate = k[(CH3)3C - Br]
Hence, with respect to the nucleophile (OH-), the order of the reaction is zero.

547687.explanation: In SN1 reaction, the order of reactivity of alkyl/aryl halides is 3° > 2° > 1°. The compounds in (ii) and (iii) are secondary halides. But the carbocation intermediate obtained from (ii) is more stable than that obtained from (iii) because it is stabilized by two phenyl groups due to resonance. Hence, (ii) is more reactive than (iii) in SN1 reactions.

  3. Reaction (b) is faster than reaction (a) since concentration of (CH3)3CCl in reaction (b) is double than that in reaction (a).

In the case of biphenyls, the bulky ortho-substituents restrict the free rotation and the rings remain perpendicular to each other. When these substituents are different as in the given option

674937.explanation:
The given example contains two chiral carbons C - 2 and C - 3 (figure, a).

From figure (b), it can be seen that, the four substituents attached to C-2 can be arranged in order of priority as -OH (1'), H3C-{tex}\mathop C\limits^| {/tex}H-Cl (2'), -COOH (3') and -H(4'). Similarly, the four substituents attached to C - 3 can be arranged in order of priority as -Cl(1"), HOOC - CH - OH (2"), -CH3(3") and -H(4"). In case of C-2, the order of priority is in clockwise direction. But the lowest priority group (-H) is on the horizontal line i.e., projects above the plane of paper. Therefore, the actual configuration is opposite to that of the observed configuration. Thus, C-2 is assigned S-configuration. In case of C-3, the order of priority is in anticlockwise direction. But the lowest priority group (-H) is on the horizontal line i.e., projects above the plane of paper. Therefore, the actual configuration is opposite to that of the observed configuration. Thus, C-3 is assigned R-configuration. So, the correct answer is (2S, 3R).

675000.explanation: The presence of electron withdrawing groups at o- and p- positions with respect to halogen atom activates the aryl halides towards nucleophilic substitution reactions due to negative charge present at these positions. Such carbanions are stabilized by resonance offered by the electron withdrawing group as well as by the {tex}\pi{/tex}-electrons of the benzene ring. However, in case of m-substituted aryl halides, there is no charge at m-position in the resonating structures. Therefore, the presence of electron withdrawing group at m-position has no effect on reactivity. Thus, compound (II) and (IV) are more reactive towards nucleophilic substitution than compound (III).

675002.explanation: The presence of electron-withdrawing groups at o- and p-positions with respect to halogen atom activates the aryl halides towards nucleophilic substitution reactions. As the number of electron-withdrawing groups at o- and p-positions increases, the reactivity of aryl halides also increases.

680818.explanation:
NH3​ < primary amine < tertiary amine < secondary amine
This is because:

  1. Steric hindrance
    The size of an alkyl group is more than that of a hydrogen atom. So, an alkyl group would hinder the attack of a hydrogen atom, thus decreasing the basicity of the molecule. So, the more the number of alkyl groups attached, lesser will be its basicity. 
  2. Solvation of ions
    When amines are dissolved in water, they form protonated amines. Also, the number of possibilities for hydrogen bonding also increases. More the number of hydrogen bonding more is the hydration that is released in the process of the formation of hydrogen bonds.

volume of solution = 50 ml = 0.05 L

680841.explanation:
In a lead storage battery, all of these statements are true.
Explanation:

  1. The lead storage battery is a rechargeable battery.
  2. This battery has a pure lead as anode; lead(IV) oxide as a cathode and dilute sulfuric acid as an electrolyte.
  3. When the battery is being used, lead(IV) oxide is reduced to PdSO4 and lead is oxidized to PdSO4.
  4. When the battery is being charged, PdSO₄ is again converted into PbO2 and Pb as cathode and anode respectively.

683816.explanation: Alkyl halides on alkaline hydrolysis (aqueous NaOH) get converted into alcohol. This takes place by a nucleophilic substitution reaction where the −X atom is substituted by a nucleophile i.e −OH group. The primary alkyl halides undergo nucleophilic substitution reaction by SN2​ substitution mechanism, while tertiary alkyl halides follow SN1​ substitution mechanism.

683872.explanation:

Since the overall charge on the complex Ni(CO)4 is 0, the sum of oxidation states of all elements in it should be equal to 0.
Therefore, x + 4(0) = 0
Hence, x = 0
Thus, the oxidation state of Ni in Ni(CO)4​ is equal to 0.

Now if an electrode is having greater electrode potential than standard or zero scales then we can infer that its reduced form is more stable compared to hydrogen (H​​​​​​2) gas.  It is more likely that metals ions will be formed than the hydrogen gas.

730732.explanation:
The decomposition of PH3 on tungsten at low pressure is a first-order reaction. So, the rate is directly proportional to the concentration of PH3
Rate = k[PH3]
At low pressure, the surface area covered is proportional to the partial pressure of PH3 and hence, the rate is directly proportional to the surface coverage.

730733.explanation:
The rate of the reaction is
{tex}+\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{D}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=+\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{1 \mathrm{~d}[\mathrm{~B}]}{2 \mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
Terms {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{D}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} have a negative sign. This indicates that D and B are reactants. Terms {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} have a positive sign. This indicates that C and A are products. Therefore, the reaction is B + D {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A + C.
The fraction {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} in front of {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} indicates that the coefficient of C in the equation is 3. Similarly, the coefficients of D, A and B are 3, 4 and 2, respectively. Hence, the reaction is 2B + 3D {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4A + 3C.

730734.explanation:
2N2O5(g) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 4NO2(g) + O2(g)
Rate = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{4} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{4}{2}\left(-\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.04 = 0.08 M/s

730736.explanation:
For first order reaction, Rate = k[NO2Cl] Therefore,
Rate = k[NO2Cl] = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2} \mathrm{Cl}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d}\left[\mathrm{Cl}_{2}\right]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k[NO2Cl] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} k1[NO2Cl] = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} k2[NO2Cl] = k3[NO2Cl]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}k1 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}k2 = k3
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  k1 = k2 = 2k3

730737.explanation:
The rate law is, rate = k[A]x[B]y
where, rate = 0.02 M s-1,
rate constant (k) = 0.01 s-1
Concentration of A = [A] = 2M;
Concentration of B = [B] = 2M
Substituting values in the rate law,
0.02 M s-1 = 0.01 s-1 [2 M]x [2 M]y
{tex}\therefore \frac{0.02 \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}}{0.01 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{/tex} = [2 M]x+y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2M = [2 M]x+y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 1

730739.explanation:
The rate law expression is k[A]x[B]y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate = k[A]x[B]y
Substituting the given values in above equation for three separate experiments we get,
1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = k[0.2]x[0.2]y ...(i)
1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = k[0.2]x[0.4]y ...(ii)
1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-2 = k[0.4]x[0.2]y ...(iii)
Dividing equation (i) by (ii)
{tex}\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.2]^{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.4]^{\mathrm{y}}}{/tex}
1 = {tex}\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^{y}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 0
Now Dividing equation (i) by (iii)
{tex}\frac{1.2 \times 10^{-2}}{2.4 \times 10^{-2}}=\frac{\mathrm{k}[0.2]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.2]^{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{k}[0.4]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.2]^{y}}{/tex}
{tex}\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^{1}=\left[\frac{1}{2}\right]^{x}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 1
Hence, the rate law expression becomes
Rate = k[A]1 [B]0 = k[A] ({tex}\because{/tex} [B]0 = 1]

= 0.013 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.013)2

730757.explanation:
Rate of the given reaction is doubled when the concentration of A is doubled and it is halved when concentrations of A and B are doubled.
Let the rate, R = k[A]x[B]y ...(i)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} When concentration of A is doubled, the new rate (R1) will be
R1 = k[2A]x[B]y ...(ii)
But R1 = 2R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k[2A]x[B]y = 2k[A]x[B]y ...[From (i) and (ii)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k2x [A]x[B]y = 2k[A]x[B]y
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x = 2 and thus x = 1
Similarly, when concentrations of both A and B are doubled, the new rate (R2) will be
R2 = k[2A]x[2B]y ...(iii)
But R2 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}R
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k[2A]x[2B]y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}k[A]x[B]y ...[From (i) and (iii)]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2x{tex}\cdot{/tex}2y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2{tex}\cdot{/tex}2y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} ({tex}\because{/tex} x = 1)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 2y = {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex} = 2-2 and thus, y = -2
Substituting the values of x and y in (i), the rate expression for the given reaction is rate, R = k[A]1[B]-2
Thus, the order of the given reaction with respect to:
i. 'A' is 1 and ii. 'B' is -2
The overall order of the given reaction is -1.

730760.explanation:
In general, for nth order reaction, the unit of rate constant = (Concentration)1-n (time)-1
So, 1 - n = -2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} n = 3
Thus, the unit of rate constant M-2 s-1 indicates that the given reaction must be third order.
Rate = k[A][B]2 is third order overall.

730772.explanation:
pi = 2 bar, pf = 0.1 bar, t = 1000 min
For a gas phase first order reaction,
k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\text {t}}{/tex} log10{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{i}}}{\mathrm{p}_{\mathrm{f}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{1000}{/tex} log10{tex}[\frac{2}{0.1}]=\frac{2.303}{1000}{/tex} log10(20)
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{1000}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 1.3010
= 2.99 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 {tex}\approx{/tex} 3.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 min-1

730781.explanation:
For first order reaction, integrated rate law is derived as
k = {tex}\frac{1}{\text{t}}{/tex} in{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex}
If [A] at t = 0 is 'a' and decrease in concentration of A at 't' time is 'x' then
k = {tex}\frac{1}{\text{t}}{/tex} ln{tex}\frac{a}{(a-x)}{/tex} ...(i)
k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{\text{t}}{/tex} log10{tex}\frac{a}{(a-x)}{/tex}
Equation (i) can be written as
kt = ln{tex}\frac{a}{(a-x)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} -kt = ln{tex}\frac{(a-x)}{a}{/tex}
Taking antilog,
e-kt = {tex}\frac{(a-x)}{a}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} (a - x) = a{tex}\cdot{/tex}e-kt
Half-life {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} is the time when concentration is reduced to half of original concentration,
x = {tex}\frac{a}{2}{/tex}
From equation (i),
t = {tex}\frac{1}{\text{k}}{/tex} ln{tex}\frac{a}{(a-x)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\text{k}}{/tex} ln{tex}\frac a{\frac a2}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\ln (2)}{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{\ln (2)}{\mathrm{t_\frac{1}{2}}}{/tex}

730784.explanation:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} 90 = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.693}\times t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 27.08 min
3{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 27.08 = 81.24 min

730787.explanation:

{tex}\therefore{/tex} t(99.9%) = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.693}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 9.9696 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Thus, it takes 10 half-lives to complete the reaction by 99.9 %.
