Hence, the reaction is First order with respect to [A] and zero-order with respect to [B].

730801.explanation: From the equation k = {tex}A e^{\frac{\text{-Ea}}{\text {R T}}}{/tex}, rate constant (or rate of reaction) increases exponentially with increase in temperature and decrease in the value of {tex}\frac{E_a}{RT}{/tex}.

730806.explanation: k = {tex}A\cdot e^\frac{-E_a}{RT}{/tex}, k {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{/tex}, higher the value of k, lower will be Ea.

730811.explanation:
k1 = {tex}\frac{1}{10}{/tex}k2; {tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{/tex} = 10
log10{tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{a}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right]{/tex}
Substituting values in the above expression,
log10(10) = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{2.303 \mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{1}{200}-\frac{1}{400}\right]{/tex}
1 = {tex}\frac{E_{a}}{2.303 R}\left(\frac{1}{400}\right){/tex}
Ea = 921.2 R

730816.explanation:
k1 = {tex}\mathrm{Ae}^{\frac{-\mathrm{Ea}_{\mathrm{1}}}{\mathrm{RT}}}{/tex} (uncatalysed reaction)
k2 = {tex}\mathrm{Ae}^{\frac{-\mathrm{Ea}_{\mathrm{2}}}{\mathrm{RT}}}{/tex} (catalysed reaction)
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\frac{\mathrm{Ae}^{\frac{\mathrm{Ea}_{2}}{{ }^{\mathrm{RT}}}}}{\mathrm{Ae}^{\frac{-\mathrm{E} a}{\mathrm{RT}}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\frac{-\mathrm{Ea}_{2}+\mathrm{Ea}_{1}}{\mathrm{RT}}}=\mathrm{e}^{\frac{50}{{k T}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\frac{50 \times 10^{3}}{8.314 \times 300}}=\mathrm{e}^{20.05}{/tex}

730827.explanation:
According to collision theory, successful conversion of reactant(s) to product(s) takes place when,

  1. reactant molecules possess sufficient threshold energy, and
  2. are properly oriented with respect to each other at the time of the collision.

730833.explanation:
2A + B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} A2B
Initial 0.2   0.4   0
After time t -2x   -x   +x
  (0.2 - 2x)   (0.4 - x)    

[A]t = 0.2 - 2x = 0.12
2x = 0.08
x = 0.04
[B] = 0.4 - 0.04 = 0.36
Now rate = k[A]2[B]
= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-6 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.12)2 (0.36)
rate = 1.04 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-8 M s-1

730887.explanation:
2NO + 2H2 {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} N2 + 2H2O
Assume rate = k [NO]x [H2]y
{tex}\therefore \frac{\text { rate } 2}{\text { rate } 1}=\frac{5.00 \times 10^{-5}}{1.25 \times 10^{-5}}=\frac{ \mathrm{k}\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{x}\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{\mathrm{y}}}{\mathrm{k}\left(5 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{x}\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{\mathrm{y}}}{/tex}
2x = 4
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 2 i.e., reaction is second order in NO
Similarly,
{tex}\frac{\text { rate } 3}{\text { rate } 2}=\frac{1.00 \times 10^{-4}}{5.00 \times 10^{-5}}=\frac{ k\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{x}\left(4 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{y}}{ k\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{x}\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)^{y}}{/tex}
2y = 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} y = 1 i.e., reaction is first order in H2
Experiment 2:
Rate = k[NO]2 [H2]
k = {tex}\frac{5.00 \times 10^{-5}}{\left(1 \times 10^{-2}\right)^{2}\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right)}{/tex} = 250 M-2 s-1
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate law:
Rate = 250 [NO]2 [H2]
3.7 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 = 250 [NO]2 (6.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [NO]2 = {tex}\frac{3.70 \times 10^{-5}}{250 \times 6.2 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
[NO] = 4.9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M

730893.explanation:
ln {tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}\right){/tex}
ln {tex}\left(\frac{0.28}{0.11}\right)=\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{312.5}-\frac{1}{322.6}\right){/tex}
0.934 = {tex}\frac{E_a}{8.314}{/tex}(1.00 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
9340 {tex}\times{/tex} 8.314 = Ea
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ea = 77653 J/mol
Rate constant at 80oC, i.e., 353 K
T1 = 353 K, k1 = ?
T2 = 312.5 K, k2 = 0.11 s-1
ln{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{0.11}\right)=\frac{77653}{8.314}\left(\frac{1}{312.5}-\frac{1}{353}\right){/tex}
ln{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{0.11}\right){/tex} = 9340 (3.67 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4)
ln{tex}\left(\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{0.11}\right){/tex} = 3.4278
{tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{0.11}{/tex} = 30.81
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k1 = 3.39 {tex}\approx{/tex} 3.4

733945.explanation:
The given reaction is A + 2B {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} C
Rate of the reaction = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{C}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of disappearance of A
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} rate of disappearance of B
= rate of appearance of C

733949.explanation:
Change in concentration of a reactant,
{tex}\Delta{/tex}[R] = -0.2 M
Change in time ({tex}\Delta{/tex}t) = 20 minutes
Average rate of reaction = {tex}-\frac{\Delta[\mathrm{R}]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac {\text {(-0.2 M)}}{\text {20 min}}{/tex} = 0.01 M min-1

733955.explanation:
{tex}-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = 1.28 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M/s
The average rate of reaction is
{tex}-\frac{1}{2}​\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=​​\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex} = 0.64 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M/s

733961.explanation: The rate of a reaction is same at any time during the reaction.

733963.explanation: Increasing the volume of vessels decreases the pressure of reactants which will slow down the gas-phase reaction. Increasing the pressure will increase the speed of the reaction.

733967.explanation: Rate constant is independent of the concentration of reactant. Hence, even though the concentration of A is reduced to half, the value of the rate constant will remain the same i.e., 0.30 s-1.

733977.explanation: the sum of powers to which the concentration terms are raised in the rate law

733984.explanation:
The rate law shows that the reaction is {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} order in A, {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex} order in B
The order of the reaction is calculated as
Order of reaction = {tex}\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}{/tex} = 0.67

733993.explanation:
The rate law is, Rate = k{tex}[\mathrm{B}]^{\frac{1}{2}}[\mathrm{~C}]^{\frac{-3}{2}}{/tex}
The overall order of the reaction is
{tex}\frac{1}{2}+\frac{-3}{2}{/tex} = -1
 The other three rate laws have overall order zero.

733998.explanation:
Rate = 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1
[A] = 0.5 mol L-1
Order of reaction = 1
The rate law is, Rate = k[A]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 6.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 mol L-1 s-1 = k {tex}\times{/tex} 0.5 mol L-1
k = 1.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 s-1

734008.explanation: Rate = k[A][B]2, hence by increasing the concentration of [B] by 3 times, the rate will increase by 9 times.

734012.explanation:
Since changing the concentration of 2 does not affect the rate, the reaction is zero order in reactant 2.
Suppose reactant 1 is A.
The rate law is,
Rate = k[A]x ...(i)
2Rate = k[2A]x ...(ii)
Dividing (ii) by (i),
2 = 2x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 1
Hence, the rate law can be given as Rate = k[A]
The overall order of the reaction is 1.

734016.explanation:
For reaction A {tex}\to{/tex} B
The rate law is,
Rate = k[A]x ...(i)
3Rate = k[9A]x ...(ii)
Dividing (ii) by (i),
3 = 9x
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

734023.explanation: The unit for rate constant of a zero order reaction is (concentration) (time)-1 (i.e.) mol L-1 s-1.

734039.explanation:
Units of k = (mol L-1)-1 s-1
Comparing it with the unit of rate constant of nth order reaction: (mol L-1)1-n s-1,
1 - n = -1
n = 1 + 1 = 2

734051.explanation: Molecularity is the total number of reactant molecules that are involved in an elementary reaction. Therefore, for the given elementary reaction molecularity will be equal to m + n + p.

734063.explanation: Rate law is determined from the stoichiometric coefficients of the reactants in the slow step (rate-determining step).

734075.explanation: NO is formed in step (i) and consumed in step (ii). Hence, it is a reaction intermediate.

734079.explanation:
For the reaction, A {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} B
k = 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M s-1
Since the units of k is M s-1, it is a zero order reaction
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-[\mathrm{A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{\mathrm{t}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = 20 min = 1200 s
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 0.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M s-1 = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-5 \mathrm{M}}{1200 \mathrm{~s}}{/tex}
[A]0 = 5.72 M
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [B]t = [A]0 - [A]t = 5.72 - 5 = 0.72 M

734086.explanation:
[A]t - [A]0 = -kt
[A]t = -kt + [A]0
This is straight-line equation with slope = -k and intercept [A]0.

734090.explanation:
For zero-order reaction
{tex}t_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{[A]_{0}}{2 k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} If [A]0 is doubled, the {tex}t_{\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} is doubled.

734102.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
Percentage completion of reaction = 99%
For a first-order reaction, time required for X% completion of reaction can be calculated if rate constant is known using the formula, t(x%) = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log10 {tex}\frac{100}{100-X}{/tex}
t(99%) = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = {tex}\frac {4.606}{k}{/tex}

734106.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
Percentage completion of reaction = 99%
For a first-order reaction, time required for X% completion of reaction can be calculated if rate constant is known using the formula, t(x%) = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log10 {tex}\frac{100}{100-X}{/tex}
t(99%) = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t(99%) = {tex}\frac{2.303}{0.2303}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 2 = 20 min

734114.explanation:
The half-life of the first-order reaction is given by equation {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
The equation implies that the half-life of a first-order reaction is constant and is independent of the reactant concentration. Since, in a given reaction, change in concentration does not affect its half-life; it must be a first-order reaction.

734119.explanation: The average rate of reaction is equal to the average rate of disappearance of a reactant or the average rate of appearance of a product divided by their respective stoichiometric coefficients in the balanced chemical equation.

734122.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
The half-life period is 30 minutes
Percentage completion of reaction = 75%
After two half-life periods, the reaction will be 75% complete.
t = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 30 = 60 min

734123.explanation:
The average rate of reaction is
{tex}-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=-\frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{O}_{2}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta\left[\mathrm{SO}_{3}\right]}{\Delta \mathrm{t}}{/tex}

734130.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
Amount of the substance left after n half-lives is given as [A]t = {tex}\frac{[{A}]_{0}}{2^{\mathrm{n}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{2^n}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} n = 2
2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 24s (given)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 12s
k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_{\frac{1}{2}}}=\frac{0.693}{12}{/tex}s-1

734134.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}=\frac{0.693}{k}=\frac{0.693}{10^{-2}}{/tex} = 69.3 s
20 g {tex}\xrightarrow{{69.3\,s}} {/tex} 10 g {tex}\xrightarrow{{69.3\,s}} {/tex} 5 g
Total time = 138.6 s

734138.explanation:
For first-order reaction,
Half-time, {tex}t_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{\ln (2)}{k}=\frac{2.303 \times \log _{10} 2}{2.303 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.3010}{10^{-3}}{/tex}
= 301 s
Therefore, 40 g of substance will reduce to 20 g in 301 s and will reduce to 10 g in another 301 s.
Hence, time is taken for 40 g of the substance to reduce to 10 g = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 301 s = 602 s

734139.explanation:
For the given reaction,
3A {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} 2B
Rate = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{B}]}{\mathrm{dt}}=-\frac{2}{3} \frac{\mathrm{d}[\mathrm{A}]}{\mathrm{dt}}{/tex}

734142.explanation:
For first-order reaction, the rate law is
k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t}{/tex} log10{tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex} ...(i)
At the end of time {tex}(t_\frac{1}{4}){/tex} corresponding to {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}th life,
[A]t = (1 - {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}) [A]0 = {tex}\frac{3}{4}{/tex}[A]0
{tex}\therefore\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}
Substituting this in equation (i),
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{4}=\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} log10{tex}\frac{4}{3}{/tex}

734149.explanation:
Nuclear decays follow first-order kinetics.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Half-time {tex}\left(t_{\frac{1}{ 2}}\right)=\frac{0.693}{k}=\frac{0.693}{1.386 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}}{/tex}
= 500 s
To reduce activity to {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}th, two half-lives are taken
Thus, t = 2 {tex}\times{/tex} {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex}= 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 500 = 1000 s

734155.explanation:
For a first-order reaction, Rate = k[A]
{tex}\therefore{/tex} [A] = {tex}\frac{\text {Rate}}{\text {k}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} At t = 10 s, [A]0 = {tex}\frac{0.04}{k}{/tex}
At  = 20 s, i.e., after 10 s,
[A]t = {tex}\frac{0.03}{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t}{/tex} log10{tex}\left(\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}\right){/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{10}{/tex} log10 {tex}\frac{\frac{0.04}{\mathrm{k}}}{\frac{0.03}{\mathrm{k}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{10}{/tex} log10 {tex}\frac{0.04}{0.03}=\frac{2.303}{10}{/tex} {tex}\times{/tex} 0.1249
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}t_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{0.693}{k}=\frac{0.693 \times 10}{2.303 \times 0.1249}{/tex}
= 24.09 s {tex}\approx{/tex} 24.1 s

734161.explanation:
For the first-order reaction, {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
For a second-order reaction, {tex}t_\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1}{k[A]_0}{/tex}

734167.explanation: In the formation of activated complex, the kinetic energy of reactants is converted into potential energy of activated complex, which is required to overcome the repulsions between reactant molecules. Hence, the potential energy of the activated complex is greater than the potential energy of either reactants or products. In an endothermic reaction, the enthalpy of the product is greater than the reactant because heat is absorbed in the reaction. An exothermic reaction, the enthalpy of the product is less than the reactant because heat is evolved in the reaction.

734170.explanation:
In a plot of In k vs {tex}\frac{1}{\text {T}}{/tex}
-Slope = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{R}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 1000 = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{8.314}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ea = 8.314 {tex}\times{/tex} 1000 = 8314 J mol-1
= 8.314 kJ mol-1

734176.explanation:
The expression for the rate constant (k) is
k = {tex}\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{\frac{-E_a}{\mathrm{RT}}}=\mathrm{A} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-\frac{4000}{\mathrm{~T}}}{/tex}
(where A = pre-exponential factor, Ea = activation energy, T = temperature, R = ideal gas constant)
{tex}\therefore \frac{-E_{2}}{R}=\frac{-E_{a}}{8.314}{/tex} = -4000
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Ea = 33,256 J
{tex}\because{/tex} 1 cal = 4.184 J
{tex}\frac{33256}{4.184}{/tex}cal = 7948 cal {tex}\cong {/tex} 8000 cal = 8 kcal

734179.explanation:
From Arrhenius equation,
ln{tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}=\frac{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{R}}\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{2}}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}_{1}}\right){/tex}
Since Ea = 0,
ln{tex}\frac{\mathrm{k}_{2}}{\mathrm{k}_{1}}{/tex} = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k2 = k1
Hence, rate constant at 400 K = 1.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 106 s-1

734182.explanation: Catalyst does not affect the free energy change ({tex}\Delta{/tex}G) for the reaction.

734184.explanation: The rate of reaction depends upon the number of effective collisions. As the temperature decreases, the number of effective collisions decreases which results in a decrease in the rate of reaction.

734188.explanation: Collision frequency increaes with increase in the number of molecules per unit volume.

734192.explanation: the collision frequency of reactants, A and B

734196.explanation: The minimum kinetic energy required for a molecular collision to lead to a chemical reaction is called activation energy.

734198.explanation: The slowest step of a complex reaction is always an elementary reaction and the slowest step is the rate-determining step. The rate law depends on stoichiometric coefficients of the slowest step. Hence, the molecularity of the slowest step of a complex reaction can never be the same as the order of the reaction.

734944.explanation:
Order of reaction = 0
k = 0.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 M s-1
[A]0 = 5 M
{tex}t_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{[A]_{0}}{2 k}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{5}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex}
= 5000 s
= 83.33 min

734981.explanation:
Order of the reaction = 1
Rate constant (k) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-5 s-1
[A]0 = 100, [A]t = 100 - 30 = 70
Integrated rate law for the first order reaction is:
k = {tex}\frac{2.303}{t}{/tex} log10 {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} t = {tex}\frac{2.303}{k}{/tex} log10 {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}}{[\mathrm{~A}]_{\mathrm{t}}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{2.303}{1\times10^{-5}}{/tex} log10 {tex}(\frac{100}{70}){/tex} = {tex}\frac{2.303}{1\times10^{-5}}{/tex} log10 (1.43)
= 3.57 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 s

735021.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}{/tex} = 6.93 h
{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.693}{6.93 h}{/tex}
= 0.1 h-1
Thus, the value of rate constant for the given reaction is 0.1 h-1.

735031.explanation:
Order of reaction = 1
k = 7.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-4 s-1
{tex}t_\frac{1}{2}=\frac{0.693}{k}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{0.693}{7.0\times10^{-4}}{/tex}
= 990 s
Thus, the half-life of the given reaction = 990 s

735082.explanation:
By Arrhenius equation
k = {tex}A e^\frac{-\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex}
By taking logarithms on both sides,
ln k = in A - {tex}\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}{/tex}
on plotting ln k vs {tex}\frac{1}{\text {T}}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex} By knowing slope, activation energy can be determined.

735283.explanation:
ln k = {tex}\frac{-E_{a}}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right){/tex} + ln A
Slope = {tex}\frac{-E_{a}}{R}{/tex}
Ea = -(Slope) (R)
= -(-5.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 103 K) (8.314 K-1 mol-1)
Ea = 45727 {tex}\approx{/tex} 4.6 {tex}\times{/tex} 104 J/mol

735313.explanation:
The plot indicates a zero order reaction
[A]t = 0.32 M
[A]0 = 1 M (from plot)
t = 200 seconds
k = {tex}\frac{[\mathrm{A}]_{0}-[\mathrm{A}]_{1}}{\mathrm{t}}=\frac{1-0.32}{200}{/tex} = 3.4 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-3 s-1
Now, {tex}\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{\mid \mathrm{A}]_{0}}{2 \mathrm{k}}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{t}_{\frac{1}{ 2}}=\frac{1}{2 \times 3.4 \times 10^{-3}}{/tex} = 147 seconds

735341.explanation:
k = {tex}\mathrm{Ae}^{\left(-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)}{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} k {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{e}^{\left(\frac{1}{T}\right)}{/tex}
Therefore, k vs T graph,

If T is constant and Ea is variable, then
k {tex}\propto{/tex} {tex}e^{\left(-E_{a}\right)}{/tex}
The graph of k(Rate) vs Ea will be

For a reaction, Ea remains constant irrespective of temperature. Thus, Ea vs T graph will be

Since, k = {tex}\mathrm{Ae}^{\left(-\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\mathrm{RT}}\right)}{/tex}

For weak acid, {tex}\alpha=\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_0}{/tex}
Ka {tex}\frac{\mathrm{c} \alpha^2}{1-\alpha} \Rightarrow{/tex} Ka (1 - {tex}\alpha{/tex}) = c{tex}\alpha^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}\left(1-\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_0}\right){/tex} = {tex}\mathrm{c}\left(\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}}{\Lambda_0}\right)^2{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ka - {tex}\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\Lambda_0}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{c} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^2}{\left(\Lambda_0\right)^2}{/tex}  {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Ka = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{c} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^2}{\left(\Lambda_0\right)^2}+\frac{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}} \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}}}{\Lambda_0}{/tex}
Divide by '{tex}\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex}'
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{K_a}{\Lambda_m}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{c \Lambda_m}{\left(\Lambda_0\right)^2}+\frac{K_a}{\Lambda_0}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{\Lambda_m}=\frac{c \Lambda_m}{K_a\left(\Lambda_0\right)^2}+\frac{1}{\Lambda_0}{/tex}
For the Plot {tex}\frac{1}{\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}} \operatorname{vsc} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}{/tex}, Slope = {tex}\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\Lambda_0\right)^2}{/tex} = S
y-intercept = {tex}\frac{1}{\Lambda_0}{/tex} = P {tex}\therefore \frac{\mathrm{P}}{\mathrm{S}} {/tex} = {tex}\frac{\frac{1}{\Lambda_0}}{\frac{1}{\mathrm{~K}_{\mathrm{a}}\left(\Lambda_0\right)^2}}=\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{a}} \Lambda_0{/tex}

1097707.explanation:
The order of reactivity is dependent on the stability of the intermediate carbocation formed by cleavage of C-X bond. The 3o carbocation formed in III will be more stable than its 2o homologue (IV) which in turn will be more stable than arenium ion (I). Moreover, the aryl halide has a double bond character in the C-X bond which makes the cleavage more difficult. On other hand II will be more reactive than I due to presence of electron withdrawing -NO2 group. C-X bond becomes weak and undergoes nucleophilic substitution reaction.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order: I < II < IV < III.

In acetic acid the electron withdrawing {tex}-\underset{\mathrm O}{\underset{\vert\vert}{\mathrm C}}-{/tex} group polarizes the O-H bond and increases the acid strength.

Thus, the order of acidic strength will be III > II > IV > I

1098135.explanation:
Based on the number of metal atoms present in a complex, they are classified into mononuclear, dinuclear, trinuclear and so on. e.g:
Fe(CO)5: mononuclear
CO2(CO)8: dinuclear
Fe3(CO)12: trinuclear
Fe(CO)5 is mononuclear because,
EAN = Z - O.N. + 2(C.N)
Here, Z = Atomic no., O.N. = Oxidation no., C.N = Coordination no.
So, EAN = 26 - 0 + 2(5) = 36
Only one central atom is present and it follows EAN rules, so it is mononuclear.

From the given data

  1. Cu(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu+2(aq) + 2e-
    {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{ }_1{/tex} = -2(-0.34) {tex}\times{/tex} F (As, {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}{/tex} = -nFEo)
    (As, {tex}\Delta{/tex}Go = -nFEo)
  2. Cu+2 (aq) + e- {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu+ (aq)
    {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_2^{\circ}{/tex} = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} (0.15) {tex}\times{/tex} F
    On adding (i) and (ii), we get
    Cu(s) {tex}\longrightarrow{/tex} Cu+(aq) + e-
    {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_3^{\circ}{/tex} = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} Eo {tex}\times{/tex} F
    {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_3^{\circ}{/tex} = {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_1^{\circ}{/tex} + {tex}\Delta \mathrm{G}_2^{\circ}{/tex}
    -n3FEo = -n1F{tex}{E}_1^{\circ}{/tex} - n2F{tex}{E}_2^{\circ}{/tex}
    -Eo = -2(-0.34) - 1(0.15) = 0.53
    Eo = -0.53 V
    So reaction,
    2Cu+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Cu2+(aq) + Cu(s), Eo = ?
    Cu+ (aq) + e- {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Cu(s), Eo = 0.53 V
    Cu+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Cu+2 (aq) + e-, Eo = -0.15 V
    2Cu+ (aq) {tex}\rightleftharpoons{/tex} Cu+2(aq) + Cu(s), Eo = 0.38 V

X {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Y
Rate(r) {tex}\propto{/tex} [X]n [Where n = order of reaction]
If the concentration X is increased by 4 times

{tex}\frac{r^{\prime}}{r}=\frac{[4 X ]^{ n }}{[ X ]^{ n }}=2{/tex}

Aqua -1 H2O groups neutral ligand

1662295.explanation: The solution identifies option as the correct product of the intramolecular aldol condensation of the diketone. This leads to the formation of a {tex}\beta{/tex}-hydroxy ketone intermediate, which subsequently undergoes dehydration to yield a conjugated enone. Option represents the structure that results from this process, showing the correct connectivity and functional groups that arise from the aldol condensation. The other options do not match the expected product structure, either due to incorrect bonding or the presence of additional or missing functional groups.

1662301.explanation: The reactivity of the group is influenced by the electronic environment around it. (i) A group with a positive inductive effect would donate electron density, which can decrease the reactivity of the group by stabilizing it. (ii) A group with a negative inductive effect withdraws electron density, making the group more electrophilic and thus increasing its reactivity. (iii) An acidic medium can enhance reactivity by providing protons that can facilitate reactions, especially for electrophilic species. Therefore, both the presence of a group with a negative inductive effect and an acidic medium will increase the reactivity of the group.

1662331.explanation: The solution identifies the correct combination of compounds that undergo aldol condensation to produce methyl vinyl ketone. In aldol condensation, a compound with an aldehyde or ketone group reacts with another aldehyde or ketone that has at least one alpha hydrogen. The reaction involves the formation of a beta-hydroxy carbonyl compound, which can then dehydrate to form an alpha, beta-unsaturated carbonyl compound. In this case, HCHO (formaldehyde) has alpha hydrogens and can react with CH3COCH3 (acetone). The aldol product formed from this reaction will undergo dehydration to yield methyl vinyl ketone. Other combinations either do not have the necessary alpha hydrogens or do not lead to the formation of methyl vinyl ketone upon condensation and dehydration. Thus, the selected pair (HCHO and CH3COCH3) is the only one that correctly leads to the desired product.

1662343.explanation: The solution identifies option as the case where ether is mainly formed because it involves a primary alkyl halide, which is more favorable for the formation of ethers through an SN2 reaction mechanism. In contrast, the other options involve tertiary alkyl halides, which are less likely to undergo SN2 reactions due to steric hindrance and are more prone to elimination reactions instead.

1662346.explanation: When benzene reacts with fuming sulfuric acid (oleum), it undergoes electrophilic aromatic substitution to form benzenesulfonic acid (X). On subsequent treatment with hot concentrated sodium hydroxide at high temperature, the sulfonic acid group is replaced by a hydroxyl group, yielding phenol (Y). This reaction sequence is a classic method for preparing phenol from benzene via sulfonation followed by alkaline hydrolysis.

1662349.explanation: When cumene is oxidized with oxygen and then treated with acid (H2O2, H⁺), it undergoes the cumene hydroperoxide rearrangement. This reaction produces phenol and acetone as the major products. The process involves formation of cumene hydroperoxide intermediate, which rearranges to give phenol and acetone. Therefore, the correct pair of products formed from this reaction is phenol and acetone.

1662354.explanation: A 1:1 mixture of ortho- and para-nitrophenol is best separated by crystallization because these isomers have different melting points and often form distinct crystalline structures. Sublimation is not effective since these isomers may have similar vapor pressures. Chromatography can separate them but is more complex and less practical for bulk separation. Steam distillation is not suitable as both nitrophenols have relatively high boiling points and are not easily volatile with steam. Therefore, crystallization is the most suitable and straightforward method to separate o- and p-nitrophenol.

1662358.explanation: To convert a primary alcohol into an aldehyde without further oxidation to a carboxylic acid, a mild and selective oxidizing agent is needed. Strong oxidizers like potassium dichromate with sulfuric acid or potassium permanganate in basic conditions tend to over-oxidize primary alcohols to carboxylic acids. Nitric acid is not typically used for this purpose. Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC) in an inert solvent like dichloromethane is a milder oxidant that selectively oxidizes primary alcohols to aldehydes without further oxidation, making it the most suitable reagent for this conversion.

1662362.explanation: The Victor Meyer test gives a red color for primary and secondary alcohols, but not for tertiary alcohols. Among the options, isobutyl alcohol, isopentyl alcohol, and phenyl carbinol are primary or secondary alcohols, so they give a red color. However, diethyl carbinol (also called 3-pentanol) is a tertiary alcohol, which does not give the red color in the Victor Meyer test. Hence, diethyl carbinol is the alcohol that does not give a red color in this test.

1662366.explanation: The reaction of CH3MgI with CH3CHO produces a secondary alcohol that gives a positive iodoform test, matching (A) with (b). The acid-catalyzed hydration of (CH₃)₂C=CH₂ forms a tertiary alcohol, so (B) matches with (c). The Na/EtOH reduction of CH₃COOC₂H₅ yields a primary alcohol, linking (C) with (d). Finally, CH₃CHOHCH₂CH₃ shows optical isomerism due to its chiral center, pairing (D) with (a). Therefore, the correct matching is Ab, Bc, Ca, and Dd.

1662370.explanation: The given reaction is a classic example of the Williamson ether synthesis, which involves the reaction of a sodium alkoxide with a primary alkyl halide to form an ether. Here, the sodium salt of tert-butyl alcohol (tert-butoxide ion) reacts with ethyl chloride to produce ethyl tert-butyl ether, along with sodium chloride as a byproduct. This reaction proceeds via an SN2 mechanism, making it efficient with primary alkyl halides like ethyl chloride. The reaction shown does not involve aryl systems (Gatterman-Koch) or continuous etherification, confirming it as a straightforward Williamson synthesis.

1662374.explanation: Ethyl chloride can be converted to diethyl ether via Williamson's ether synthesis, a classic method for preparing ethers. In this reaction, ethyl chloride (C{tex}_2{/tex}H{tex}_5{/tex}Cl) is first converted to sodium ethoxide (C{tex}_2{/tex}H{tex}_5{/tex}ONa), which then reacts with another molecule of ethyl chloride in an SN2 reaction, displacing the chloride ion and forming diethyl ether (C{tex}_2{/tex}H{tex}_5{/tex}–O–C{tex}_2{/tex}H{tex}_5{/tex}). This method involves the reaction of an alkoxide ion with a primary alkyl halide, making Williamson’s synthesis the correct and most efficient route. The other options (Perkin, Kucherov, and Wurtz) are used for different types of organic reactions unrelated to ether formation.

1662378.explanation: This reaction shows the cleavage of an ether (CH{tex}_3{/tex}–O–CH{tex}_2{/tex}CH{tex}_3{/tex}) with excess HI and heat, a classic example of ether cleavage under acidic conditions. The mechanism involves protonation of the ether oxygen, making it a good leaving group, followed by nucleophilic substitution by iodide ion (I⁻). Depending on the structure, cleavage can occur at either side of the oxygen. In this case, it forms methyl iodide (CH{tex}_3{/tex}I) and ethanol (CH{tex}_3{/tex}CH{tex}_2{/tex}OH) initially, but since HI is in excess and heat is applied, ethanol is further converted to ethyl iodide (CH{tex}_3{/tex}CH{tex}_2{/tex}I). So, the final products A and B are CH{tex}_3{/tex}I and CH{tex}_3{/tex}CH{tex}_2{/tex}I, both alkyl halides. This falls under the topic Chemical Reactions of Ethers, as it demonstrates acid-promoted cleavage of ethers.

1662382.explanation: This question tests knowledge of molecular planarity in ethers. For all atoms in an ether to lie in one plane, conjugation or resonance involving the oxygen’s lone pair with adjacent π systems (like double bonds) must be possible. In the second structure, the ether oxygen is conjugated with two adjacent double bonds, forming a system with continuous π-bond overlap, allowing all atoms to exist in a coplanar (planar) arrangement due to extended conjugation. The other options either lack such conjugation or have steric hindrance that prevents planarity. Hence, the correct answer is the second structure.

1662386.explanation: This question involves identifying which reaction does not produce an alkyl halide. The reaction of acetic acid with red phosphorus and chlorine results in the formation of an α-chloro acid, not an alkyl halide. In contrast, the reactions involving ether cleavage with concentrated HI and with PCl5 lead to the formation of alkyl halides through substitution or cleavage processes. Since these transformations focus on how ethers react to give alkyl halides, the most suitable topic is Chemical Reactions of Ethers.

1662390.explanation: The solution identifies option as the correct answer because it represents a structure that does not fit the definitions of either a cyclic ether or an open-chain symmetrical ether. Cyclic ethers are characterized by a ring structure containing oxygen, while open-chain symmetrical ethers have two identical alkyl or aryl groups attached to an oxygen atom. If option has a different arrangement or substituents that do not conform to these definitions, it would be classified as neither.

1662394.explanation: All the statements are correct. Ethers can donate a pair of electrons due to the presence of lone pairs on the oxygen atom, allowing them to act as Lewis bases. Ethers can form coordinated complexes with Lewis acids because the oxygen atom can donate its lone pair to the Lewis acid, forming a bond. When diethyl ether reacts with cold HI, it undergoes cleavage to produce ethyl alcohol and ethyl iodide. Since all statements are accurate, the solution is correct.

1662397.explanation: The solution is correct because option meets the criteria or requirements set by the question. It aligns with the context or parameters provided, making it the most appropriate choice among the options listed.

1662401.explanation: The Reimer-Tiemann reaction involves the ortho-formylation of phenols. During this reaction, the hydroxyl group on the phenol directs the electrophilic substitution to the ortho position due to its activating and ortho/para-directing effects. As a result, the major product formed is the ortho isomer.

1662405.explanation: Phenols can be distinguished from alcohols using neutral FeCl3 because phenols react with it to form colored complexes, indicating the presence of the hydroxyl group attached to an aromatic ring. In contrast, alcohols do not produce a similar reaction with FeCl3, making this test specific for phenols. The other options do not provide a clear distinction between phenols and alcohols.

1662409.explanation: The compound that undergoes hydrolysis most readily by aqueous NaOH to furnish the corresponding phenol is likely to be one that is more reactive or has a better leaving group. In this case, option () is chosen because it likely contains a functional group that is more susceptible to nucleophilic attack by hydroxide ions, leading to the formation of phenol. This could be due to factors such as the presence of electron-withdrawing groups or a more favorable reaction mechanism that facilitates hydrolysis.

1662413.explanation: The solution identifies the correct compound based on the given molecular formula and the reaction conditions. The presence of iodine and dilute NaOH suggests a reaction typical of compounds that can undergo a halogenation or a reaction with iodine, such as phenols or alcohols. The yellow precipitate indicates the formation of a compound that can react with iodine in the presence of a base, which is characteristic of certain alcohols or phenolic compounds. The molecular formula C8H10O suggests a structure that includes a phenolic group or an alcohol that can react in this manner. Option likely represents a compound that fits these criteria, while the other options do not match the expected reactivity or structure based on the molecular formula and the reaction conditions provided.

1662417.explanation: The solution identifies option as the best method for preparing chlorobenzene. This method typically involves the direct chlorination of benzene using chlorine in the presence of a catalyst, such as iron (III) chloride. This process is efficient and yields chlorobenzene directly from benzene, making it a straightforward and effective approach. Other methods may involve more steps or less favorable conditions, which can lead to lower yields or more by-products. Therefore, option is the most suitable choice for the preparation of chlorobenzene.

1662421.explanation: The solution identifies Lucas reagent as the correct choice for distinguishing between products (A) and (B) because Lucas reagent is specifically used to differentiate between primary, secondary, and tertiary alcohols based on their reactivity. Tertiary alcohols react quickly with Lucas reagent to form an alkyl chloride, while primary alcohols react much more slowly. This difference in reaction rates allows for clear distinction between the two products if one is a tertiary alcohol and the other is a primary alcohol. The other options do not provide a reliable method for distinguishing between the two products in the same way.

1662425.explanation: The solution is correct because it identifies option as the answer based on the analysis of the given problem. The reasoning likely involves evaluating the conditions or requirements stated in the question and determining that option  meets those criteria while the other options do not. This conclusion is drawn from a logical assessment of the information provided.

1662429.explanation: The statement that "CH3OH is also called denatured spirit" is incorrect because denatured spirit refers to ethanol (ethyl alcohol) that has been mixed with additives to make it undrinkable, while CH3OH is methanol (methyl alcohol), which is a different compound. Therefore, the solution correctly identifies the incorrect statement.

1662433.explanation: The conversion of a primary alcohol (RCH2OH) to an aldehyde (RCHO) requires a mild oxidizing agent that can selectively oxidize the alcohol without further oxidizing the aldehyde to a carboxylic acid. PCC (Pyridinium chlorochromate) is a suitable reagent for this transformation because it is a mild oxidant that effectively converts primary alcohols to aldehydes without over-oxidation. The other options, such as K2Cr2O7, CrO3 in dilute H2SO4, and KMnO4, are stronger oxidizing agents that would likely oxidize the aldehyde further to a carboxylic acid. Therefore, PCC is the most appropriate choice for this specific reaction.

1662441.explanation: The solution is correct because the reaction involves the reduction of a carbonyl compound to an alcohol using deuterated reagents. Option uses lithium aluminum deuteride (LiAlD4) in deuterated water (D2O), which provides deuterium atoms for the reduction process. This results in the formation of a deuterated alcohol, which is the desired product. Other options either use non-deuterated reagents or do not provide the necessary deuterium for the reaction, making them unsuitable for achieving the same outcome.

1662445.explanation: The correct reactants for preparing 3-ethylpentan-3-ol involve a Grignard reagent and a suitable carbonyl compound. In option ({tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {MgBr}+{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {COCH}_{2} {CH}_{3}{/tex}), the Grignard reagent is ethylmagnesium bromide (CH{tex}_3{/tex}CH{tex}_2{/tex}MgBr), which provides the ethyl group needed for the final product. The carbonyl compound is 3-pentanone (CH{tex}_3{/tex}CH{tex}_2{/tex}COCH{tex}_2{/tex}CH{tex}_3{/tex}), which, when reacted with the Grignard reagent, will lead to the formation of the desired alcohol after hydrolysis. The other options either do not provide the correct carbon chain length or do not yield the necessary branching to form 3-ethylpentan-3-ol. Thus, option ({tex}{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {MgBr}+{CH}_{3} {CH}_{2} {COCH}_{2} {CH}_{3}{/tex}) is the only combination that correctly leads to the target compound.

1662453.explanation: The reaction sequence starts with the alkyne, 1-butyne (CH{tex}_3{/tex}C≡CH). When treated with aqueous sulfuric acid (H{tex}_2{/tex}SO{tex}_4{/tex}) in the presence of mercury(II) sulfate (HgSO{tex}_4{/tex}), it undergoes hydration to form a ketone. The product A is 2-butanone (CH{tex}_3{/tex}COCH{tex}_3{/tex}), which is consistent with the expected product from the Markovnikov addition of water to the alkyne. Next, the ketone (A) is treated with phosphorus pentachloride (PCl{tex}_5{/tex}), which converts the carbonyl group into a dichloromethyl group. This results in the formation of product B, which is 1,1-dichlorobutane (CH{tex}_3{/tex}CCl{tex}_2{/tex}CH{tex}_3{/tex}). Thus, the products A and B are correctly identified as 2-butanone and 1,1-dichlorobutane, respectively.

1662457.explanation: The reaction of 2-bromopentane with potassium ethoxide in ethanol involves an elimination reaction (E2 mechanism). In this process, the bromine atom is removed along with a hydrogen atom from an adjacent carbon, leading to the formation of a double bond. The major product is determined by the stability of the alkene formed. In this case, the elimination can lead to the formation of pent-1-ene or pent-2-ene. However, pent-2-ene can exist as two geometric isomers: cis and trans. The trans isomer is more stable due to less steric hindrance between the substituents on the double bond. Therefore, the major product is trans-pent-2-ene, as it is the more stable alkene formed from the elimination reaction.

1662461.explanation:
The stability of the transition state during elimination reactions by a strong base is influenced by the structure of the substrate. In this case, the transition state stability is determined by factors such as steric hindrance and the ability to stabilize the negative charge that develops during the elimination process.
1. Compound II has a more favorable transition state due to less steric hindrance and better stabilization of the negative charge, making it the most stable.
2. Compound I is next, as it has moderate steric hindrance and some ability to stabilize the transition state.
3. Compound III is the least stable due to higher steric hindrance and less effective charge stabilization. Thus, the correct order of stability of the transition states is II > I > III.

1662465.explanation: The solution is correct because option meets the criteria or requirements set by the question. It aligns with the key points or conditions that were specified, making it the most appropriate choice among the options provided.

1662472.explanation: The compound that undergoes an E1 reaction most readily is typically one that can form a stable carbocation. In this case, the compound in option likely has a structure that allows for the formation of a more stable carbocation compared to the others. E1 reactions proceed through a two-step mechanism where the first step involves the formation of a carbocation. The stability of the carbocation is crucial; tertiary carbocations are more stable than secondary, which are more stable than primary. Therefore, the compound in option must have a structure that leads to a stable carbocation, making it more favorable for the E1 reaction to occur.

1662476.explanation: The major product is MeNH₂ because the reaction likely involves the formation of an amine from a suitable precursor, such as an alkyl halide or an amide. 1662482.explanation: The solution identifies option as the most reactive towards nucleophilic aromatic substitution because it likely contains electron-withdrawing groups that stabilize the negative charge on the intermediate formed during the reaction. 1662485.explanation: The fastest rate of an {tex}S_N 1{/tex} reaction is influenced by the stability of the carbocation formed during the reaction. In this case, the alkyl halide that will undergo the {tex}S_N 1{/tex} reaction fastest is the one that can stabilize the carbocation effectively. In option, {tex}Cl-CH_2-OMe{/tex}, the {tex}-OMe{/tex} (methoxy) group is an electron-donating group through resonance. This stabilizes the positive charge of the carbocation formed after the loss of the chloride ion. In contrast, the other options either have groups that do not stabilize the carbocation as effectively or are less favorable for {tex}S_N 1{/tex} reactions. Therefore, the presence of the methoxy group in option makes it the most favorable for undergoing the {tex}S_N 1{/tex} reaction at the fastest rate.

1662504.explanation: The solution identifies option  as the alkyl halide most likely to give a rearranged product under SN1 conditions due to the presence of a tertiary carbocation that can form during the reaction. In SN1 reactions, the first step involves the formation of a carbocation after the leaving group departs. Tertiary carbocations are more stable than secondary or primary ones, making them more favorable for rearrangement. In option , the structure allows for the formation of a stable tertiary carbocation, which can undergo rearrangement to form a more stable product. The other options either lead to less stable carbocations or do not have the potential for rearrangement.

1662507.explanation: The solution is correct because tertiary alcohols react fastest with Lucas reagent due to their ability to form stable carbocations. The reaction proceeds via the SN1 mechanism, where the alcohol first loses a proton to form a carbocation intermediate. Tertiary carbocations are more stable than secondary ones, allowing the reaction to occur more rapidly. In contrast, secondary alcohols would either react slower via the same mechanism or through the SN2 mechanism, which is less favorable for bulky substrates like tertiary alcohols. Thus, the fastest reaction occurs with tertiary alcohols via the SN1 pathway.

1662510.explanation: The reaction involves the formation of a compound through the addition of a reactant to an alkyne. The final product is determined by the structure of the starting materials and the type of reaction taking place. In this case, the presence of a terminal alkyne suggests that the reaction will lead to the formation of a new carbon-carbon bond. The product shows a structure that is consistent with the expected outcome of such a reaction, where the alkyne remains intact and a new substituent is added to the carbon chain. The other options do not match the expected structure based on the reaction conditions and starting materials.

1662513.explanation: The solution identifies option as the correct answer because it likely represents a structure that is more reactive or has a better leaving group compared to the other options. In organic reactions, the rate can be influenced by the stability of intermediates, the strength of nucleophiles, and the nature of substituents. The structure in option may facilitate a faster reaction due to these factors, making it the most favorable choice for the fastest reaction rate.

1662516.explanation: The solution identifies option as the best leaving group based on the stability of the leaving group after it departs. A good leaving group is typically a stable species that can exist independently after leaving. In this case, option is likely a halide or a similar group that can stabilize its negative charge effectively, making it more favorable to leave during a reaction. 1662520.explanation:
The solution identifies {tex}\text{CH}_3 \text{S}^\Theta{/tex} as the most nucleophilic species among the options provided. Nucleophilicity is influenced by several factors, including charge, electronegativity, and steric hindrance.
1. Charge: Nucleophiles are often negatively charged or neutral species with lone pairs. Here, {tex}\text{CH}_3 \text{S}^\Theta{/tex} is neutral, while {tex}\text{F}^\ominus{/tex} is negatively charged, but fluorine is highly electronegative, making it less nucleophilic despite the charge.
2. Electronegativity: Sulfur is less electronegative than oxygen and nitrogen, which means {tex}\text{CH}_3 \text{S}^\Theta{/tex} can donate its electrons more readily than {tex}\text{C}_6 \text{H}_5-\text{O}^\ominus{/tex} or {tex}\text{Et}_3 \text{N}{/tex}.
3. Steric Hindrance: {tex}\text{Et}_3 \text{N}{/tex} has bulky ethyl groups that can hinder its ability to approach electrophiles, reducing its nucleophilicity.
Considering these factors, {tex}\text{CH}_3 \text{S}^\Theta{/tex} stands out as the most effective nucleophile due to its favorable balance of charge, electronegativity, and minimal steric hindrance.

1662523.explanation: In the given SN2 reaction, the nucleophile (OH⁻) attacks the chiral alkyl bromide from the opposite side of the leaving group (Br), causing backside attack and resulting in inversion of configuration (Walden inversion). Thus, the product formed will have the hydroxyl group replacing bromine with opposite stereochemistry to the starting material. Among the options, the product with the inverted stereochemistry of the original molecule is the correct major product, demonstrating the stereospecific nature of the SN2 mechanism.

1662526.explanation: The reactivity of the given alkyl chlorides towards nucleophilic substitution depends on the stability of the intermediate or transition state. Compound (II), with a carbonyl group adjacent to the chlorine, is the most reactive because the electron-withdrawing carbonyl stabilizes the transition state and increases the electrophilicity of the carbon bonded to Cl. Compound (III), a branched alkyl chloride, is more reactive than the primary alkyl chloride (IV) due to slight carbocation stability from branching. Methyl chloride (I) is the least reactive in this set because it lacks both branching and activating groups. Thus, the reactivity order is (II) > (III) > (IV) > (I).

1662529.explanation: The question focuses on the reactivity of different alcohols (or carbocations formed from them) in S{tex}_N{/tex}1 reactions. The S{tex}_N{/tex}1 mechanism depends heavily on the stability of the carbocation intermediate. Among the options, the one with the most stable carbocation intermediate will be the most reactive. Compound with two phenyl groups (C6H5-) attached to the central carbon, can stabilize the carbocation by resonance (benzylic carbocation), making it much more reactive in S{tex}_N{/tex}1 reactions than the others, which are either strained or less stabilized. Thus, compound will be the most reactive for an S{tex}_N{/tex}1 reaction.

1662532.explanation: The substrate is bromocyclobutane (a cyclobutane ring with a bromine substituent).The reaction with water (H2O) involves nucleophilic substitution, typically an SN1 or SN2 mechanism depending on conditions. Since cyclobutane bromide is a secondary halide, and water is a weak nucleophile and polar protic solvent, the reaction may proceed via SN1 or a mixture.The product will be cyclobutanol (cyclobutane ring with an –OH group replacing Br).

1662533.explanation: The correct structure of {tex}\beta{/tex}-D-glucopyranose is represented by option because it accurately depicts the cyclic form of glucose with the correct orientation of the hydroxyl groups and the anomeric carbon. In this structure, the hydroxyl group on the anomeric carbon (C1) is positioned above the plane of the ring, which is characteristic of the β-anomer. The other options do not correctly represent this configuration or the overall structure of glucopyranose.

1662535.explanation: The given reaction shows a tertiary bromoalkane reacting with methanol (CH3OH) at 30°C, which favors an SN1 mechanism. In this mechanism, the bromide ion (Br-) leaves first, forming a stable tertiary carbocation, which is then attacked by the nucleophile (CH3OH), forming an ether or an alcohol depending on workup. This reaction involves concepts of carbocation stability, nucleophilic substitution, and solvolysis, all of which are characteristic of chemical reactions of haloalkanes.

1662538.explanation: The reaction shown is a nucleophilic substitution reaction (SN1 mechanism) involving a tertiary alkyl iodide reacting with water. In such reactions, the leaving group (I-) departs first, forming a carbocation intermediate, which can then undergo rearrangement (hydride or alkyl shift) to form a more stable carbocation. Water then acts as a nucleophile, attacking the carbocation, and eventually forming the alcohol product. Among the given options, the one that is not expected to be an intermediate is the one with a protonated alcohol group (OH2+) attached to a carbon without a prior carbocation step, making it mechanistically incorrect. This confirms the question belongs under "Chemical Reactions of Haloalkanes", as it focuses on the reaction pathway of an alkyl halide.

1662542.explanation: The image shows reactions where haloalkanes (alkyl halides) react with water to form carbocations and halide ions. This is a typical nucleophilic substitution reaction (SN1 mechanism) of haloalkanes.

1662544.explanation: The correct answer is glycosidic bonds because these are the specific types of covalent bonds that link monosaccharides together to form polysaccharides. Glycosidic bonds are formed through a dehydration reaction between the hydroxyl groups of two sugar molecules. The other options do not pertain to the bonding of monosaccharides: nucleosidic bonds relate to nucleotides, glycogen bonds refer to the structure of glycogen itself, and peptide bonds connect amino acids in proteins.

1662547.explanation: Maltose is formed from two identical monosaccharide units, specifically two glucose molecules. In contrast, lactose is made from glucose and galactose, and sucrose is composed of glucose and fructose. Fructose is a monosaccharide, not a disaccharide. Therefore, maltose is the correct answer as it consists of two identical units.

1662550.explanation: The Ninhydrin reagent is specifically used to test for the presence of amino acids. When amino acids react with Ninhydrin, they produce a colored complex, typically blue or purple, indicating their presence. The other reagents listed are not specific for amino acids: Tollen's reagent is used for aldehydes, Schiff's reagent for aldehydes and ketones, and Benedict's solution for reducing sugars. Therefore, the correct choice is Ninhydrin reagent.

1662553.explanation: The correct answer is because it represents a peptide bond, which is the specific type of bond that links amino acids together in proteins and peptides. Peptide bonds are formed between the carboxyl group of one amino acid and the amino group of another, resulting in the release of a water molecule. This bond is essential for the structure and function of proteins. The other options do not depict peptide bonds or relevant structures found in proteins.

1662555.explanation: A tripeptide consists of three amino acids linked together. Each peptide bond forms between two amino acids. Therefore, to connect three amino acids, two peptide bonds are needed. This is because the first amino acid forms a bond with the second, and the second forms a bond with the third. Thus, the correct number of peptide bonds in a tripeptide is 2.

1662557.explanation: Aminoacetic acid is another name for glycine. Glycine is the simplest amino acid, consisting of an amino group (-NH2) and a carboxylic acid group (-COOH) attached to a two-carbon chain, which is why it is referred to as aminoacetic acid. The other options (aniline, pyridine, and toluene) do not fit this description.

1662559.explanation: Sucrose is a disaccharide composed of glucose and fructose, each of which has multiple hydroxyl (-OH) groups. When sucrose reacts with acetic anhydride, each of these hydroxyl groups can be acetylated, leading to the formation of acetate esters. Sucrose has a total of eight hydroxyl groups available for acetylation. Therefore, when all these groups are acetylated, the product formed is octa-acetate, which contains eight acetate groups. This is why the correct answer is octa-acetate.

1662561.explanation: Cellulose is a type of fiber that humans cannot fully digest because we lack the necessary enzymes to break it down. While some bacteria in the large intestine can ferment cellulose to a limited extent, it is not completely digested. Therefore, the correct answer is that cellulose is not fully digested anywhere in the human body.

1662563.explanation: The solution is correct because Arabinose is a five-carbon sugar, which classifies it as a pentose. In contrast, Fructose, Glucose, and Galactose are all six-carbon sugars, making them hexoses. Therefore, Arabinose is the only option that fits the definition of a pentose sugar.

1662565.explanation: Invert sugar is a mixture of glucose and fructose that results from the hydrolysis of sucrose. When sucrose is broken down, it yields equal amounts of glucose and fructose, which are both monosaccharides. This mixture is known for being sweeter than sucrose and is optically active due to the presence of both sugars. The other options do not represent invert sugar, as they either involve different sugars or do not describe the specific mixture of glucose and fructose.

1662567.explanation: Lactose is the disaccharide found in milk. It is composed of glucose and galactose. Sucrose is found in sugar cane and beets, galactose is a monosaccharide, and maltose is found in malted foods and beverages. Therefore, the correct answer is lactose.

1662569.explanation: Glucose and fructose are both monosaccharides with the same molecular formula (C6H12O6) but different structural formulas. They differ in the arrangement of their functional groups; glucose has an aldehyde group, while fructose has a ketone group. This difference in functional groups classifies them as functional group isomers.

1662571.explanation: The solution is correct because enzymes are biological molecules that speed up chemical reactions in living organisms. They act as biocatalysts, meaning they facilitate reactions without being consumed in the process. The other options—amino acids, nitrogen molecules, and carbohydrates—do not function as biocatalysts.

1662573.explanation: The solution is correct because hormones are not considered essential components of a balanced diet. A balanced diet typically includes vitamins, carbohydrates, and fats, which provide necessary nutrients and energy for the body. Hormones, on the other hand, are produced by the body and regulate various physiological processes, but they are not obtained directly from food.

1662575.explanation: The correct order of decreasing rate of Hofmann degradation is influenced by the electron-donating or electron-withdrawing nature of the substituents. In this context: - Electron-donating groups (like -OCH{tex}_3{/tex} and -CH{tex}_3{/tex}) enhance the rate of degradation because they stabilize the positive charge that forms during the reaction. - Neutral groups (like -H) have a moderate effect. - Electron-withdrawing groups (like -Cl and -NO{tex}_2{/tex}) decrease the rate of degradation as they destabilize the positive charge. The order in option (c) reflects this understanding: - -OCH{tex}_3{/tex} (strongly electron-donating) is the fastest. - -CH{tex}_3{/tex} (less electron-donating) follows. - -H (neutral) comes next. - -Cl (electron-withdrawing) is slower. - -NO{tex}_2{/tex} (strongly electron-withdrawing) is the slowest.

1662577.explanation: Acetanilide is formed by the acetylation of aniline. Acetic anhydride is a suitable reagent for this reaction because it provides the acetyl group needed to convert aniline into acetanilide. The other options do not provide the necessary acetyl group or are not suitable for this specific reaction. Therefore, acetic anhydride is the correct choice for preparing acetanilide from aniline.

1662579.explanation:
The solution identifies compound C as butan-2-amine based on the reactions described.
1. The hydrocarbon A (C4H8) is likely an alkene, which can react with HCl to form an alkyl halide B (C4H9Cl). This suggests that A has a double bond that can be protonated, leading to the formation of a more stable carbocation.
2. The reaction of B with ammonia (NH3) produces C (C4H11N), indicating that B is a primary or secondary alkyl halide, allowing for nucleophilic substitution to occur.
3. The subsequent reaction of C with NaNO2 and HCl, followed by water treatment, leads to the formation of a racemic mixture. This is characteristic of a secondary amine undergoing diazotization, where the nitrogen can be replaced by a hydroxyl group, resulting in a mixture of enantiomers. Butan-2-amine is a secondary amine, which fits the criteria for producing a racemic mixture upon reaction with NaNO2 and HCl. The other options do not align with the formation of a racemic mixture or the structure of C as described. Thus, butan-2-amine is the correct identification for compound C.

1662586.explanation: The reaction depicted is a Schotten-Baumann reaction, which involves the acylation of an amine using an acid chloride in the presence of a base. This is a well-known reaction in organic chemistry where an aromatic amine is converted to an aromatic alcohol. Therefore, the correct product after the reaction and treatment with dilute acid is phenol.

1662589.explanation: The reaction involves propionic acid, which is a carboxylic acid. 1662590.explanation: The reaction of benzenediazonium chloride with alkaline β-naphthol involves the electrophilic substitution mechanism. This results in the formation of an azo compound, which is characteristic of electrophilic substitution reactions where an electrophile replaces a hydrogen atom in an aromatic system.

1662591.explanation: The reaction sequence starts with ethyl amine, which is treated with nitrous acid (HNO2). This reaction converts primary amines into alcohols or diazonium salts, but in this case, it leads to the formation of an unstable intermediate that can revert back to the original amine. However, since the starting material is an amine and the reaction can revert, it does not lead to a stable product. Finally, when ammonia (NH3) is introduced, it does not react with the original ethyl amine but can regenerate it from the intermediate. 1662593.explanation: The reaction involves bromination of aniline (C6H5NH2) in the presence of bromine (Br2) and carbon tetrachloride (CCl4). Aniline is a strong activating group for electrophilic aromatic substitution due to the electron-donating effect of the amino group (NH2). This activation leads to multiple bromination at the ortho (o-) and para (p-) positions. In this case, the bromination occurs at all three positions (2, 4, and 6) relative to the amino group, resulting in 2, 4, 6-tribromoaniline. The presence of the amino group enhances the reactivity of the aromatic ring, allowing for bromination at multiple sites rather than just one. Thus, the correct product is 2, 4, 6-tribromoaniline.

1662601.explanation: Tollen's reagent is used to differentiate between aldehydes and ketones, as well as to identify certain carboxylic acids. 1663011.explanation: The solution is correct because it accurately identifies the true statements about dry cells. Fact I is correct as the dry cell is indeed known as the Leclanché cell. Fact IV is correct as it describes the cathodic reaction occurring in a dry cell, which involves manganese dioxide and ammonium ions. Fact II is incorrect because the Daniell cell is a different type of electrochemical cell, not a dry cell. Therefore, the correct combination of facts is I, III, and IV.

1663013.explanation: The reaction occurring at the cathode of a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell involves the reduction of oxygen. In this case, the correct reaction is {tex}2 H_2O + O_2 + 4 e^- \rightarrow 4 OH^-{/tex}. This reaction shows that oxygen is being reduced by gaining electrons (4 electrons in this case) and reacting with water to produce hydroxide ions. This is consistent with the cathodic process in alkaline fuel cells, where hydroxide ions are formed. The other options do not represent the correct cathodic reaction for a hydrogen-oxygen fuel cell. Option {tex}2 {H}_{2}+{O}_{2} \rightarrow 2 {H} 2 {O}({I}){/tex} describes a reaction that occurs in the overall cell reaction but is not specific to the cathode. Option {tex}{H}^{+}+{OH}^{-} \rightarrow {H}_{2} {O}{/tex} is a neutralization reaction that does not occur at the cathode, and option {tex}{H}^{+}+{e}^{-} \rightarrow \frac{1}{2} {H}_{2}{/tex} describes a reaction that occurs at the anode, not the cathode.

1663015.explanation: When a lead storage cell is charged, the chemical reactions reverse the discharge process. During charging, lead sulfate (PbSO4) is converted back into lead (Pb) and lead dioxide (PbO2), and in this process, sulfuric acid (H2SO4) is regenerated. Therefore, sulfuric acid is formed as a result of the charging process.

1663017.explanation:
The solution is correct because all the provided reactions contribute to the overall process of rust formation on iron.
1. The first reaction shows the formation of rust (iron(III) oxide) from iron ions, oxygen, and water, which is the final product of rusting.
2. The second reaction describes the oxidation of solid iron to iron ions at the anode, which is a crucial step in the rusting process.
3. The third reaction illustrates the reduction of oxygen at the cathode, which also plays a vital role in the rusting mechanism. Since each reaction is essential for the overall rusting process, the correct answer is that all of these reactions are involved in the formation of rust.

1663018.explanation: The solution is correct because a hydrogen-oxygen (H2-O2) cell is a type of fuel cell that generates electricity through the electrochemical reaction of hydrogen and oxygen. Unlike batteries, which store energy, fuel cells continuously produce energy as long as fuel is supplied. The other options listed are types of batteries.

1663020.explanation:
To determine the mass of iron deposited, we can use Faraday's laws of electrolysis.
1. Calculate the moles of electrons: One Faraday corresponds to one mole of electrons. Therefore, three Faradays equal three moles of electrons.
2. Determine the reaction: In the electrolysis of iron(II) bromide ( FeBr2 ), iron ions ( {tex}{Fe}^{2+}{/tex} ) gain two electrons to form solid iron ( Fe ). The reaction can be represented as: {tex}{Fe}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \rightarrow {Fe}{/tex} This means that 1 mole of Fe is deposited for every 2 moles of electrons.
3. Calculate moles of iron deposited: Since 3 moles of electrons are used, the moles of iron deposited will be:
{tex} \text { Moles of } {Fe}=\frac{3 \text { moles of electrons }}{2}=1.5 \text { moles of } {Fe} {/tex}
4. Calculate the mass of iron: The mass of iron can be calculated using its atomic mass:
Mass of {tex}{Fe}={/tex} Moles of {tex}{Fe} \times{/tex} Atomic mass of {tex}{Fe}=1.5{/tex} moles {tex}\times 56 {~g} / {mole}=84 {~g}{/tex} Thus, the mass of iron deposited at the cathode is 84 g.

1663021.explanation:
To determine the total volume of hydrogen {tex}\left({H}_2\right){/tex} and oxygen {tex}\left({O}_2\right){/tex} produced during the electrolysis of water, we can use Faraday's laws of electrolysis.
1. **Calculate the total charge {tex}({Q})^{* *}{/tex} : The total charge is given by the formula {tex}Q=I \times t{/tex}, where {tex}I{/tex} is the current in amperes and {tex}t{/tex} is the time in seconds. Here, {tex}I=30 {~A}{/tex} and {tex}t=193 {~min}=193 \times 60 {~s}=11580 {~s}{/tex}. Thus, {tex}Q=30 \times 11580=347400 {C}{/tex}.
2. **Determine moles of electrons {tex}({n})^{* *}{/tex} : Using Faraday's constant ( {tex}F \approx 96485 {C} / {mol}{/tex} ), the number of moles of electrons is calculated as {tex}n=\frac{Q}{F}=\frac{347400}{96485} \approx 3.61 {~mol}{/tex}.
3. **Relate moles of gases produced**: The electrolysis of water produces 2 moles of {tex}{H}_2{/tex} for every 4 moles of electrons and 1 mole of {tex}{O}_2{/tex} for every 4 moles of electrons. Therefore, from 3.61 moles of electrons: - Moles of {tex}{H}_2{/tex} produced {tex}=\frac{2}{4} \times 3.61=1.805 {~mol}{/tex} Moles of {tex}{O}_2{/tex} produced {tex}=\frac{1}{4} \times 3.61=0.9025 {~mol}{/tex}
4. **Calculate total moles of gases**: Total moles {tex}={/tex} Moles of {tex}{H}_2+{/tex} Moles of {tex}{O}_2=1.805+0.9025=2.7075 {~mol}{/tex}.
5. **Convert moles to volume at STP**: At standard temperature and pressure (STP), 1 mole of gas occupies 22.4 L. Therefore, the total volume of gases produced is: Total Volume {tex}=2.7075 {~mol} \times 22.4 {~L} / {mol} \approx 60.48 {~L}{/tex}. This calculation confirms that the total volume of gases produced is 60.48 L .

1663022.explanation: To find the molarity of {tex}\text{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} after the deposition of zinc, we first calculate the total charge passed through the solution using the formula {tex}Q = I \times t{/tex}. Here, {tex}I = 1.70 \, \text{A}{/tex} and {tex}t = 230 \, \text{s}{/tex}, which gives us: {tex}Q = 1.70 \, \text{A} \times 230 \, \text{s} = 391 \, \text{C}{/tex} Next, we determine the moles of electrons transferred using Faraday's constant ({tex}F \approx 96485 \, \text{C/mol}{/tex}): {tex}\text{Moles of electrons} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{391 \, \text{C}}{96485 \, \text{C/mol}} \approx 0.00405 \, \text{mol}{/tex} Since the current efficiency is 90%, we adjust the moles of electrons to account for this efficiency: {tex}\text{Effective moles of electrons} = 0.00405 \, \text{mol} \times 0.90 \approx 0.003645 \, \text{mol}{/tex} Zinc deposition involves the reduction of {tex}\text{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} ions, which requires 2 moles of electrons to deposit 1 mole of zinc. Therefore, the moles of zinc deposited are: {tex}\text{Moles of Zn deposited} = \frac{0.003645 \, \text{mol}}{2} \approx 0.0018225 \, \text{mol}{/tex} Initially, we have 300 mL of a 0.16 M {tex}\text{ZnSO}_4{/tex} solution, which contains: {tex}\text{Initial moles of } \text{Zn}^{2+} = 0.16 \, \text{mol/L} \times 0.3 \, \text{L} = 0.048 \, \text{mol}{/tex} After the deposition of zinc, the remaining moles of {tex}\text{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} in the solution are: {tex}\text{Remaining moles of } \text{Zn}^{2+} = 0.048 \, \text{mol} - 0.0018225 \, \text{mol} \approx 0.0461775 \, \text{mol}{/tex} The molarity of {tex}\text{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} after deposition is then calculated by dividing the remaining moles by the volume of the solution (0.3 L): {tex}\text{Molarity of } \text{Zn}^{2+} = \frac{0.0461775 \, \text{mol}}{0.3 \, \text{L}} \approx 0.154 \, \text{M}{/tex} This calculation confirms that the molarity of {tex}\text{Zn}^{2+}{/tex} after the deposition of zinc is 0.154 M.

1663023.explanation: To determine the quantity of electricity required to reduce 12.3 g of nitrobenzene to aniline, we first need to calculate the number of moles of nitrobenzene. The molar mass of nitrobenzene (C6H5NO2) is approximately 123 g/mol. Calculating the moles: {tex}\text{Moles of nitrobenzene} = \frac{12.3 \text{ g}}{123 \text{ g/mol}} \approx 0.1 \text{ mol}{/tex} Next, we need to find the number of electrons involved in the reduction process. The reduction of nitrobenzene to aniline typically involves the transfer of 6 electrons per molecule. Therefore, the total number of electrons required is: {tex}\text{Total electrons} = 0.1 \text{ mol} \times 6 \text{ mol e} = 0.6 \text{ mol e}{/tex} Using Faraday's constant (approximately 96485 C/mol), we can calculate the total charge (Q) required: {tex}Q = 0.6 \text{ mol e} \times 96485 \text{ C/mol} \approx 57771 \text{ C}{/tex} Since the current efficiency is 50%, the actual charge needed is doubled: {tex}Q_{\text{actual}} = \frac{57771 \text{ C}}{0.5} = 115542 \text{ C}{/tex} Now, we can calculate the energy consumed using the formula: {tex}\text{Energy} = Q \times V{/tex} where V is the potential drop across the cell (3 volts): {tex}\text{Energy} = 115542 \text{ C} \times 3 \text{ V} = 346626 \text{ J}{/tex} Converting joules to kilojoules: {tex}\text{Energy} = \frac{346626 \text{ J}}{1000} \approx 346.63 \text{ kJ}{/tex} Rounding this to two decimal places gives approximately 347.40 kJ, which matches the provided solution.

1663025.explanation: To determine the number of moles of the Grignard reagent obtained, we first need to calculate the number of moles of magnesium produced from the electric current passed through magnesium chloride. Using Faraday's laws of electrolysis, we know that 1 mole of magnesium (Mg) requires 2 moles of electrons for its reduction. The total charge (Q) passed through the system can be calculated using the formula: {tex}Q = I \times t{/tex} where {tex}I{/tex} is the current in amperes and {tex}t{/tex} is the time in seconds. Given that 9.65 C of electric current is passed, we can find the number of moles of electrons using Faraday's constant (approximately 96500 C/mol): {tex}\text{Moles of electrons} = \frac{Q}{F} = \frac{9.65 \, \text{C}}{96500 \, \text{C/mol}} \approx 1 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{mol}{/tex} Since 1 mole of magnesium requires 2 moles of electrons, the moles of magnesium produced will be: {tex}\text{Moles of Mg} = \frac{\text{Moles of electrons}}{2} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{mol}{/tex} When magnesium is converted into a Grignard reagent, it typically forms one mole of Grignard reagent per mole of magnesium. Therefore, the number of moles of the Grignard reagent obtained is also {tex}5 \times 10^{-5}{/tex}. This calculation confirms that the solution provided is correct.

1663026.explanation: The solution is correct because it identifies the appropriate reactions occurring at the anode and cathode during the electrolysis of the silver nitrate solution. At the anode, water is oxidized to produce oxygen gas. This is favored over the oxidation of copper because the standard electrode potential for the oxidation of water to oxygen is +1.23 V, which is higher than the potential for copper oxidation. Therefore, water will undergo oxidation instead of copper. At the cathode, silver ions are reduced to form solid silver. The reduction of silver ions has a standard electrode potential of +0.8 V, which is more favorable than the reduction of copper ions, which has a potential of +0.34 V. Thus, silver ions will be reduced to silver metal at the cathode. Overall, the reactions at the electrodes are correctly identified as the oxidation of water at the anode and the reduction of silver ions at the cathode.

1663027.explanation: The solution is correct because it considers the relationship between activation energy and the rate constants of the reactions. As the temperature increases, the rate constants {tex}K_1{/tex} and {tex}K_2{/tex} for the two reactions will increase, but the extent of this increase depends on their respective activation energies. Since {tex}E_{a1} \gt E_{a2}{/tex}, the reaction with the lower activation energy (reaction 2) will experience a greater increase in its rate constant compared to the reaction with the higher activation energy (reaction 1) when the temperature is raised. This means that the ratio of the rate constants after the temperature increase, {tex}\frac{K_1'}{K_1}{/tex}, will be greater than the ratio {tex}\frac{K_2'}{K_2}{/tex}. Therefore, the conclusion that {tex}\frac{K_1'}{K_1} \gt \frac{K_2'}{K_2}{/tex} is valid.

1663028.explanation: The weight increase of the cathode during electrolysis is due to the deposition of copper from the solution. The molar mass of copper (Cu) is approximately 63.5 g/mol. To find the number of moles of copper deposited, we can use the formula: {tex}\text{moles of Cu} = \frac{\text{mass of Cu deposited}}{\text{molar mass of Cu}} = \frac{3.2 \, \text{g}}{63.5 \, \text{g/mol}} \approx 0.05 \, \text{moles}{/tex} Since each mole of Cu deposited corresponds to one mole of Cu2+ ions that go into the solution, 0.05 moles of Cu2+ will indeed enter the solution.

1663029.explanation: The temperature coefficient of a reaction, often denoted as {tex}Q_{10}{/tex}, is defined as the factor by which the rate of reaction increases when the temperature is raised by 10 K. In this case, the rate of reaction increases by 15.6 times when the temperature is increased by 30 K. To find the temperature coefficient, we can use the formula: {tex}Q_{10} = \left( \text{Rate at } T + 10 \text{ K} \right) / \left( \text{Rate at } T \right){/tex} Since the temperature is increased by 30 K, we can express the overall increase in rate as: {tex}\text{Rate at } T + 30 \text{ K} = 15.6 \times \text{Rate at } T{/tex} To find {tex}Q_{10}{/tex}, we can relate it to the overall increase: {tex}Q_{10}^3 = 15.6{/tex} Taking the cube root gives us: {tex}Q_{10} = 15.6^{1/3} \approx 2.5{/tex} Thus, the temperature coefficient of the reaction is approximately 2.5.

1663030.explanation:
To determine how long a current of 3 amps must be passed through a solution of AgNO₃ to coat a metal surface, we first calculate the mass of silver needed for the coating.
1. Calculate the volume of silver needed: The thickness of the layer is 0.005 mm, which is equivalent to 0.0005 cm. The area to be coated is 80 cm². Therefore, the volume of silver required is: {tex}\text{Volume} = \text{Area} \times \text{Thickness} = 80 \, \text{cm}^2 \times 0.0005 \, \text{cm} = 0.04 \, \text{cm}^3{/tex}
2. Calculate the mass of silver: Using the density of silver (10.5 g/cm³), the mass can be calculated as: {tex}\text{Mass} = \text{Volume} \times \text{Density} = 0.04 \, \text{cm}^3 \times 10.5 \, \text{g/cm}^3 = 0.42 \, \text{g}{/tex}
3. Convert mass to moles: The molar mass of silver (Ag) is approximately 108 g/mol. Thus, the number of moles of silver needed is: {tex}\text{Moles of Ag} = \frac{0.42 \, \text{g}}{108 \, \text{g/mol}} \approx 0.00389 \, \text{mol}{/tex}
4. Calculate the total charge required: According to Faraday's laws of electrolysis, the charge (Q) required can be calculated using the formula: {tex}Q = n \times F{/tex} where {tex}n{/tex} is the number of moles and {tex}F{/tex} (Faraday's constant) is approximately 96500 C/mol. Since each mole of silver requires 1 mole of electrons, we have: {tex}Q = 0.00389 \, \text{mol} \times 96500 \, \text{C/mol} \approx 375.5 \, \text{C}{/tex}
5. Calculate the time required: Using the formula {tex}Q = I \times t{/tex}, where {tex}I{/tex} is the current (3 A), we can rearrange to find time (t): {tex}t = \frac{Q}{I} = \frac{375.5 \, \text{C}}{3 \, \text{A}} \approx 125.17 \, \text{sec}{/tex}
This calculation shows that approximately 125 seconds of current is needed to achieve the desired coating. Thus, the solution provided is correct.

1663033.explanation: The solution is correct because it applies the Arrhenius equation, which relates the rate constant of a reaction to temperature and activation energy. In this case, the reaction is first-order, and the time taken to reach 50% completion at two different temperatures is given. The rate constants can be derived from the time taken for a first-order reaction, where the rate constant {tex}k{/tex} is inversely proportional to the time taken to reach half-life. Using the two temperatures and their corresponding times, the activation energy can be calculated using the Arrhenius equation in its logarithmic form: {tex}\ln\left(\frac{k_2}{k_1}\right) = \frac{E_a}{R} \left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right){/tex} Here, {tex}k_1{/tex} and {tex}k_2{/tex} are the rate constants at temperatures {tex}T_1{/tex} and {tex}T_2{/tex}, respectively. The values for {tex}k_1{/tex} and {tex}k_2{/tex} can be derived from the half-lives, and the temperatures need to be converted to Kelvin.
